references.context: Cited as authority on the Holy Spirit's witness that we are children of God, and on our status as heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ Jesus.

references.name: Saint Macrina
references.context: Cited alongside other Church Fathers as teaching that the Holy Trinity is 'a dance, a rotation, an interpenetration.'

references.name: Saint Basil the Great
references.context: Cited alongside other Church Fathers as teaching that the Holy Trinity is 'a dance, a rotation, an interpenetration.'

references.name: Saint Gregory of Nyssa
references.context: Cited alongside other Church Fathers as teaching that the Holy Trinity is 'a dance, a rotation, an interpenetration.'

references.name: Romans 8
references.context: Referenced as the location of Paul's teaching about being heirs of God and joint heirs with Christ Jesus.

references.name: Romans (Letter to the Romans)
references.context: Paul's epistle cited for the teaching that the Holy Spirit bears witness that we are children of God when we cry 'Abba, Father.'

references.name: Nicene Creed
references.context: The historic Christian creed is quoted and analyzed throughout the sermon, particularly the line 'We believe in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the giver of life, who proceeds from the Father' and its concluding clauses on baptism, resurrection, and life to come.

references.name: Aramaic language and 'Abba, Father'
references.context: The preacher identifies 'Abba' as 'an Aramaic phrase of endearment for a parent,' grounding Christian address to God in intimate, familial language.

references.name: Mystical theology of divine perichoresis (interpenetration)
references.context: The sermon articulates the Eastern Orthodox/Cappadocian understanding of the Trinity as dynamic mutual indwelling, describing it as 'a dance, a rotation, an interpenetration' where the three persons flow into one another.

references.name: Pneumatology (theology of the Holy Spirit)
references.context: The entire sermon focuses on the third person of the Trinity and the Spirit's role in bringing Christ alive, sustaining creation through breath, and enabling union with God.

references.name: Theosis/Deification (union with God)
references.context: The preacher emphasizes that believers are 'already at one with God' and come to church to 'train [their] senses and awaken [themselves] to this reality,' reflecting Eastern Orthodox emphasis on theosis.

references.name: Christian discipleship as participation rather than imitation
references.context: The preacher, quoting Williams, describes discipleship not as 'following of a distant figure' but as 'participation in the rhythm that sustains the universe,' echoing participatory rather than forensic soteriological models.

references.name: Ground of being
references.context: The phrase 'ground of our being' appears when describing what believers 'return home' to when reciting the creeds—resonating with Tillichian language.

references.name: Trinitarian theology (doctrine of the Trinity)
references.context: The entire sermon series focuses on God the Holy Trinity; this final reflection emphasizes the relational, dynamic, loving nature of the three persons as a model for human life and community.

references.name: Breath/Pneuma (Spirit as divine breath)
references.context: The preacher repeatedly uses breath as a metaphor for the Holy Spirit, grounding theology in embodied experience: 'God the Holy Spirit, God's very breath, God's wind who is inside of us, breathing in us.'

references.name: Prayer as response to God
references.context: The preacher defines prayer not primarily as petition but as 'a response to God with or without words,' reflecting contemplative and relational approaches to prayer.

primary_theme: God the Holy Spirit brings us into participation in the eternal threefold rhythm of Trinitarian love, sustaining all life through breath and inviting us into deeper union with God and one another.
rhetorical_style: Contemplative pastoral reflection blending liturgical practice (breathing exercises, creedal recitation), scholarly theological analysis (Church Fathers, contemporary theologians), poetic imagery (dance, waves, breath), and intimate pastoral address, designed to move from intellectual understanding to embodied spiritual experience.

references.name: Real Housewives of Beverly Hills
references.context: Preacher uses the reality TV show as a contemporary cultural reference point to describe Jacob's complicated family dynamics: 'Real Housewives of Beverly Hills complicated.'

references.name: The Young and the Restless
references.context: Preacher compares Jacob's family drama to the soap opera: 'Young and the Restless complicated.'

references.name: Genesis 25
references.context: Jacob born grasping Esau's heel; parents favor different sons; Jacob deceives Esau for birthright and red stew.

references.name: Genesis 27
references.context: Jacob and Rebekah deceive Isaac by disguising Jacob as Esau to steal the blessing meant for the firstborn.

references.name: Genesis 32
references.context: Jacob receives news that Esau is coming with 400 security officers; Jacob wrestles a mysterious being until daybreak and receives the name Israel.

references.name: The Parable of the Persistent Widow (Luke 18:1-8)
references.context: Preacher references Jesus's parable about an unjust judge and a widow who badgers him repeatedly for justice, suggesting God is like the widow who persistently wrestles with us.

references.name: Psalm 121
references.context: Implicit reference to 'the God who neither slumbers nor sleeps' - language directly from Psalm 121:4.

references.name: Name changes in scripture (Abram to Abraham, Sarai to Sarah, Simon Peter, Saul to Paul)
references.context: Preacher cites biblical pattern of God changing names to signify threshold crossings, with Jacob/Israel as the most demonstrative example.

references.name: Psychological discourse (implicit reference to psychology as interpretive framework)
references.context: Preacher explicitly disclaims: 'I am not a psychologist, so I can't really get into Jacob's mind,' but then proceeds to use psychological language about personality, desire, and ambition.

references.name: Prayer as wrestling/spiritual struggle (implicit mystical tradition)
references.context: Preacher characterizes prayer not as calm and peaceful but as wrestling, tussling, and fighting with God - language consistent with apophatic or contemplative mysticism.

references.name: The Message translation (of Luke 18)
references.context: Preacher explicitly cites 'the message translation' when quoting the judge's response to the widow about being beaten black and blue.

primary_theme: God encounters us as a wrestling partner in prayer, persistently engaging with our whole selves—our ambitions, deceptions, and drives—not to change who we are but to redeem and refine the person we are already created to be.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection and contemporary cultural analogy; preacher uses the Jacob story as extended metaphor for spiritual struggle, then invites congregational participation through direct questions and relatable scenarios before tying to theological claim about prayer and God's persistent love.

references.name: Book of Deuteronomy
references.context: The preacher writes in the margins: 'Can it really be this simple?' in response to Deuteronomy's presentation of choice between life and death, blessing and curse.

references.name: Deuteronomy (6th century BCE composition during Babylonian exile)
references.context: Historicized as a text composed during ancient Israel's Babylonian-imposed exile, reflecting theological responses to trauma and injustice.

references.name: Egyptian enslavement and Exodus tradition
references.context: Referenced as the foundational salvific narrative: 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God who delivered them from enslavement in Egypt.'

references.name: St. Paul's Letter to Philemon
references.context: Extended discussion of Paul's letter from prison regarding Onesimus, a enslaved Christian, and Paul's 'characteristic messy and circuitous' theological approach to liberation.

references.name: Baptismal theology (waters of baptism)
references.context: Used to argue for shared indissoluble status before God: 'because of the waters of baptism, he and Onesimus share an indissoluble status before God.'

references.name: Christmas hymn: 'Chains shall he break, for the slave is our brother'
references.context: Cited as embodying the baptismal principle that 'in his name, all oppression shall cease,' connecting to liberation theology.

references.name: Christological titles and imagery
references.context: Extended invocation of Jesus as 'Prince of Peace, the lily of the valley, the rose of Sharon, the bright and morning star, the wheel in the middle of the wheel, the friend to the friendless, a mother to the motherless, a strong tower, Mary's baby.'

references.name: Theologia crucis (Theology of the Cross)
references.context: The cross is presented not as a problem-solver but as God's companionship in suffering: 'God gains credibility not as a cosmic problem solver, but as a companion in the midst of life's various vicissitudes and challenges.'

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: The sermon emphasizes baptism and eucharist (bread and wine) as outward visible signs of God's election and care, capable of meeting 'the contours and curves of our complex experiences.'

references.name: Hebrews 13:5 (implied)
references.context: The promise 'I will never leave you. I will never forsake you' echoes the biblical assurance given through the cross.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: Implicit throughout the sermon's engagement with slavery, oppression, exile, and the claim that 'the path of liberation and freedom is not laid out for us with a map, but it is laid out for us with a cross.'

references.name: Existential theology
references.context: The sermon engages existential themes of uncertainty, vulnerability, and lack of control: 'I don't know how I got here. I don't know what I'm doing here. And I sure don't know how I'm going to get out of here.'

references.name: Augustine's confessional mode (implicit)
references.context: Personal testimony about family Bible study practices and honest admission of human limitation reflects Augustinian confessional tradition.

references.name: Mystical theological anthropology
references.context: The closing metaphor of humans as 'a shoreline composed of millions of grains of sand, not of our own making or our own choosing' suggests mystical understanding of human composition and divine knowledge.

primary_theme: Life's complexity cannot be reduced to simple binary choices between blessing and curse; instead, God meets us in the midst of suffering through the cross, offering companionship rather than easy answers.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with scholarly historical analysis, using personal testimony, historical-critical engagement with scripture, prophetic challenge against oversimplification, and liturgical-sacramental language to build theological argument through narrative accumulation rather than systematic logic.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Referenced as one of the four gospels in which Jesus proclaims the kingdom of God.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Referenced as one of the four gospels in which Jesus proclaims the kingdom of God.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Referenced as one of the four gospels in which Jesus proclaims the kingdom of God.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: Referenced as one of the four gospels; notes that John uses 'eternal life' rather than 'kingdom of God'.

references.name: 2016 U.S. Election
references.context: Preacher comments that the gospel reading about family division 'one would think that was written after the 2016 election.'

references.name: Hebrews
references.context: Cited for the phrase about God shutting the mouths of lions; extensive passage about mothers receiving dead by resurrection, public shame, taunting, and violence.

references.name: Red Sea crossing / Exodus narrative
references.context: Referenced as example of God delivering Israelites on dry ground.

references.name: Rahab
references.context: Referenced as figure whose life was preserved by God during a reconnaissance mission.

references.name: Gordon Lathrop
references.context: Identified as 'the great Lutheran theologian' who argues that baptism has a reputation as a family affair due to 'haunting of old customs' but is actually a sacrament that transcends family.

references.name: Lutheran theological tradition
references.context: Invoked through Gordon Lathrop's sacramental theology of baptism.

references.name: Christian creeds
references.context: Preacher notes 'The creeds don't explain it' regarding the nature of heaven and afterlife, indicating limits of creedal formulation.

references.name: Early church mothers and fathers
references.context: Referenced as having thought they understood the nature of heaven/afterlife, but the preacher characterizes their attempts as 'a shot in the dark.'

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: Central to the sermon's understanding of baptism as an action that 'burns a hole in the middle of the illusionary walls of our imaginations.'

references.name: Mystical theology / apophatic theology
references.context: Implicit in the preacher's embrace of mystery regarding the afterlife: 'we speak of eternal life, and we know that it's not linear and it's nothing like what we know now' with an 'ellipsis.'

references.name: Death and resurrection pattern
references.context: Preacher articulates a threefold pattern: Jesus' death and resurrection, baptismal death and resurrection, and the life-long waiting for literal death and resurrection, then extends this to everyday life cycles.

references.name: Climate crisis / environmental ethics
references.context: Preacher discusses climate crisis as an 'existential' challenge and argues for the interconnection of human exploitation of earth and of each other.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Embedded in the image of God as 'a flame at the center of the cosmos, burning but not consuming' and the conviction that God is 'between and within and without us'.

primary_theme: Baptism as a sacramental encounter with God that inaugurates a lifelong pattern of dying and rising, calling Christians to recognize God's presence in all of life's transformations and to live as stewards of creation in solidarity with those who suffer.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with prophetic challenge; blends personal narrative (baptisms performed that day), theological argument (via Lathrop and creedal tradition), scriptural witness (especially Hebrews), honest acknowledgment of Christian mystery, and urgent social-ecological concern. Uses metaphorical language ('flame at the center of the cosmos') alongside direct address to the congregation. Moves from liturgical particularity to cosmic scope.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Reference to Jesus proclaiming the kingdom of God in Mark (alongside Matthew, Luke, and John).

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Reference to Jesus proclaiming the kingdom of God in Matthew (alongside Mark, Luke, and John).

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Reference to Jesus proclaiming the kingdom of God in Luke (alongside Mark, Matthew, and John).

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: John uses 'eternal life' rather than 'kingdom of God' to refer to the same concept.

references.name: Gospel reading (Tenth Sunday after Pentecost)
references.context: Opening gospel lesson featuring family division: 'Father against son, son against father, mother against daughter, mother-in-law against daughter-in-law' (likely Luke 12:51-53 or Matthew 10:34-36).

references.name: The Creeds
references.context: The creeds do not explain the nature of heaven, eternal life, or the afterlife.

references.name: Early Church Fathers and Mothers
references.context: The early church fathers and mothers 'thought they knew what they were talking about' regarding the afterlife, but their claims are described as 'a shot in the dark.'

references.name: Gordon Lathrop
references.context: 'The great Lutheran theologian' who argues that baptism has a reputation for being a family affair 'due to the haunting of old customs that we can't seem to rid ourselves of,' though baptism is actually a great sacrament.

references.name: Unnamed college professor
references.context: Professor who articulated the threefold shape of death and resurrection: (1) Jesus died and was resurrected, (2) in baptism we die and are resurrected, (3) we live waiting for our literal death and resurrection.

references.name: Unnamed pastor friend and mentor
references.context: A female pastor who coined the insight that 'dysfunctional family is redundant' — dysfunction is simply 'dis' is how we function.

references.name: Book of Hebrews
references.context: Multiple allusions to Hebrews 11 (the 'Hall of Faith'): God shutting the mouths of lions, delivering Israelites across the Red Sea, preserving Rahab's life, giving mothers back their dead by resurrection, and God being a companion to those experiencing public shame and violence.

references.name: Exodus narrative (Red Sea crossing)
references.context: God delivered the Israelites on dry ground across the Red Sea—cited as an example of God's sustained action in history.

references.name: Story of Rahab
references.context: Rahab's life was preserved during a reconnaissance mission, exemplifying God's use of ordinary people to bring about justice.

references.name: Sacramental theology / Sacramental understanding
references.context: Baptism as one of Christianity's 'great sacraments,' understood as an action that 'burns a hole in the middle of the illusionary walls of our imaginations,' and the waters as sacramental waters that warn of God's immediate involvement.

references.name: Trinitarian theology
references.context: Reference to 'God the Holy Trinity' in the baptismal formula; later reference to God as holding together contradictions (death and resurrection, power and vulnerability).

references.name: Mystical theology / Medieval mysticism (implicit)
references.context: Description of God as 'a flame at the center of the cosmos, burning but not consuming' echoes apophatic mysticism and negative theology's paradoxical language about divine presence.

references.name: Environmental theology / Eco-theology (implicit)
references.context: Extended reflection on climate crisis as an 'existential' challenge; assertion that 'our life and the life of the land and the sea' are 'interconnected' and that 'we cannot in any way extricate ourselves from that reality'; discussion of exploitation of earth and exploitation of each other as linked.

references.name: Incarnational theology (implicit)
references.context: The repeated assertion that God is not 'up there or out there or simply not involved with this dimension at all' reflects incarnational theology emphasizing God's immanent involvement in creation and history.

references.name: The concept of 'the other side of the mortal coil'
references.context: Phrase evoking Hamlet's meditation on death and the unknowability of the afterlife; sermon argues Christian baptism is 'a recurring rehearsal for what meets us on the other side of this mortal coil.'

primary_theme: Baptism as a sacramental encounter with God's sustaining love that initiates believers into a pattern of death and resurrection they will experience throughout their lives, and that commitment to baptismal life requires ecological and social justice witness in an age of existential planetary crisis.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection blended with prophetic challenge; conversational yet theologically sophisticated; mixing personal testimony, liturgical commentary, theological reasoning, and moral exhortation; uses paradox and mystery as a central rhetorical mode to hold together faith and uncertainty.

references.name: Luke's Gospel
references.context: The preacher notes that Luke's version of the Lord's Prayer differs from Matthew's version.

references.name: Matthew's Gospel
references.context: Referenced as an alternative version of the Lord's Prayer that differs from Luke's account.

references.name: Luke 11:3 (or Matthew 6:11)
references.context: The third line of the Lord's Prayer: 'Give us the bread we need for today.'

references.name: King James Version
references.context: The preacher cites the KJV rendering: 'give us day by day our daily bread.'

references.name: John 6:35
references.context: Jesus saying, 'I am the bread of life,' cited during discussion of bread in scripture.

references.name: The Loaves and the Fishes
references.context: Multiple Gospel accounts of Jesus multiplying bread and fish; first bread story recalled by congregation.

references.name: 1 Kings or 2 Kings (Elijah and the hearth cake)
references.context: The preacher acknowledges Elijah's bread story as 'a more obscure story' from the Kings.

references.name: Exodus (Manna from heaven)
references.context: The wilderness provision of manna to the Israelites, explained as meaning 'what is this?'

references.name: Genesis 14:18-20 (Abraham and Melchizedek)
references.context: Abraham encounters the mysterious priest Melchizedek from Salem who brings bread and wine; described as 'strange and obscure.'

references.name: The Last Supper
references.context: Bread and wine in the Eucharistic meal, cited as a major bread narrative in scripture.

references.name: Passover/Feast of Unleavened Bread
references.context: When Israelites arrive in the promised land, their week-long celebration culminates in a feast of unleavened bread; parents tell children the liberation story.

references.name: The Gospels (accounts of Jesus' ministry)
references.context: Multiple stories of bread multiplied and distributed throughout Gospel accounts of Jesus announcing God's reign.

references.name: The Lord's Prayer / The Our Father
references.context: The ancient prayer said countless times over 21 centuries; central text for the entire sermon.

references.name: Christian and Jewish liturgical tradition
references.context: The preacher references the memory of Christians and Jews throughout history praying the Lord's Prayer.

references.name: Eucharistic/Sacramental theology
references.context: The sermon culminates in discussion of taking, blessing, breaking, and sharing the sacramental loaf as a symbol of God's love.

references.name: Christological mysticism
references.context: The idea that Christ prays inside us and the Holy Spirit nurses us to wholeness reflects contemplative Christian spirituality.

references.name: Liberation theology / prophetic critique
references.context: The sermon's critique of food crises, famine, and economic injustice, and the vision of an economy where 'every person is fed' reflects liberationist concerns.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The preacher resists spiritualizing bread and insists on its tangible, physical reality present in all cultures.

references.name: Apophatic theology (negative theology)
references.context: God is described as 'both revealed and hidden, intimate and mysterious,' reflecting via negativa traditions.

primary_theme: God's providential care and generosity as revealed through the petition for daily bread in the Lord's Prayer, with implications for eucharistic practice and economic justice.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic blend combining congregational dialogue, personal narrative (grandmother's prayer), scriptural exegesis, sacramental theology, and prophetic challenge to social consciousness.

references.name: Luke's Gospel
references.context: The sermon opens by discussing 'the first line of Luke's version of the Lord's Prayer, which you notice is different from Matthew's.'

references.name: Matthew's Gospel
references.context: Referenced as the contrasting version of the Lord's Prayer compared to Luke's account.

references.name: Luke 11:3 (or Matthew 6:11)
references.context: 'Give us the bread we need for today' or 'give us day by day our daily bread' (King James Version)—the third line of the Lord's Prayer.

references.name: King James Version
references.context: Cited as the rendering of the Lord's Prayer: 'give us day by day our daily bread.'

references.name: 1 Kings and 2 Kings
references.context: Referenced for the story of 'Elijah and the hearth cake,' which the preacher identifies as coming from 'one of the kings.'

references.name: Exodus
references.context: Referenced for the story of 'Manna from heaven,' God's provision of food to the Israelites in the wilderness.

references.name: John 6:35 (or John's Gospel)
references.context: Referenced for Jesus saying 'I am the bread of life' in John's gospel.

references.name: The Last Supper
references.context: Referenced as a bread narrative: 'There's bread and wine in the Last Supper.'

references.name: Genesis 14:18-20 (Melchizedek narrative)
references.context: When Abraham encounters the mysterious priest Melchizedek from Salem, 'Melchizedek places bread and wine before him,' described as 'a strange and obscure story.'

references.name: The Passover/Feast of Unleavened Bread
references.context: Referenced when 'the Israelites arrive in the land of promise, their week-long celebration culminates in a feast that consists of unleavened bread,' with the instruction to tell children why they celebrate.

references.name: The Feeding of the Five Thousand (and other Gospel multiplication narratives)
references.context: 'The loaves and the fishes' and 'numerous stories of bread multiplied and distributed throughout the Gospel accounts of Jesus' work.'

references.name: The Lord's Prayer / Our Father
references.context: The entire sermon is an exposition of the Lord's Prayer, specifically the petition for daily bread, within 'the context not only of the Lord's Prayer or the Our Father, but in the memory of Christians and Jews throughout history.'

references.name: Sacramental theology / Eucharistic practice
references.context: Invoked in the closing section: 'Take the sacramental loaf, bless the sacramental loaf, break the sacramental loaf, and share the sacramental loaf, a symbol of the shape of God's love for the whole world.'

references.name: Christological mysticism / Prayer theology
references.context: The sermon invokes 'the crucified and risen Christ, praying inside of us' and 'the Holy Spirit who is always nursing us to wholeness and fullness of stature.'

references.name: Spiritualization of language (rejected)
references.context: The preacher explicitly rejects the temptation 'to spiritualize the word bread and make it something intangible, and invisible,' choosing instead to 'focus on the tangible, physical, tactile food.'

references.name: Liberation theology / Economic justice
references.context: Implicit in the closing prophetic vision: 'an invitation to imagine a world wherein God is in charge. A world in which every person is fed. Every person is full. Every person is forgiven,' and the critique of 'food crises and famine' and the vision of 'an economy where we ask for and receive enough.'

primary_theme: The petition for daily bread in the Lord's Prayer calls Christians to recognize God as the ultimate provider and to work toward a world of economic justice where all people are fed, forgiven, and whole.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral exposition combined with prophetic challenge—the preacher uses biblical narrative examples, congregational dialogue, personal memoir (the grandmother's prayer), scholarly biblical translation analysis, sacramental theology, and concludes with a prophetic vision of economic justice.

references.name: Donyell McCray
references.context: Preacher borrows the phrase 'mystery, humility, and risk' from theologian Donyell McCray to describe the nature of the disciples' mission.

references.name: Luke 10:1-12 (The 70/72 Disciples)
references.context: Primary text for the sermon; Jesus sends out 70 (or 72 in some translations) disciples with instructions to carry no purse, bag, or sandals.

references.name: Isaiah 66:12-13
references.context: God described as a mother who comforts her children and nurses infants; used to characterize God's nurturing nature.

references.name: Matthew 10:1-15 (The 12 Disciples)
references.context: Referenced as the more well-known sending, contrasted with the sending of the 70.

references.name: Luke 10:30-37 (The Good Samaritan)
references.context: Allusion to the parable to illustrate how shalom requires systemic justice, not just individual charity.

references.name: Genesis 17-21 (Abraham and Sarah)
references.context: Called as witnesses in a hypothetical celestial courtroom regarding God's promises fulfilled in old age.

references.name: Daniel 3 (Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego in the fiery furnace)
references.context: Listed as witnesses to God's promise-keeping power.

references.name: Luke 1 (Mary and Joseph)
references.context: Called as witnesses to God's promises.

references.name: John 11 (Lazarus, Mary and Martha)
references.context: Called as witnesses to God's power to keep promises, particularly resurrection.

references.name: John 4 (Woman at the well)
references.context: Referenced as a witness to God's promises in John's Gospel.

references.name: Luke 7:1-10 (Roman centurion and his servant)
references.context: Called as witness to Jesus' healing power and God's promise-keeping.

references.name: Luke 8:40-56 (Jairus and his daughter)
references.context: Called as witness to Jesus raising the dead.

references.name: Luke 4:18 (Isaiah 61:1 - Year of Jubilee)
references.context: Referenced when describing Jesus' mission to announce good news to the poor and recovery of sight to the blind and the year of Jubilee.

references.name: Jericho Road (parable context)
references.context: Used as example of systemic injustice requiring structural solutions, not just individual aid.

references.name: Gospel hymn tradition: 'Have you any rivers that seem uncrossable?'
references.context: An 'old gospel song' quoted to frame divine promise; associated with the tradition of African American spiritual music.

references.name: Shalom
references.context: Extended exploration of the Hebrew concept of shalom as holistic peace/wholeness, including living wage, healthcare, housing, food security, and systemic justice.

references.name: African American testimonial tradition ('I have tried God for myself')
references.context: Preacher invokes ancestral wisdom claiming personal experience of God as lawyer, doctor, friend, and confidant.

references.name: Marshall McLuhan
references.context: Phrase 'the medium is the message' attributed to 'marketing experts' but rooted in McLuhan's media theory.

references.name: Bonhoeffer's 'cheap grace' tradition
references.context: Contrast between 'cheap calm' and authentic 'holistic peace' echoes Bonhoeffer's critique of superficial grace.

primary_theme: God's promise of holistic peace (shalom) and wholeness is announced through vulnerable, trust-based discipleship that relies on divine providence rather than material security, exemplified in Jesus' sending of the 70 disciples and God's restoration of Jerusalem.
rhetorical_style: Blended approach combining pastoral reflection, prophetic challenge, legal/courtroom metaphor, and African American sermonic tradition (call-and-response, testimonial witness, extended celebration of God's power), with scholarly attention to biblical translation and historical context.

references.name: Dorothy Fortenberry
references.context: Playwright and screenwriter cited for her 2017 essay about attending Catholic Mass; quoted on trusting the Church's doctrinal formulations and the tension between abstract doctrine and lived belief.

references.name: Dorothy Fortenberry, The Handmaid's Tale
references.context: Noted that Fortenberry formerly wrote for and produced the Hulu series, establishing her credibility as a cultural observer.

references.name: Andrei Rublev's Trinity
references.context: Icon referenced as adorning the worship leaflet; invoked as visual representation of Trinitarian theology during prayer.

references.name: Rowan Williams
references.context: Welsh poet and Archbishop quoted as saying 'God happens' in prayer, that the life of the Trinity becomes our life.

references.name: The Mountain Goats
references.context: Fortenberry's essay makes a joke comparing the Nicene Creed ending to 'the title of a Mountain Goats album' (humorous reference to the band).

references.name: Nicene Creed
references.context: Implicit reference in Fortenberry's quote about reciting 'a long list that starts with I Believe'; directly referenced in liturgical context as containing Trinitarian formula.

references.name: Baptismal Formula (Trinitarian)
references.context: Quoted directly: 'I baptize you in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit' as the liturgical utterance spoken over the baptized.

references.name: Liturgical blessing formula
references.context: Referenced as 'Blessed be God, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit,' spoken in the liturgy.

references.name: Romans 8:26
references.context: Implicit reference when discussing 'groans and moans within you that are too deep for words'—echoing Paul's language about the Spirit's intercession.

references.name: Apostles' Creed / Traditional Creedal Language
references.context: Referenced in the final doxology: 'Praise for the Father Almighty, maker of heaven and earth. Praise for the only Son of God through whom all things were made.'

references.name: Christian mystical tradition
references.context: Invoked through discussion of mystics, prophets, and practitioners of Christian faith who speak of mystery as God's domain of hiding and revealing.

references.name: Medieval mystical theology / Apophatic theology
references.context: Implicit in the emphasis on unknowability, mystery beyond comprehension, and the critique of attempting to rationalize or explain the Trinity.

references.name: Liturgical scholarship / Sacramental theology
references.context: Reference to 'liturgical scholars' who teach that Holy Communion is the 'repeatable dimension of holy baptism,' connecting sacraments in a unified theological vision.

references.name: Consumeristic / Wellness-culture critique
references.context: Identified as the contemporary cultural problem: approaching prayer and sacraments as 'performance evaluation' rather than relational being.

references.name: Patristic / Early Church teaching on the Trinity
references.context: Invoked when stating 'It has long been the church's teaching' regarding the Trinity's role in prayer as addressing Father through Christ in the Spirit.

references.name: Human being vs. human doing
references.context: Philosophical anthropology distinguishing ontological being from functional doing; used to critique performance-oriented spirituality.

references.name: God as creative Word
references.context: References God speaking a 'capital W word of invitation' at baptism, drawing on theological language of God's creative utterance (logos theology).

primary_theme: The Trinity is not a doctrine to be explained but a living mystery to be experienced in prayer, baptism, and Eucharist—calling us from consumeristic performance into genuine encounter with God's circular dance of love.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with prophetic critique; narrative-driven (opens with comedy analogy); balanced between scholarly reference (liturgical scholars, Rowan Williams) and conversational accessibility; builds from intellectual tension toward mystical affirmation; uses contemporary cultural critique (consumerism, wellness culture) to make theological points urgent.

references.name: The Bible - Genesis
references.context: Genesis begins in a garden, establishing God's interest in creation and natural order.

references.name: The Bible - Revelation
references.context: Revelation ends the Bible with a city (New Jerusalem), demonstrating God's interest in civilization and human organization.

references.name: Revelation 21 (New Jerusalem vision)
references.context: Vision of new Jerusalem that Christians often defer to eschatological future rather than embodying in present.

references.name: Psalms 13
references.context: Quoted for the psalmist's lament: 'How long, O Lord? Will you forget me forever?' — model for Christian lament.

references.name: Acts 11
references.context: Appointed reading for Fifth Sunday of Easter about early church debates on circumcision and inclusion of gentiles; Peter's vision of the sheet with unclean animals.

references.name: Pastor Jonathan McIntosh
references.context: Evangelical pastor from Memphis with whom Greer had a podcast discussing the biblical metanarrative from garden to city.

references.name: Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
references.context: Referenced for his 'I Have a Dream' sermon at Mason Temple Church of God in Christ (April 3, 1968) and his prophetic critique of Christianity's deferral of justice to heaven.

references.name: Martin Luther King Jr. - 'I Have a Dream' / 'Mountaintop' Sermon
references.context: Greer paraphrases King's critique: 'I need shoes down here on earth' and 'We need a new Jerusalem to break in on earth,' not just in heaven.

references.name: Mason Temple Church of God in Christ
references.context: Headquarters of the largest Black Pentecostal denomination; site where King preached the night before his assassination.

references.name: Lectionarypage.net
references.context: Referenced as resource for readers to find appointed scripture readings for the Fifth Sunday of Easter.

references.name: Jewish legal and religious tradition (circumcision practice)
references.context: Circumcision on eighth or ninth day as marker of covenant membership; background to early church's inclusion debate.

references.name: Early Christian/Apostolic tradition
references.context: Christianity as sect of Judaism; emphasis on argument and debate as part of Christian DNA, inherited from Jewish interpretive traditions.

references.name: Liberation theology / Prophetic theology
references.context: Implicit in Greer's framing of the gospel as demanding justice 'on earth' and challenging systemic racism and food insecurity as spiritual crises.

references.name: Christian lament tradition
references.context: Greer emphasizes the importance of lament in Christian life, drawing on Psalms and the permission to voice suffering to God.

references.name: Eschatological (end-times) theology
references.context: Critiqued for deferring the New Jerusalem to the future ('pie in the sky when you die') rather than embodying it now.

references.name: Christian martyrology tradition
references.context: Greer uses the language of 'martyrdom' for King's assassination, placing him in the Christian tradition of martyrs.

references.name: Incarnational theology (implicit)
references.context: The emphasis on God's interest in embodied, earthly life—food, community, cities—reflects incarnational theology that God cares about material reality.

references.name: Prophetic critique of structural injustice
references.context: Following King, Greer connects food insecurity and grocery store closure to systemic racism and white supremacy as interconnected evils.

primary_theme: The gospel demands the breaking in of the New Jerusalem not in some distant eschatological future but in embodied, material form now—particularly through addressing systemic racism, food insecurity, and the besieging of Black communities, following the prophetic witness of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic challenge combined with pastoral lament and narrative storytelling; uses biblical metanarrative (garden to city) to reframe Christian responsibility; draws on personal experience (Memphis) and scholarly scripture analysis (Acts 11 circumcision debate) to build theological argument; invokes King's prophetic legacy to challenge present-day Christian complicity in structural injustice.

references.name: Psalm 13
references.context: Quoted directly: 'How long, O Lord? Will you forget me forever?' as example of psalmists' comfort with expressing discomfort and exasperation with suffering in prayer.

references.name: Genesis (opening with garden)
references.context: Used in biblical metanarrative conversation: 'Genesis begins in a garden' as contrast to Revelation's ending.

references.name: Revelation (ending with city)
references.context: Completion of metanarrative: 'Revelation ends the Bible with a city,' establishing God's interest in civilization and common life.

references.name: Revelation 21 (New Jerusalem vision)
references.context: The appointed reading for Fifth Sunday of Easter; 'this vision of a new Jerusalem' critiqued as often deferred to afterlife rather than embodied in present.

references.name: Acts 11
references.context: Appointed reading for Fifth Sunday of Easter; used to illustrate early church debate about circumcision and inclusion of uncircumcised believers.

references.name: Acts 11 (Peter's vision of sheet)
references.context: Peter sees 'a great white sheet' with unclean animals and hears voice saying 'eat these animals,' challenging Jewish dietary law.

references.name: Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
references.context: Cited as prophetic figure; his 'mountaintop sermon' delivered at Mason Temple Church of God in Christ on April 3rd (night before assassination/martyrdom) is extended throughout sermon.

references.name: Martin Luther King Jr., 'I Have a Dream' / Mountaintop Sermon
references.context: Paraphrased: King critiques Christianity focused on heavenly shoes ('all God's children got shoes') and asserts 'I need shoes down here on earth' and 'I need the new Jerusalem to break in on earth.'

references.name: Pastor Jonathan McIntosh
references.context: Memphis evangelical pastor with whom Greer discussed biblical metanarrative of garden to city; appeared on their Memphis podcast.

references.name: Mason Temple Church of God in Christ
references.context: Headquarters of largest Black Pentecostal denomination in United States; location where MLK preached his final sermon.

references.name: Jewish tradition of circumcision and dietary law
references.context: Early church debate centers on whether non-Jewish converts must follow Jewish customs (circumcision, dietary restrictions) to belong to God of Israel.

references.name: Christian tradition (argument and disputation)
references.context: Greer notes 'Christians love to argue, and that's in the DNA of the church' and traces this to Jewish roots: 'we receive that tradition from our Jewish roots as Christians.'

references.name: Liberation theology / Prophetic Christianity
references.context: Implicit throughout sermon's emphasis on material justice (grocery stores, shoes, food as human right), embodied eschatology, and struggle against white supremacy and systemic violence.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Implicit in critique of deferring justice to heaven; 'new Jerusalem to break in on earth' reflects realized eschatology emphasizing God's presence in material, present reality.

references.name: Lectionarypage.net
references.context: Resource recommended for congregants to read appointed lectionary passages for Fifth Sunday of Easter.

primary_theme: The Church must lament suffering and work for justice in the present world rather than deferring hope to heaven, as exemplified by Martin Luther King Jr.'s prophetic call for a 'new Jerusalem' that breaks into earth amid systemic racism and violence.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic pastoral preaching combining narrative testimony (Memphis experience, MLK's witness), biblical-theological argument (garden-to-city metanarrative), pastoral invitation (lament as spiritual practice), and prophetic challenge (addressing white supremacy and food justice) with direct congregational engagement ('What newness do you need tonight?').

references.name: Black Christian Preaching Tradition
references.context: Greer grounds his sermon in 'Black Christianity in the U.S. has a rich tradition of portraying Jesus as a trickster' and references 'countless black preachers' describing Jesus' death and resurrection as a 'sly operation.'

references.name: Br'er Rabbit Folk Tale
references.context: Greer draws an extended parallel between the Br'er Rabbit and Br'er Fox story and Jesus' resurrection, arguing this 'overlap of death, resurrection, and trickery is most likely borrowed from the West African folk tale that eventually became an African American folk character.'

references.name: West African Folk Tradition
references.context: Identified as the source of the trickster motif that evolved into the Br'er Rabbit character and is embedded in Black Christian interpretations of Christ.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: The sermon's primary biblical text; Greer emphasizes that 'The crucified and risen Christ, especially in John's gospel, is playful, joyful, mysterious' and references the appearance to Mary Magdalene at the empty tomb.

references.name: John 20:11-15 (Mary Magdalene at the tomb)
references.context: Greer quotes: 'They have taken away my Lord, and I don't know where they've put him... Thinking he was the gardener, she replied, Sir, if you have carried him away, tell me where you have put him, and I will get him.'

references.name: Genesis 2-3 (Garden of Eden narrative)
references.context: Greer references 'Adam and Eve, two people to whom God entrusted a garden, but who ended up facing exile from their delightful former home' as foundational to his garden imagery and redemption theology.

references.name: Adam and Eve
references.context: Tradition asserts that Christ's crucifixion at Golgotha takes place atop 'the skull in question is that of Adam,' creating theological continuity between the Fall and Resurrection.

references.name: Church of the Holy Sepulchre
references.context: Greer notes that both the Garden (resurrection site) and Golgotha (crucifixion site) are located within this church, making their proximity theologically significant.

references.name: Cole Arthur Riley
references.context: Greer cites Riley from 'the fear chapter of this here flesh': 'I believe fear has the holy potential to draw out awe in us, to lead us into deeper patterns of protection and trust, to mold us into people engaged in the unknown, capable of making mystery of it instead of terror.'

references.name: Cole Arthur Riley, Fear (book)
references.context: Greer quotes from Riley's work on fear as a tool for deepening spiritual engagement with mystery and resurrection.

references.name: Jesus' Healing Ministry
references.context: Greer references Jesus' 'healing of the sick' as part of his work cultivating what Adam and Eve failed to cultivate, demonstrating 'that pain does not have to have the final word.'

references.name: Jesus' Parables
references.context: Greer uses Jesus' use of parables as an example of cultivation through 'natural fertilizer that is only activated through puzzlement, confoundedness, and a realization that no matter how many answers we'd like to seem confident in,' the divine can only be perceived through unknowing.

references.name: Resurrection Appearances (John's Gospel)
references.context: Greer references three post-resurrection episodes demonstrating Christ's playfulness: the ability to veil itself from recognition, walk through closed doors, eat breakfast, and miraculously fill nets with fish.

references.name: Medieval/Mystical Tradition (implicit: apophatic theology)
references.context: Greer's emphasis on encountering God when 'the GPS is malfunctioning and we don't know which way to turn' and the notion that God can 'often only be perceived' through unknowing reflects apophatic/mystical theological language.

references.name: Liberation Theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's grounding in Black Christian tradition and emphasis on trickery as spiritual resistance against death, hell, and captors reflects liberationist interpretive frameworks.

primary_theme: Easter represents God's playful and mysterious trickery against death, grounded in Black Christian tradition's portrayal of Jesus as a trickster figure (drawing from West African folk wisdom) who cultivates new creation through resurrection, drawing believers into deeper engagement with mystery and fear.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with extended folk-tale analogy, theological exegesis of Scripture, prophetic reinterpretation of Black tradition, pastoral invitation, and literary quotation. Greer moves between playful homiletical voice (Satan on Peloton), scholarly biblical analysis (garden tomb geography), folkloric narrative (Br'er Rabbit), and contemplative theological reflection.

references.name: Genesis 1-2 (Garden of Eden)
references.context: The preacher opens the biblical garden motif by noting that in Genesis, 'the first human beings are placed in a garden,' establishing gardens as spaces of divine provision and human flourishing.

references.name: Esther 7 (King Ahasuerus's palace garden)
references.context: Referenced as part of the broader biblical pattern: 'King Ahasuerus escapes to his palace garden in a fit of rage,' illustrating gardens as places of refuge and emotional respite.

references.name: Luke (Jesus's parable about a gardener and her garden)
references.context: Cited as an example of the garden motif throughout Scripture, though the specific parable is not quoted or identified by verse.

references.name: Amos 9:14-15 (Rebuilding ruins and planting gardens)
references.context: God promises Israel that 'they will rebuild their urban ruins and plant gardens and enjoy their fruit,' connecting gardens to restoration and hope.

references.name: Jeremiah 29:5-6 (Building houses and planting gardens in exile)
references.context: The prophet tells God's people in Babylon to 'build houses and live in them, plant gardens and eat what they produce,' illustrating gardens as spaces of sustainable living even in captivity.

references.name: John 19:41-42 (Garden burial of Jesus)
references.context: The opening text and central scriptural focus: 'There was a garden in the place where he was crucified, and in the garden there was a new tomb in which no one had ever been laid.' The preacher notes that John uniquely emphasizes the garden setting of the crucifixion and resurrection.

references.name: St. Romanos the Melodist (6th century lyricist and musician)
references.context: Quoted extensively to articulate the theological connection between Adam's death and Christ's redemption: 'When death saw Jesus on the cross, it said, I am forced to hand over Adam and those descended from him. They were given to me by a tree, but now a tree leads them back again to paradise.'

references.name: Cole Arthur Riley, This Here Flesh
references.context: Cited for the metaphor of God as a seamstress: 'On the day the world began to die...God becomes a seamstress,' and quoted at length about God making clothes from animal skin (Genesis 3:21 context) to restore human dignity and presence before the maker.

references.name: Church of the Holy Sepulchre tradition (Adam's skull buried at Golgotha)
references.context: The preacher invokes the ecclesiastical tradition that 'Jesus was crucified over the place where Adam's skull is buried,' creating a typological connection between the first and last Adam.

references.name: Cruciform/cross-shaped life theology
references.context: The preacher critiques a fashionable movement among theologians and preachers to encourage believers to seek 'cruciform' or 'cross-shaped lives' as imitation of Christ, arguing this tradition misses how Christ's incarnation demonstrates solidarity with human suffering rather than requiring Christians to pursue suffering.

references.name: Genesis 3:21 (God making garments)
references.context: Implicit reference in Cole Arthur Riley's reflection on God as seamstress making clothes from animal skin for Adam and Eve after the Fall, representing God's grace and restoration.

references.name: John 19:34 (Blood and water from Jesus's side)
references.context: Referenced when the preacher describes the Roman soldier piercing Jesus and 'blood and water flow from his side,' which he imaginatively connects to the idea of Christ's blood flowing backward to Adam.

references.name: Typological theology (First Adam / Last Adam)
references.context: The sermon employs Pauline typological thinking (implicit reference to 1 Corinthians 15:45) connecting Adam's exile from Eden to Christ's redemption, mediated through the Church of the Holy Sepulchre tradition.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The preacher emphasizes God's embodied presence and solidarity through the incarnation: God 'taking on flesh, living among us, teaching and healing among us...dying among us and like us' demonstrates divine identification with human suffering rather than demanding human imitation of divine suffering.

primary_theme: The theological proximity and inseparability of Golgotha (the place of death and suffering) and the garden (the place of rest and resurrection) as divine affirmation that God stands in solidarity with human suffering and transforms instruments of death into signposts of life.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection with scholarly and theological underpinnings; the preacher interweaves personal seminary experience, biblical exegesis, ecclesiastical tradition, and contemporary theological sources to move from intellectual understanding to spiritual comfort and challenge. The style is meditative, using concrete images (gardens, trees, stitches, blood flowing) to make abstract theological claims visceral and accessible.

references.name: Isaiah (Prophet)
references.context: The Fourth Sunday in Lent is called 'Laetare Sunday' from Isaiah's words 'Laetare Jerusalem' (Rejoice, O Jerusalem), sung in medieval churches.

references.name: Laetare Sunday / Laetare Jerusalem
references.context: Medieval liturgical tradition designating the Fourth Sunday in Lent as a day of rejoicing within the Lenten season, derived from Isaiah.

references.name: Cole Arthur Riley
references.context: Author cited for her perspective on joy as a central spiritual virtue; the preacher recommends her book to the congregation.

references.name: This Here Flesh
references.context: Cole Arthur Riley's book, which the preacher 'highly recommends' and quotes regarding joy becoming a central virtue in spirituality.

references.name: Dr. King (Martin Luther King Jr.)
references.context: Quoted as saying 'Don't be so heavenly minded that you're no earthly good' to illustrate the balance between spiritual practice and earthly engagement.

references.name: Spiritual bypassing
references.context: A phenomenon where spiritual worldview becomes so otherworldly that one cannot face present reality; contrasted with authentic Christian joy.

references.name: Christian liturgical practice (Lenten discipline)
references.context: The practice of abstention and self-denial during Lent, discussed as a spiritual discipline intended to reorient one toward Christ.

references.name: Baptism / Baptized life
references.context: Referenced as the foundation of Christian identity and disciplined spiritual life that should lead to joy.

references.name: Resurrection / Death and resurrection (Easter mystery)
references.context: The theological center of Christian joy; the preacher argues that losing perspective on resurrection joy undermines spiritual discipline.

references.name: Self-help cottage industry
references.context: Described as a multi-billion dollar industry associated with spiritual bypassing and false spirituality in contemporary culture.

primary_theme: Christian joy—rooted in Easter resurrection—must be the ultimate goal of spiritual discipline and practice; joy is not escapism but full embodied engagement with reality, even amid suffering and challenges.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with prophetic challenge; the preacher uses personal anecdote (ice cream and wisdom teeth) to establish credibility and humor, then deepens the argument through liturgical history, contemporary cultural criticism, and quoted authorities, concluding with a prophetic call to joyful engagement with the world's real crises.

references.name: Isaiah (Prophet)
references.context: The Fourth Sunday in Lent derives from the Latin phrase 'Laetare Jerusalem' (Rejoice, O Jerusalem) from Isaiah, sung in medieval churches on Lent 4.

references.name: Laetare Sunday / 'Laetare Jerusalem'
references.context: Medieval liturgical practice naming the Fourth Sunday in Lent and its Latin refrain from the prophet Isaiah.

references.name: Cole Arthur Riley
references.context: Riley is cited as the author of 'This Here Flash' regarding joy as a spiritual virtue; the preacher quotes: 'joy which once felt as frivolous as love to me has become a central virtue in my spirituality.'

references.name: This Here Flash
references.context: Cole Arthur Riley's book is recommended and quoted to support the argument that joy is a central Christian virtue.

references.name: Dr. King (Martin Luther King Jr.)
references.context: Quoted as saying, 'Don't be so heavenly minded that you're no earthly good,' to caution against spiritual bypassing.

references.name: Spiritual bypassing
references.context: A psychological/spiritual concept described as a phenomenon where one's spiritual worldview becomes so otherworldly that it prevents engagement with present reality; explicitly distinguished from the Christian call to joy.

references.name: Lenten discipline / Lent
references.context: Christian liturgical practice of self-denial and abstention that frames the entire sermon's exploration of discipline and joy.

references.name: Ash Wednesday
references.context: The beginning of Lent, marking when the preacher's Lenten discipline of ice cream abstention was to begin.

references.name: Easter / death and resurrection
references.context: The theological heart of Christian joy; the preacher warns against losing perspective on 'the joy of Easter, the joy found in death and resurrection.'

references.name: Baptism / baptized life
references.context: Referenced as a frame for understanding Christian disciplined life and the call to follow Jesus.

references.name: Self-help cottage industry / spiritual consumerism
references.context: Critiqued as a multi-billion dollar industry that can lead to spiritual bypassing in contemporary culture.

primary_theme: Christian joy, grounded in Easter hope and disciplined engagement with reality, must remain central to spiritual practice lest self-denial become spiritually hollow and disembodied.
rhetorical_style: Narrative pastoralism with prophetic critique: the preacher begins with personal anecdote (broken Lenten discipline), moves to liturgical-historical explanation (Laetare Sunday), then develops the theological argument through contemporary social critique and strategic literary/spiritual references, concluding with an exhortatory challenge about humility and humor amid world suffering.

references.name: Book of Exodus (Moses narrative)
references.context: The entire sermon is built around the burning bush encounter with Moses, including his infancy (Exodus 1-2), flight to Midian (Exodus 2:11-22), and the divine encounter at Horeb (Exodus 3).

references.name: Exodus 3:14
references.context: God's response to Moses asking for a name: 'I am that I am' (King James Version) and 'I will be who I will be' (Robert Alter's rendering).

references.name: Exodus 3:15-16
references.context: God invokes lineage: 'The God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob' as divine self-identification.

references.name: Noah's Ark narrative
references.context: Preacher notes Moses' mother's 'creative resistance' using a wicker ark recalls 'another ark story' (Genesis 6-8).

references.name: Psalm 23:4
references.context: Reference to 'the valley of the shadow of death' in describing God as fearless companion to David.

references.name: Robert Alter
references.context: Cited for his 'scholarly rendering of the original Hebrew' translation of Exodus 3:14 as 'I will be who I will be.'

references.name: Julian of Norwich
references.context: Referenced as a historical figure to whom God 'makes all things well,' citing her mystical theology.

references.name: Hagar
references.context: Invoked as biblical character experiencing God 'who sees' (Genesis 16:13).

references.name: Miriam
references.context: Moses' sister referenced as 'prophet Miriam' experiencing God who 'creates paths in the great sea' (Exodus 15).

references.name: Mary (Virgin Mary)
references.context: Referenced as figure whose flesh is God's flesh, whose blood is God's blood, nourishing God at her breasts (Christological reference).

references.name: Sarah
references.context: Invoked as experiencing God as 'the laugh that comes after a lifetime of disappointment' (Genesis 18:10-15).

references.name: African American spiritual/hymnic tradition
references.context: Preacher invokes 'the words of my ancestors' describing God as 'heart fixer and mind regulator, a mother to the motherless and a father to the fatherless.'

references.name: African American hymnic epithets
references.context: Phrases like 'rose of Sharon, bright morning star, lily of the valley' reference traditional African American spiritual language and hymnic tradition.

references.name: Southern genealogical/kinship tradition
references.context: Preacher reflects on 'invocation of lineage' practice in the South: 'Roderick's son or Pat's son or Ferry's grandson,' connecting biblical identity to Southern identity formation.

references.name: William Faulkner (implicit)
references.context: The phrase 'the ever-present past' echoes Faulknerian concepts of how the past refuses to be left behind, particularly relevant to Southern and African American experience.

references.name: Mystical theology tradition
references.context: The sermon employs apophatic theology (God as elusive, unnameable, defying categories) and emphasis on divine ineffability.

references.name: Process theology or panentheistic tradition (implicit)
references.context: God described as responsive, adaptive ('I will be what you need me to be'), suggesting process-oriented rather than static divine nature.

references.name: Psalm 27:10
references.context: Underlying reference in 'a mother to the motherless and a father to the fatherless.'

references.name: King James Version
references.context: Cited explicitly as translation source for 'I am that I am' (Exodus 3:14).

primary_theme: God's identity is not confined to fixed categories or comfortable expectations, but is instead dynamically responsive to human need and grounded in ancestral lineage; the divine name 'I will be who I will be' reveals a God who defies control and containment while remaining intimately present to each person's particular circumstances.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling interwoven with scholarly exegesis, pastoral reflection, and prophetic challenge; the preacher uses biographical narrative of Moses combined with comparative biblical typology, personal memoir (Southern childhood), and African American sermonic tradition to build theological argument about divine nature.

references.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
references.context: Preacher references King's term 'beloved community' as an equivalent to Jesus's concept of the kingdom of God or commonwealth of God.

references.name: Luke's Gospel
references.context: The sermon is based on Luke's 'Sermon on the Plain,' which is contrasted with Matthew's 'Sermon on the Mount.'

references.name: Matthew's Gospel
references.context: Preacher references Matthew's version of the sermon, commonly called the 'Sermon on the Mount,' as parallel to Luke's account.

references.name: Luke 6:17-26 (Sermon on the Plain / Beatitudes)
references.context: The primary scriptural basis: Jesus comes down from the mountain with twelve disciples and speaks to a large crowd about blessedness and woes.

references.name: Common English Bible translation
references.context: Preacher cites this translation's rendering of the location as 'a great, large, level place.'

references.name: Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12 / Luke 6:20-23)
references.context: Preacher discusses Jesus's pronouncements on who is blessed, addressing how they are descriptive rather than prescriptive.

references.name: Matthew 4:4 (or similar temptation/kingdom references)
references.context: Implied reference to Jesus healing the sick, feeding the hungry, and setting prisoners free as manifestations of God's kingdom.

references.name: Isaiah 61:1-2 (implied)
references.context: Implicit allusion to Jesus's mission of healing the sick, feeding the hungry, and freeing prisoners (language Jesus claims as his own in Luke 4:18).

references.name: Luke 4:18 (Jesus's inaugural mission statement)
references.context: Underlying reference to Jesus's proclamation of healing, feeding, and liberation at the beginning of his ministry.

references.name: Black Baptist tradition
references.context: Preacher references the cultural and liturgical practice of Black Baptist churches, where members are called 'brother' or 'sister' and use finger signals during service.

references.name: Liberation theology (implied)
references.context: The sermon emphasizes God's preferential option for the poor, hungry, and weeping; inversion of earthly power structures; and the kingdom's present realization.

references.name: Pentecostalism (referenced implicitly)
references.context: Preacher invokes the expectation of miraculous healing in church and references a co-worker wondering if the preacher has joined a Pentecostal church.

references.name: Prophetic theology / eschatology
references.context: Jesus 'peers over into God's future' and 'breaks into the present' with good news that contrasts the nightmare of the current world with God's dream.

references.name: Sacramental theology (Eucharistic presence)
references.context: Preacher references Jesus present 'as bread and wine, flesh and blood' with healing power emanating from prayer stations, suggesting real presence and transformative power.

primary_theme: Jesus's Sermon on the Plain inaugurates a present, tangible kingdom of God where healing, feeding, and liberation are not future promises but current realities available to all who encounter Jesus, inverting worldly power structures and inviting the church to expect God's transformative action today.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-driven pastoral preaching grounded in personal memoir (Sister Charlie May Cook anecdote), combined with scholarly biblical interpretation (comparison of Luke vs. Matthew, discussion of prescriptive vs. descriptive readings), prophetic challenge to Christian proclivity for misinterpretation, and urgent exhortation to contemporary expectation of divine healing and wholeness.

references.name: Luke's Gospel (Sermon on the Plain)
references.context: The preacher references 'Luke's Sermon on the Plain' as the scriptural basis for the sermon, contrasting it with Matthew's version.

references.name: Matthew's Gospel (Sermon on the Mount)
references.context: Mentioned as the parallel version of Jesus's teaching, 'commonly called the Sermon on the Mount.'

references.name: Common English Bible (translation)
references.context: The preacher cites the Common English Bible's translation of the location where Jesus speaks: 'a great, large, level place.'

references.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
references.context: King is cited by name for his phrase 'beloved community' as a term for Jesus's project or the reign of God.

references.name: The Beatitudes
references.context: The preacher discusses the Beatitudes as descriptive rather than prescriptive statements about God's kingdom.

references.name: Jesus's healing ministry
references.context: Multiple references to Jesus healing the sick, feeding the hungry, and setting prisoners free as tangible manifestations of God's kingdom.

references.name: Black Baptist church tradition
references.context: The preacher reflects on his experience growing up in a 'small Baptist church in Fort Worth, Texas' and describes Black Baptist church practices (the 'brother or sister' convention, the finger gesture when leaving).

references.name: Pentecostal church tradition (implicit reference)
references.context: The preacher humorously notes that describing healing at church would make a coworker wonder 'when you joined a Pentecostal church,' implying healing expectations are more characteristic of Pentecostalism.

references.name: Jesus as present in bread and wine (Eucharistic theology)
references.context: The preacher invokes the theological understanding that 'Jesus is present here as bread and wine, flesh and blood with his healing power emanating' through the Eucharist and prayer stations.

references.name: Occupying force (Roman occupation metaphor)
references.context: The preacher uses the metaphor of an 'occupying force' breaking 'down the door' to describe how Jesus confronts oppressive worldly conditions.

references.name: Kingdom of God / Reign of God / Commonwealth of God
references.context: Multiple terminological variations for Jesus's project, with the preacher noting these different terms all refer to the same present-tense reality.

primary_theme: Jesus's proclamation of God's kingdom in the Sermon on the Plain is not a future promise but a present reality with tangible power to heal, transform, and liberate those who encounter it.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with prophetic challenge and pastoral reflection; the preacher uses a personal anecdote (Sister Charlie May Cook) to warm and engage the audience, then transitions into scholarly biblical interpretation followed by a prophetic call for congregants to expect God's healing and transformative power in their present lives.

references.name: Mark Oakley
references.context: Church of England priest quoted extensively on the baptism of Christ, who 'weds sacramentality, action, and contemplation in a sermon about the baptism of Christ, at a prayer service commemorating the 50th anniversary of the decriminalization of homosexuality in the United Kingdom.'

references.name: Mark Oakley's extended quotation on baptism
references.context: A lengthy sermon passage describing Jesus being 'pushed under the surface' of baptismal waters, hearing only his own heartbeat, then hearing God's affirming voice from heaven, followed by wilderness temptation.

references.name: Desmond Tutu
references.context: Cited as an exemplary 'active contemplative and contemplative activist' whose life demonstrates how 'God's love and initiative in holy baptism sustains them to live lives of contemplation and action,' particularly in dismantling apartheid in South Africa.

references.name: John Allen's authorized biography of Archbishop Desmond Tutu
references.context: Used as the source for detailed information about Tutu's daily prayer practices, including his 4 a.m. personal prayers, devotional reading, morning prayer with clergy, Holy Eucharist, and approximately six to seven hours of daily prayer.

references.name: Henry Nouwen
references.context: Author of 'a wonderful book about the temptations of Jesus in the wilderness' arguing that 'the greatest temptation was to take a shortcut through the wilderness' and that 'Jesus' practices of prayer and fasting and silence and contemplation enabled him by God's power to resist that temptation.'

references.name: Gospel of Luke (Luke 3 and Luke 4)
references.context: Used to establish the narrative sequence: 'Luke 3, Jesus is baptized. Luke 4, the spirit leads him' into the wilderness; the original Greek word for 'leads' is interpreted as 'forced him into the wilderness.'

references.name: Matthew 4 / Luke 4 (Temptation narrative)
references.context: Referenced implicitly when describing Satan's 'three different shortcuts to wealth, fame, and acclaim' offered to Jesus in the wilderness.

references.name: Baptism theology / sacramental tradition
references.context: The sermon invokes Christian sacramental theology, emphasizing baptism as a 'touchstone' and 'centering experience' that grounds Christian identity and action; includes reference to the liturgical practice of making the sign of the cross with holy water upon entering church.

references.name: Contemplative-activist synthesis
references.context: The sermon argues against false dichotomies between contemplation and action, drawing on the Christian contemplative-activist tradition exemplified by figures like Tutu and Nouwen.

references.name: Liberation theology / African American religious resistance
references.context: Invoked when discussing the dismantling of 'American apartheid' through 'prayer meetings, Bible studies, marching, the singing of freedom songs, and preaching forms of black Christian contemplation.'

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Referenced in the phrase: 'the good news of Christ's unconditional love in his incarnation, his death, burial, and resurrection.'

references.name: Belovedness theology / identity in Christ
references.context: The sermon's core claim that Christians derive identity from God's declaration in baptism: 'you are mine, I love you, you make me happy' and operate 'as a person who is loved and treasured by God beyond measure.'

references.name: Mystical interiority / apophatic tradition
references.context: Evident in Oakley's description of baptism as creating silence and drowning out 'the noise from the shore, the opinions, the dogmas, the criticisms, and empty chatter,' echoing medieval mystical traditions of entering into God's presence through silence.

references.name: Walt Whitman's 'Song of Myself'
references.context: The opening phrase 'if you contain multitudes, it is because God contained multitudes first' is a theological reworking of Whitman's famous line 'I am large, I contain multitudes.'

primary_theme: Christian baptism functions as a sacramental touchstone that integrates contemplative prayer and prophetic action, enabling believers to resist worldly temptations and work for justice grounded in their identity as beloved children of God.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic synthesis combining scholarly citation, extended literary quotation, liturgical observation, biographical narrative exemplification (Tutu), theological argument against false dichotomies, and repeated exhortatory calls to prayer and action; moves from intellectual framework to spiritual practice to social witness.

references.name: Mark Oakley
references.context: Church of England priest whose sermon on Christ's baptism at a 2017 prayer service commemorating the decriminalization of homosexuality in the UK is quoted extensively to demonstrate how sacramentality, action, and contemplation are inseparable.

references.name: Mark Oakley, sermon quote on Jesus's baptism
references.context: Extended quotation describing Jesus being pushed underwater, hearing only his heartbeat, then hearing God's affirming voice from heaven, followed by wilderness temptation—used to illustrate the baptism as a centering experience.

references.name: Luke 3 (Jesus's baptism)
references.context: Referenced as the gospel account of Jesus being baptized, establishing the scriptural foundation for the sermon's theological reflection.

references.name: Luke 4 (wilderness temptations)
references.context: The Spirit 'leads' (or in original language, 'forces') Jesus into the wilderness where he faces three temptations to wealth, fame, and acclaim.

references.name: Matthew 4 or Luke 4 (Satan's three temptations)
references.context: Allusion to Satan offering Jesus 'three different shortcuts to wealth, fame, and acclaim' in the wilderness.

references.name: Archbishop Desmond Tutu
references.context: Exemplified as an 'active contemplative' and 'contemplative activist' whose life of prayer (6-7 hours daily) sustained his work dismantling apartheid in South Africa.

references.name: John Allen, authorized biography of Archbishop Tutu
references.context: Source cited for detailed account of Archbishop Tutu's daily prayer practices, including 4 a.m. personal prayers, devotional reading, morning prayer with clergy, and evening prayers.

references.name: Henri Nouwen
references.context: Author of a book on Jesus's wilderness temptations arguing that the greatest temptation was taking a shortcut, and that Jesus's practices of prayer, fasting, silence, and contemplation enabled him to resist.

references.name: Henri Nouwen, book on Jesus's temptations in the wilderness
references.context: Referenced for its argument that the central temptation in the wilderness was to take a shortcut, and that contemplative practices enabled Jesus to resist.

references.name: Walt Whitman, 'Song of Myself'
references.context: The phrase 'contain multitudes' alludes to Whitman's famous line 'Do I contradict myself? Very well then I contradict myself, (I am large, I contain multitudes.)'—used to argue that complexity and apparent contradiction are natural to human experience.

references.name: Contemplative vs. activist false dichotomy
references.context: The sermon critiques Christian thinkers who falsely pit contemplation against activism, arguing instead for their inseparability—addressing a recurring theological debate.

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: The sermon invokes sacramental theology—particularly baptism and Holy Eucharist—as embodied practices that pull believers 'out of our heads into our bodies' and ground Christian identity.

references.name: Liberation theology (implied)
references.context: The extended discussion of dismantling apartheid and American apartheid (voting restrictions, housing discrimination, educational segregation) through prayer and Christian action reflects liberation theology's concern with systemic injustice.

references.name: Black Christian contemplative traditions
references.context: Referenced in the context of the civil rights movement: 'prayer meetings, Bible studies, marching, the singing of freedom songs, and preaching' as forms of 'black Christian contemplation' that dismantled American apartheid.

references.name: Christian practice of making the sign of the cross at holy water stoops
references.context: Referenced as a embodied liturgical practice through which congregants affirm 'Christ's baptism is my baptism,' demonstrating the inseparability of sacrament and personal identity.

references.name: Incarnation, death, burial, and resurrection of Christ
references.context: Referenced as the core content of the gospel ('good news of Christ's unconditional love') that baptized Christians are enabled to bear to the world.

primary_theme: Christian baptism is a sacramental-contemplative foundation from which believers integrate prayer and action to resist systemic injustice and live as beloved children of God.
