references.name: Shiphrah and Puah (Exodus 1:15-22)
references.context: Named as Hebrew midwives who experience loss of control when 'Pharaoh orders the Hebrew midwives to murder all the firstborn boys.'

references.name: Jesus calming the storm (Matthew 8:23-27, Mark 4:35-41, Luke 8:22-25)
references.context: Referenced as example of spiritual mastery: 'That like Jesus, they can nap while a storm rages around them. That they can rest while a pandemic rages around them.'

references.name: Sergeant Gerald Westbrook
references.context: Cited for his 1964 definition of 'soul' that combines 'strength and a feeling of helplessness, faith and a feeling of hopelessness,' which becomes central to the sermon's conclusion.

references.name: Lift Every Voice and Sing (hymn/spiritual)
references.context: Referenced as an example of how 'The song that begins "Lift Every Voice and Sing" portrays soul.'

references.name: James Meredith
references.context: Cited as historical example: 'James Meredith's fight to integrate the University of Mississippi manifests soul.'

references.name: Mary Lou Williams
references.context: Referenced as composer whose 'compositions display soul.'

references.name: Holy Spirit (theological concept)
references.context: Invoked in opening prayer: 'Come Holy Spirit, take our minds and think through them, take our lips and speak through them, take our hearts and set them on fire.'

references.name: Grief as spiritual practice/virtue
references.context: Implicit reference to pastoral theology and contemplative tradition where grief is reframed as a natural, even holy response to loss and love.

references.name: God's solidarity with human suffering
references.context: Rooted in incarnational theology: 'God grieves all these losses alongside us because God loves us. We are never alone in our grief.'

primary_theme: In the face of pandemic loss and disrupted routines, grief represents both a natural emotional response and a spiritual witness to love; understanding the illusory nature of control while recognizing God's compassionate presence in suffering.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling (personal anecdotes about office plants and routine), philosophical meditation on control and grief, prophetic consciousness of systemic loss, and lyrical conclusion drawing on African American intellectual and spiritual traditions.

references.name: Genesis 3
references.context: God's question to Eve and Adam 'Where are you?' parallels God's question to Elijah, both concerning self-awareness rather than physical location.

references.name: 1 Kings 19 (Elijah on Mount Horeb)
references.context: Primary biblical narrative: Elijah in a cave on Mount Horeb experiences wind, earthquake, and fire, but God is not in these manifestations; God speaks in 'sheer silence.'

references.name: 1 Kings 18 (Mount Carmel showdown)
references.context: Elijah's previous encounter with God, where fire descended from heaven and consumed the offering, establishing Elijah's expectation of God's dramatic power.

references.name: BBC's Planet Earth
references.context: Used as a cultural reference point to describe the dramatic style of the natural phenomena (wind, earthquake, fire) on Mount Horeb: 'in the style of the BBC's Planet Earth.'

references.name: The Handmaid's Tale (Hulu series)
references.context: Referenced via screenwriter Dorothy Fordenberry's observation about religious certainty and the nature of faith and doubt.

references.name: Dorothy Fordenberry
references.context: Screenwriter for 'The Handmaid's Tale' and lay Catholic who articulates the paradox of religious belief: the tension between faith and certainty, stating 'I believe too much to be certain about' atheism.

references.name: Biblical commentator (unnamed)
references.context: Cited to explain that 'On Mount Carmel, God spoke through fire. Here at Horeb, God speaks in a more subtle language, for the deity is by no means limited to seismic manifestations.'

references.name: Mystical tradition of divine encounter
references.context: The concept of encountering God through 'sheer silence' and 'still small voice' reflects apophatic or mystical theology emphasizing God's transcendence beyond dramatic manifestations.

references.name: African American church tradition
references.context: Referenced as one mode of encountering God: 'the drumbeat and hand clapping and tambourine playing of their storefront church.'

references.name: Monastic/contemplative tradition
references.context: Invoked through reference to encountering God 'in the quietness of a monastery in a rural setting' and in the 'sheer silence of a majestic cathedral.'

references.name: Liberation theology / prophetic tradition
references.context: Implicit in the reference to encountering God 'in the chaos of a protest in the streets of our cities,' linking divine presence to social justice action.

primary_theme: God cannot be confined to a single mode of revelation; believers must cultivate imaginative openness to encounter the divine in both dramatic and subtle forms, in chaos and silence alike.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-theological exposition with pastoral reflection; the preacher develops understanding through extended biblical storytelling (the Elijah narrative), contemporary cultural examples (Fordenberry), scholarly commentary, and inclusive pastoral application, concluding with prophetic affirmation of God's universal presence and love.

references.name: Book of Jeremiah, Chapter 28
references.context: The entire sermon is built on Jeremiah 28, which presents a courtroom scene between two prophets: Hananiah (false prophet) and Jeremiah (true prophet).

references.name: Jeremiah 28:1-3
references.context: Hananiah's false prophecy is introduced, setting up the framework for identifying false prophets.

references.name: Jeremiah 28:4-9
references.context: The main text cited; today's lectionary passage covering the exchange between Hananiah and Jeremiah.

references.name: Jeremiah 28:6
references.context: Jeremiah's response where he mirrors Hananiah's prophecy but drops the specific date, contrasting true and false prophecy.

references.name: Hebrew Bible prophetic tradition
references.context: The preacher notes that false prophets use the same formulaic opening ('Thus says the Lord') as genuine prophets throughout the Hebrew Bible.

references.name: Minister Candice Yonina Simpson
references.context: Cited as providing a scholarly distinction: 'the prophets are so in touch with the present that they appear to predict the future,' differentiating prophecy from fortune-telling.

references.name: Scholarly circles (biblical scholarship)
references.context: Referenced for the maxim that 'prophets are not fortune-tellers,' establishing an intellectual framework for analyzing Hananiah's errors.

references.name: Homiletical tradition (sermon construction)
references.context: The preacher references being 'taught to limit sermons to one point' early in the sermon, reflecting on classical homiletical pedagogy.

references.name: Optimism vs. Hope (theological distinction)
references.context: A sophisticated theological opposition developed throughout the final section: 'Optimism says things will be okay, while hope says I will be okay.' This reflects deeper theological anthropology about faith rooted in God's character rather than circumstance.

primary_theme: How to identify false prophets by recognizing three markers: they sound authentic (mimicking true prophetic language), they make date-specific predictions (fortune-telling rather than prophecy), and they offer optimism about circumstances rather than hope rooted in God's faithfulness.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-pastoral analysis combined with contemporary application; the preacher uses a modern email scam analogy to illustrate deception, scholarly references to establish credibility, and direct contemporary examples (pandemic, civic unrest) to make the biblical text urgently relevant to the 2020 audience.

references.name: Genesis 1
references.context: Referenced throughout as the creation account where God repeatedly calls creation 'good'; used to frame God as liturgist calling forth beauty from formlessness.

references.name: Exodus 20:4-6 (Idolatry/Golden Calf)
references.context: The commandment forbidding idol-crafting is discussed as concern with 'putting God in a box'; the Israelites fashioning a golden calf while Moses is on Mount Sinai is cited as a historical example of limiting God.

references.name: Exodus 3:14 (Burning Bush)
references.context: God's statement 'I am who I am' is quoted as the unspeakable name that resists definition.

references.name: Genesis 18 (Three Visitors/Sarah and Abraham)
references.context: Referenced as the biblical narrative portrayed in Andrei Rublev's 15th-century icon.

references.name: James Baldwin
references.context: American writer cited for his critique of Christianity's association with white power and his statement that God must 'make us larger, freer and more loving.'

references.name: The Fire Next Time
references.context: Baldwin's 1963 book on the history of Christianity and white identity; quoted extensively on God's purpose and the danger of theological conceptualization.

references.name: Andrei Rublev
references.context: Russian Orthodox monk cited as creator of the 15th-century icon of the Trinity (depicting Sarah, Abraham, and three visitors) used as exemplar of iconography.

references.name: Elizabeth Eaton
references.context: Presiding Bishop of the ELCA quoted responding to the massacre at Mother Emanuel Church by Dylann Roof; her statement frames the tragedy as personal to the church community.

references.name: Arminian Orthodox theology/theologian
references.context: An unnamed Arminian Orthodox theologian is cited for the interpretation of Genesis 1 as a 'divine litany' with God as both liturgist and respondent.

references.name: Anglican theological tradition
references.context: Anglican theologians are cited as having called God 'the ground of all being.'

references.name: The Ground of All Being
references.context: Phrase used by Anglican theologians to describe God; this is characteristic of Paul Tillich's existential theology though Tillich is not named.

references.name: Hebrew Bible/Prophetic Literature
references.context: The preacher notes that throughout the Hebrew Bible, prophets struggle to expand Israel's imagination about who and where God is, resisting limited conceptualizations.

references.name: Jewish theological tradition
references.context: Ancient Israelites and modern-day Jews are cited as correctly maintaining that God's name is unspeakable.

references.name: Christian Trinitarian theology
references.context: Early Christians called the divine reality 'the Holy Trinity,' whose feast is celebrated on Trinity Sunday; framed as one naming among many for the same reality.

references.name: Iconography (Eastern Orthodox tradition)
references.context: The practice of icon veneration is contrasted with idolatry; icons are presented as windows to the divine, not objects of worship themselves—a portal for imagination rather than limitation.

references.name: Theodicy
references.context: The preacher's mother poses a theodicy question: 'How can people who claim to love God also be racist?' This philosophical tradition of questioning divine justice is framed as a method of understanding God as question rather than answer.

references.name: Dylann Roof massacre (June 2015)
references.context: The 2015 shooting at Mother Emanuel African Methodist Episcopal Church in Charleston, South Carolina is used as a contemporary case study in how theological idolatry enables white supremacist violence.

references.name: Image of God (Imago Dei)
references.context: The preacher references 'those made in God's image' and questions whether white supremacists conceive of God as made in their image instead of the reverse—a reversal of the imago dei doctrine.

primary_theme: God cannot be reduced to fixed conceptual categories or limited idolatrous forms; instead, God is best understood as an expansive, dynamic, unspeakable mystery that makes us 'larger, freer, and more loving,' and the danger of theological idolatry (reducing God to a single identity or ideology) enables violence and supremacy.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic challenge combined with pastoral reflection and narrative storytelling; the preacher weaves together academic theological discourse (iconography, trinitarian theology, theodicy), contemporary witness (family memories, James Baldwin, the Dylann Roof case), and scriptural exegesis to build a moral argument about the spiritual dangers of theological reductionism.

references.name: Kim Ji-young, Born 1982
references.context: Preacher reads this novel about a Korean woman rendered invisible by those around her; uses it as the primary lens for understanding erasure and invisibility in contemporary life.

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Referenced throughout as the crucified and risen Christ whose presence sustains hope; his way of life, teaching, meals, trial, crucifixion, and resurrection are cited as the language of God's new creation and love.

references.name: Easter/Eastertide
references.context: Sermon is preached on the Sixth Sunday of Easter, invoking the liturgical calendar and resurrection theology as framework for hope during pandemic.

references.name: Liturgy (etymology and meaning)
references.context: Preacher defines liturgy as 'public work' and connects essential workers' labor to the original Christian understanding of liturgy as work done by few for the many.

references.name: Liberation theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's focus on invisible laborers, structural injustice (sexism, racism, classism), and preferential attention to the marginalized reflects liberation theological commitments.

references.name: Prophetic tradition (implicit)
references.context: The sermon critiques systemic erasure and names injustice (meat processing plants, pandemic exposure, invisible labor) in the tradition of prophetic speech.

references.name: Romans 8:28-39 (implicit)
references.context: The assertion that 'there is nothing you can do or feel that will separate you from God's love' echoes Romans 8:38-39 about separation from God's love.

references.name: Genesis creation narrative (implicit)
references.context: References to 'God's image disfigured,' 'new creation,' and humans as bearers of God's image evoke the Genesis creation account.

references.name: Incarnational theology (implicit)
references.context: The presence of Christ in workers and the sacralization of ordinary labor reflects incarnational and sacramental theology.

references.name: Resurrection theology
references.context: The sermon concludes with 'every time we practice love, we are practicing resurrection,' connecting ethical action to Christian eschatology.

references.name: O Come, O Come Emmanuel (implicit)
references.context: The phrase 'the thrill of hope' appears to allude to the hymn 'O Come, O Come Emmanuel' ('O come, O come Emmanuel, and ransom captive Israel, that mourns in lonely exile here, until the Son of God appear. Rejoice! Rejoice! Emanuel shall come to thee, O Israel.').

primary_theme: Essential workers and marginalized people rendered invisible by society are beloved and cherished by God; practicing love is an act of resurrection that brings the invisible into community and builds God's new creation.
rhetorical_style: Narrative pastoral theology blended with prophetic social critique; uses the specific story of Ashut Deng and the novel Kim Ji-young, Born 1982 as entry points into theological reflection on erasure, dignity, and love during the COVID-19 pandemic.

references.name: Hebrew Bible scholar (unnamed)
references.context: One scholar of the Hebrew Bible has posited that the Psalms fall into one of three categories: orientation, disorientation, and reorientation.

references.name: Psalm 23
references.context: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want; Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death.

references.name: Psalm (unnamed, referencing despair)
references.context: How long, O Lord, will you forget me? Forever?

references.name: Psalm (unnamed, referencing reorientation)
references.context: When the Lord restored the fortunes of Zion, then was our mouth filled with laughter and our tongue with shouts of joy.

references.name: Psalm 116
references.context: I love the eternal one who has heard the voice of my supplication...The cords of death entangled me. The grip of the grave took hold of me.

references.name: Psalm (unnamed, referencing God's intimate knowledge)
references.context: The God who knit us together in our mother's womb is the same God who knows our most intimate feelings and desires.

references.name: Gospel of Luke 24:13-35 (Emmaus Road narrative)
references.context: The two disciples making the seven-mile trek between Jerusalem to Emmaus on the day of Jesus' resurrection, encountering the risen Christ.

references.name: Luke 24 (Cleopas)
references.context: The narrator offers the name of one of the disciples, Cleopas, the other goes unnamed.

references.name: Luke 24:17 (They stood still looking sad)
references.context: They stood still looking sad, the narrator says.

references.name: Luke 24:34 (Jesus' crucifixion and burial)
references.context: Jesus whose trial and crucifixion we witnessed, placed in a borrowed tomb at dusk on the first Good Friday.

references.name: Luke 24:32 (Did not our hearts burn within us)
references.context: Were not our hearts burning within us while he was talking to us on the road? While he was opening the scriptures to us, breaking bread with us?

references.name: Barry Motes (American artist)
references.context: American artist Barry Motes painted a compelling contemporary depiction of this scene; Motes' Supper at Emmaus takes place at a rest stop food court with Christ blessing KFC combos.

references.name: Orientation/Disorientation/Reorientation framework
references.context: A helpful interpretive lens when praying with the Psalms that categorizes spiritual-emotional states within Scripture.

references.name: Eucharist/Breaking Bread theology
references.context: The crucified and risen Christ as shape-shifting presider at the first Eucharist on Easter Day, recognized in the breaking of bread.

references.name: Resurrection theology (implicit Easter proclamation)
references.context: The Easter surprise: that even after long seasons of despair and disorientation, the crucified and risen one is right there with us.

primary_theme: In times of disorientation and grief (whether personal loss or pandemic isolation), honest admission of suffering before God opens us to encounter the risen Christ's compassionate presence, transforming despair into reorientation toward divine love.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling interwoven with pastoral reflection, using scriptural exegesis and contemporary artistic allusion to make the ancient Emmaus road story speak to modern pandemic-era suffering and spiritual seeking.

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Jesus demonstrates the new commandment through deed (foot washing) rather than persuasive discourse, and his love serves as the model for Christian action.

references.name: John 13:34-35
references.context: Direct quotation: 'I give you a new commandment, that you love one another. Just as I have loved you, you also should love one another. By this, everyone will know that you are my disciples if you have love for one another.'

references.name: Genesis
references.context: Referenced as the origin of God's love for all creation, where plants sprout forth, establishing the ancient roots of the commandment to love.

references.name: Hebrew Bible (Exodus and Exile narratives)
references.context: Cited as historical demonstrations of God's faithful guidance through wilderness, exodus, and national trauma of exile and return.

references.name: Mandatum Novum (Latin theological term)
references.context: The Latin name for Jesus' new commandment, which forms the etymological and theological basis for 'Maundy Thursday.'

references.name: Dr. Valerie Beemo
references.context: Cited as saying 'individual behavior is important for collective safety,' establishing the public health rationale for social distancing as an act of love.

references.name: Unknown scholar (justice and love)
references.context: Quoted as saying 'justice is what love looks like in public,' framing social distancing as a justice issue.

references.name: Dan Albergati
references.context: Poet whose work 'Things to do in the belly of a well' is cited in full to provide metaphorical framework for spiritual practice during isolation.

references.name: Dan Albergati's 'Things to do in the belly of a well'
references.context: Poem quoted extensively (8 lines) as a guide for contemplative practice and listening for love even during pandemic confinement.

references.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
references.context: Referenced as the institutional source of pandemic precautions that shape how the commandment to love must be lived out.

references.name: Existentialism (implied)
references.context: The phrase 'existential question' is used to describe the crisis of how to practice Eucharist and foot-washing during social distancing.

primary_theme: The new commandment to love one another must be practiced contextually and flexibly; during a global pandemic, love is expressed through social distancing and physical restraint rather than physical service, demonstrating that love adapts to meet the needs of each historical moment.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with prophetic reinterpretation; the preacher uses personal pastoral concerns, scriptural exegesis, contemporary poetry, and social ethics to resolve a theological paradox created by pandemic circumstances.

references.name: Genesis (Book of)
references.context: The entire sermon is structured around Genesis narratives, particularly the stories of Adam and Eve, Noah, the Tower of Babel, and Abraham and Sarah.

references.name: Genesis 1-2 (Creation and stewardship)
references.context: God invites Eve and Adam to steward and care for creation, establishing the pattern of gift-giving.

references.name: Genesis 6-9 (Noah and the Flood)
references.context: Noah builds an ark, survives the flood, and builds an altar after the waters recede; he blesses rather than gloats.

references.name: Genesis 11 (Tower of Babel)
references.context: The Tower of Babel represents the squandering of God's gift through the concentration of human power and the homogeneity of language and ambition.

references.name: Genesis 12:1-3 (Call of Abram)
references.context: God commands Abram and Sarai: 'Go forth from your land and your birthplace and your father's house to the land I will give you. I will bless you and make your name great and you shall be a blessing.'

references.name: Abraham (Abram/Abraham narrative)
references.context: Abraham is presented as a distant descendant of Noah (10 generations removed), called by God to leave the familiar and journey into mystery.

references.name: Noah (genealogical ancestor)
references.context: Noah appears as a wise figure who spends a lifetime building an ark in preparation, then blesses rather than gloats after the flood.

references.name: Adam and Eve
references.context: The first humans entrusted with the care of animals and land; they squander this initial gift from God.

references.name: The Wilderness (Lenten theme)
references.context: The sermon is titled 'The Wilderness – The Second Sunday in Lent,' invoking the liturgical season of Lent and its association with wilderness, testing, and spiritual discipline.

references.name: Pattern of gift and squander
references.context: The preacher identifies a theological pattern in Genesis: 'Gift, squander, gift, squander' as a metaphor for how sin operates across generations.

references.name: Sin as generational atrophy
references.context: Sin is characterized not as a 'pronounced one-time action' but as 'a generations-long process of atrophy, inattentiveness, and numbness'—suggesting existential or process theological perspectives.

references.name: Mystery and surrender to God
references.context: The sermon emphasizes that 'it is only in the unknown, the surrender to mystery, the surrender to God, to ambiguity, that the course is corrected'—echoing apophatic or mystical theology.

references.name: Course correction (divine intervention)
references.context: God offers 'dramatic course correction' when humanity pushes the limits of cruelty, isolation, and domination—a Pauline or Reformed theological concept of divine interruption.

references.name: Hegemony and homogeny (Tower of Babel critique)
references.context: The preacher uses the terms 'hegemony and homogeny' to describe the Tower of Babel as a concentration of power and sameness—suggesting postcolonial or liberation theological critique.

references.name: Episcopal identity (playful reference)
references.context: The preacher calls Sarai and Abram 'the original Episcopalians' in a playful reference to their willingness to move and embrace change—a light ecclesiological allusion.

references.name: Abraham (Lincoln) - the one who was shot
references.context: A student confuses Abraham (the biblical figure) with Abraham Lincoln; mentioned in passing to establish the need for clarification.

primary_theme: God persistently offers gifts and course corrections throughout human history, calling believers to abandon certainty and familiarity and embrace mystery and surrender, which paradoxically enables us to become blessings for others and the world.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-theological storytelling with pastoral reflection; the preacher weaves biblical narrative analysis with contemporary application, using humor and personal asides ('I'm sure Emily got tired of me...') to make abstract theological concepts accessible, while building toward an existential challenge about embracing ambiguity and mystery as essential to spiritual transformation.

references.name: Genesis (Book of)
references.context: The preacher reads Genesis chronologically during the week, exploring the genealogy from Adam through Noah to Abraham and the pattern of God's gift followed by human squandering.

references.name: Genesis 1-2 (Creation and stewardship)
references.context: God invites Eve and Adam to steward and care for creation, which they squander.

references.name: Genesis 6-9 (Noah and the Flood)
references.context: Noah is described as a distant ancestor of Abraham who builds an ark far from water, survives the flood, builds an altar, and blesses his descendants.

references.name: Genesis 11 (Tower of Babel)
references.context: The Tower of Babel is presented as Noah's descendants squandering God's gift by seeking to concentrate human power through architectural and linguistic homogeneity.

references.name: Genesis 12 (Abraham's Call)
references.context: God calls Abram and Sarai to leave their land, birthplace, and father's house with the promise 'I will bless you and make your name great, and you shall be a blessing.'

references.name: Adam and Noah genealogy (10 generations)
references.context: The preacher notes there are 10 generations between Adam and Noah and 10 generations between Noah and Abraham, creating a structural pattern of gift and squander.

references.name: Abraham (not Lincoln)
references.context: The sermon opens with a humorous anecdote about a student confusing the biblical Abraham with Abraham Lincoln, who was 'shot in the theater.'

references.name: Mystery and surrender theology
references.context: The preacher argues that 'it is only in the unknown, the surrender to mystery, the surrender to God, to ambiguity, that the course is corrected.'

references.name: Apophatic theology / Negative theology
references.context: The emphasis on God's revelation through dreams, intuition, and prayer rather than certainty and measurable knowledge reflects apophatic theological tradition.

references.name: Incarnational theology / God's persistent love
references.context: The sermon's central claim that 'the love of God persists' and God offers 'dramatic course corrections' reflects incarnational and covenantal theology.

references.name: Sin as gradual atrophy
references.context: Sin is characterized as 'not a pronounced one-time action, but a generations-long process of atrophy, inattentiveness, and numbness' rather than as discrete moral failures.

references.name: Episcopalian theology / liturgical tradition
references.context: The preacher jokes that Abram and Sarai 'were the original Episcopalians,' invoking the tradition's association with movement, change, and adaptability.

primary_theme: God's persistent love manifests through a pattern of gift, human squandering, and divine course correction, culminating in Abraham's call to abandon certainty and embrace mystery as a model for believers today.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-driven pastoral reflection with biblical exegesis, interspersed with contemporary humor and anecdotes, moving from scholarly genealogical analysis to prophetic application and personal invitation.

references.name: The Nicene Creed
references.context: Preacher notes that the creed is recited every Sunday and is 'very heavy on belief' with repeated affirmations of 'we believe.'

references.name: Book of Common Prayer
references.context: Referenced for its teaching that 'the bond that God forms in baptism is indissoluble' and that baptismal love is permanent.

references.name: Paul Klee
references.context: Early 20th century artist whose work 'Joyful Mountain Landscape' appears on the handout and serves as a springboard for discussing inner spiritual landscape interrogation.

references.name: Jesus's baptism
references.context: Referenced as an example of receiving 'core identity of love' through baptism, paralleling Christian baptismal experience.

references.name: Christian baptism/sacrament of baptism
references.context: Central to the sermon's argument that baptism establishes an indissoluble bond of God's love that practices help us become conscious of.

references.name: Protestantism
references.context: Preacher critiques one downside of being Protestant: excessive focus on 'what we can do for God or what we can do for ourselves' to be more like Jesus.

references.name: Virginia Seminary
references.context: Mentioned as the alma mater of Broderick Greer in the introduction.

primary_theme: Christian practice (particularly during Lent) makes people increasingly conscious of God's love rather than pursuits of personal perfection; practices deepen awareness of baptismal belovedness.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling (mother's sayings, personal anecdotes) and dialogue with the congregation; conversational and self-aware rather than didactic.

references.name: Henri Nouwen
references.context: Harvard Divinity School professor and Roman Catholic priest described as 'one of the most profound and prolific theologians and experts on Christian spirituality in the 20th century'; his book on Christian leadership forms the intellectual foundation of the sermon.

references.name: In the Name of Jesus: Reflections on Christian Leadership
references.context: Nouwen's book written in late 1980s about Christian leadership in the 21st century; uses the structure of Jesus's temptations in the desert paired with disciplines to resist temptation; described as having 'coffee stains and wrinkled pages' from the preacher's repeated use.

references.name: Brian McLaren
references.context: Author cited for his discussion of Christian practices and comparison to Buddhism as a practice-based faith rather than belief-based system.

references.name: Finding Our Way Again
references.context: McLaren's work about Christian practices that sustain the baptized life; discusses how Buddhism is a practice, not a system of belief.

references.name: Richard Rohr
references.context: Episcopal theologian 'of great fame at St. John's Cathedral' in Colorado; cited for the principle that 'we practice our way into a new way of thinking' rather than thinking our way into new beliefs.

references.name: Dr. Judy Fentress-Williams
references.context: Hebrew Bible professor who taught the preacher about intertextuality in scripture reading—recognizing when certain words or literary tropes deserve attention.

references.name: Matthew 3
references.context: Gospel account of Jesus's baptism; read in context with Matthew 4 to show Jesus immediately entering the wilderness after baptism.

references.name: Matthew 4
references.context: Gospel account of Jesus's temptation in the wilderness for 40 days and 40 nights; contains the three temptations: turning stones to bread, throwing himself from the temple, and bowing to Satan.

references.name: Matthew 3:16-17
references.context: Jesus's baptism account: 'When Jesus had been baptized...the heavens were opened to him...the Spirit of God descending like a dove...voice from heaven: This is my son, the beloved, with whom I am well pleased.'

references.name: Matthew 4:3-4
references.context: First temptation: 'If you are the son of God, command these stones to become loaves of bread. But he answered, it is written, one does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God.'

references.name: Deuteronomy 8:3
references.context: Jesus's response to the first temptation quotes this Deuteronomy passage about living by God's word, not bread alone.

references.name: Exodus wilderness narrative
references.context: Israelites wandering in the wilderness for 40 years; used as intertextual parallel to Jesus's 40-day desert temptation.

references.name: Hagar narrative
references.context: Abraham's Egyptian concubine encountered in the wilderness; noted as 'the first person in the Bible to name God,' highlighting marginalized figures in Scripture.

references.name: Lenten discipline tradition
references.context: Ancient church practice where Lent originally served as a 40-day season of preparation for baptism, contrasted with baptismal revival patterns in Baptist tradition.

references.name: Intertextuality
references.context: Literary-critical method of reading scripture that recognizes meaningful connections between repeated words and tropes across biblical texts.

references.name: Practice-based spirituality
references.context: The theological principle that Christian faith is fundamentally a 'way of life' and practice rather than a system of doctrinal beliefs; contrasts with mere intellectual assent.

references.name: Baptist revivalism
references.context: Preacher's childhood tradition contrasted with Episcopal Lenten practice; cited through a friend's observation that 'Baptists have revivals and Episcopalians have Lent.'

references.name: Textual hermeneutics
references.context: The principle that 'a text without context is a pretext for a subtext'—emphasizing the importance of biblical reading in proper context.

primary_theme: Christian spiritual formation through the practice of discipline and temptation-resistance during Lent, grounded in Jesus's wilderness experience and rooted in the principle that we practice our way into new ways of thinking rather than think our way into new practices.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-scholarly synthesis combining personal narrative (preacher's conversion story and seminary experience), scholarly citations (Nouwen, McLaren, Rohr, biblical studies), and liturgical-theological reflection on church tradition; uses comparative religious analysis (Buddhism vs. Christianity), intertextual biblical reading, and group discussion pedagogy.

references.name: Isaiah 58:3-7
references.context: Core biblical text: 'Why did we fast and you did not see? We afflicted ourselves and you took no note.' God responds by condemning liturgical performance divorced from justice.

references.name: Exodus narrative (Egyptian slavery and liberation)
references.context: Referenced as the defining covenant memory: 'The God who sent the prophet Moses before Pharaoh...and extracted them from Egypt's economy of exploitation.'

references.name: Deuteronomy 15:15 (or similar covenant injunction)
references.context: 'Remember that you were enslaved in Egypt, and I redeemed you from there. Remember to never do to others what was done to you.'

references.name: Ash Wednesday liturgy
references.context: Referenced as the ecclesial practice where believers receive ashes and participate in fasting, which the sermon critiques as potentially becoming 'liturgical self-help routines.'

references.name: Scholar (unnamed) on ancient fasting practices
references.context: 'One scholar says that the underlying idea of such fasts was that such practices would engage the compassion of the deity and the disaster would end.'

references.name: Rabbit's foot theology
references.context: The preacher's coinage for transactional/superstitious theology: 'If I do this, God will do that. This is what one can call rabbit's foot theology.'

references.name: The poet (Isaiah as poet)
references.context: Isaiah 58 is referred to as 'the poet's words ringing in our inmost selves,' emphasizing the lyrical and prophetic authority of the text.

references.name: Quid pro quo theology
references.context: Recurring critique throughout: 'Relationship with God is not a quid pro quo, a favor for a favor' and the warning against 'liturgical quid pro quo.'

references.name: Covenant theology
references.context: The sermon's theological framework: God's covenantal relationship with Israel obligates justice and remembrance, not transactional performances.

references.name: Liberation theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's emphasis on God's concern for 'the downtrodden,' 'the wretched poor,' workers being oppressed, and the critique of 'economics of enslavement' reflects liberation theological analysis.

primary_theme: True fasting and Christian practice require not transactional liturgical performances seeking to impress God, but rather embodied commitment to justice for the oppressed, rooted in God's covenant memory of liberation and God's unconditional love that already claims us.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic pastoral preaching combining scriptural exegesis, prophetic critique of institutionalized religion, pastoral reassurance about God's grace, and social justice analysis; uses humor, repetition, and direct address to challenge congregational assumptions while grounding the message in biblical narrative and covenant theology.

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: Identifies Jesus as 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world' and understands sin as social, not merely personal.

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Presented as the liberating force who removes violence, impoverishment, and imperialism; 'the one who loved his enemy so fully that he died for them.'

references.name: The Gospel of John
references.context: John 1:29 - 'Here is the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world' (uses singular 'sin' rather than 'sins').

references.name: Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
references.context: Called 'a prophet not unlike John the Baptist' who identified 'the giant triplets of social sin': racism, extreme materialism, and militarism.

references.name: Martin Luther King Jr.'s 1967 Riverside Church Speech (April 4, 1967)
references.context: Speech opposing the Vietnam War, given one year before his assassination; contains the 'giant triplets of social sin' and the quote about machines and computers being valued more than people.

references.name: Martin Luther King Jr.'s 1964 Nobel Peace Prize Acceptance
references.context: King speaks of his Nobel Peace Prize as a commissioning from the international community and reframes it in relation to his commitment to Jesus Christ's ministry.

references.name: Candace Simpson
references.context: Baptist minister and friend cited for the insight that 'prophets are so attuned to the present that they appear to tell the future.'

references.name: Hebrew prophetic tradition
references.context: Referenced through the concept of 'Shalom' as the vision the Hebrew prophets called for—God's wholeness in creation.

references.name: Shalom (Hebrew concept)
references.context: Defined as peace and God's wholeness: 'when creation is at rest and flourishes' and 'people and ecosystems are given the space they need to restore and reach their fullest potential.'

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: The sermon's entire framework interpreting Jesus and prophets as liberators from systemic oppression (racism, materialism, militarism, imperialism) reflects liberation theology concerns.

references.name: Roman imperialism and colonial studies framework
references.context: The sermon employs analysis of occupied territories, statues and coins as tools of imperial propaganda, and the relationship between colonizer and colonized.

references.name: Matthew 5-7 or similar (enemy love teaching)
references.context: Implicit reference to Jesus's teaching on loving enemies, invoked when King says he must 'share life' rather than 'threaten with death' following Jesus's example.

primary_theme: Jesus as the Lamb of God liberates humanity not from individual sin alone but from systemic social sin—racism, materialism, and militarism—fulfilling the prophetic vision of Shalom that John the Baptist and Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. embodied.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic-pastoral narrative woven with scholarly historical analysis; the preacher builds from biblical exegesis (John's language of 'sin' vs. 'sins') through historical context (Roman occupation) to contemporary prophetic witness (King's 1967 speech), using extended quotation, literary precision, and theological interpretation to challenge listeners toward liberation and wholeness.

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: John identifies Jesus as 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world' and understands sin as social rather than merely personal.

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Presented as the liberating force who removes violence, impoverishment, and imperialism; the one who loves enemies and died for them.

references.name: Martin Luther King Jr.
references.context: His 1967 Riverside Church speech against the Vietnam War and his identification of 'the giant triplets of social sin' (racism, extreme materialism, militarism) are cited as prophetic analysis parallel to John the Baptist.

references.name: Candace Simpson
references.context: A Baptist minister friend cited for the definition that 'prophets are so attuned to the present that they appear to tell the future.'

references.name: Hebrew prophets
references.context: Referenced as originators of the concept of 'Shalom' (peace and wholeness in creation).

references.name: John 1:29
references.context: 'Here is the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world' - the opening and closing refrain of the sermon.

references.name: Matthew 5:44 (or Luke 6:27) - Love your enemies
references.context: Implicit reference to Jesus's teaching about loving enemies, referenced in King's quotation: 'the one who loved his enemy so fully that he died for them.'

references.name: Prophetic tradition
references.context: The sermon frames both John the Baptist and MLK as prophets attuned to divine justice and social transformation.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: The sermon's emphasis on liberation from 'cycles of racism, materialism, and militarism' and focus on social sin rather than individual sin reflects liberation theology's social critique.

references.name: Roman imperialism
references.context: Historical context provided about Palestinian Jews under occupation; John's understanding of sin as tied to imperial structures reflects postcolonial biblical interpretation.

references.name: Shalom (Hebrew concept)
references.context: Defined as more than mere peace—it is creation at rest and flourishing, wholeness where all beings reach their fullest potential.

references.name: MLK's 1964 Nobel Peace Prize
references.context: Referenced as a commissioning by the international community, though King emphasizes his commitment to Christ's ministry supersedes this recognition.

primary_theme: Jesus, through the lens of John the Baptist and the prophetic witness of Martin Luther King Jr., liberates humanity from systemic social sin (racism, materialism, militarism) rather than merely personal transgression, establishing God's shalom (wholeness and peace) in creation.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic-pastoral narrative, combining biblical exegesis, historical contextualization, direct quotation from King's speeches, and theological reflection on the nature of prophecy and social justice. The preacher uses repeated refrains ('Here is the Lamb'), narrative detail (Roman statues and coins), and contemporary relevance (King's birthday) to bridge ancient and modern concerns.

references.name: Mark (friend of preacher)
references.context: Extended quote about Jesus being submerged in baptism, hearing only his heartbeat while external voices are drowned out, then emerging to hear God's affirmation.

references.name: Matthew's Gospel
references.context: Referenced as the source of 'this iconic scene' of Jesus's baptism; preacher returns to 'that scene in Matthew' multiple times as anchor for reflection.

references.name: Matthew 3:13-17 (Baptism of Jesus)
references.context: The narrative account of Jesus being baptized, hearing the voice from heaven affirming 'You are my child, I love you, I delight in you.'

references.name: Matthew 4:1-11 (Temptation in the Wilderness)
references.context: Implicit reference to Jesus going 'into a wilderness where all the empty voices come back at him, tempting and torturing his mind' after baptism.

references.name: The Jordan River
references.context: The specific location of Jesus's baptism, used as a metaphorical space of silence and divine communion ('just under the surface of the Jordan').

references.name: Church teaching on Christ's sinlessness
references.context: Preacher notes 'the church teaches without sin' Jesus, which creates the theological puzzle of why he would need baptism if understood as sin-removal ritual.

references.name: Baptism as sacrament of solidarity
references.context: Preacher presents this theological framework as alternative to 'eternal fire insurance' model, emphasizing baptism as identification with human suffering and joy.

references.name: Christian grace theology
references.context: Preacher references criticisms of baptism from those who argue 'Christians believe in grace' and that baptism is a 'work' contradicting grace-based faith.

references.name: Baptist theology and full immersion baptism
references.context: Preacher identifies as Baptist ('I was Baptist, so we did full immersion') and reflects on his own childhood immersion baptism experience.

references.name: Epiphany liturgical calendar
references.context: Sermon is delivered 'On this feast of Jesus's baptism' described as 'The First Sunday after the Epiphany,' situating it within Christian liturgical tradition.

references.name: Implicit reference to consumerism and modern distraction
references.context: Preacher lists 'voices of cable news talking heads and commercial advertisers and billboards and phone bots and email bots and text bots' competing for attention—echoing prophetic critique of consumer capitalism.

references.name: Mystical/contemplative theology of silence
references.context: The sermon emphasizes 'quiet, near silent moments' underwater as spaces where only God's voice can be heard, drawing on contemplative tradition.

references.name: Social justice theology (implicit)
references.context: Preacher argues baptism 'brings us into proximity with everyone from migrant workers at our border to people escaping dangerous wildfires across Australia,' invoking theology of solidarity with the suffering.

primary_theme: Baptism is an act of spiritual centering and solidarity that temporarily silences the world's competing voices, allowing one to hear God's affirmation of beloved identity, and it commits the baptized to identification with all human suffering and joy.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combining extended quotation from a trusted theological friend, personal memoir (preacher's own baptism at age 8), scriptural meditation, and prophetic social awareness; the preacher builds argument primarily through evocative imagery and embodied testimony rather than scholastic argument.

references.name: Gil Scott-Heron
references.context: Preacher cites Scott-Heron as a 'prophetic poet' from the 1960s-70s who coined the phrase 'the revolution will not be televised' while watching political demonstrations on the evening news.

references.name: The Revolution Will Not Be Televised
references.context: The famous phrase by Gil Scott-Heron is used as the theological frame for the entire sermon, connecting political revolution to the kingdom of God.

references.name: Blessed Virgin Mary
references.context: Mary is listed as one of the prophets for whom 'the advent of God...is something we participate in,' and her Magnificat is referenced as placing her hand to her stomach declaring 'we are the ones we have been waiting for.'

references.name: Oscar Romero
references.context: Named as one of the prophets (alongside Blessed Virgin Mary and Prathia Hall) for whom the coming of God is something to participate in rather than observe passively.

references.name: Prathia Hall
references.context: Named as one of the prophets for whom the advent of God is participatory rather than spectatorial.

references.name: Reverend Verzola Law
references.context: Cited as 'a pastor and friend' known for saying 'dysfunctional family is redundant' to explain how Jesus's family functioned through triangulation and indirect communication.

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: John is presented as expecting Jesus to usher in 'a new era of justice for their people' and 'knock the oppressive Romans off their backs,' but struggles with doubt about whether Jesus is 'the one we have been waiting for.'

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Jesus responds to John's question by reversing it and asking 'are you the ones we have been waiting for?' while describing signs of transformation: the blind see, crippled walk, lepers healed, dead raised, poor have what they need.

references.name: Elizabeth
references.context: Referenced as realizing 'we are the ones we have been waiting for' in her confusion and excitement, presumably about the incarnation.

references.name: June Jordan
references.context: American poet who 22 years after the 1956 South African women's march recited a poem commemorating that march with the refrain 'we are the ones we have been waiting for.'

references.name: June Jordan's poem (commemorating the 1956 women's march)
references.context: Quoted: 'And who will join this standing up? And the ones who stood without sweet company will sing and sing back into the mountains and if necessary, even under the sea, we are the ones we have been waiting for.'

references.name: Mary's Magnificat
references.context: Referenced as the theological statement of resistance where 'oppressive power structures are resisted and reversed,' sung by the choir and invoked as Mary saying 'we are the ones we have been waiting for.'

references.name: Luke 1:46-55 (Mary's Magnificat)
references.context: The Magnificat is cited as an example of prophetic witness through which oppressive power structures are resisted and reversed.

references.name: Matthew 11:2-6 / Luke 7:18-23 (John's question and Jesus's response)
references.context: Core passage: John asks 'Are you the one we have been waiting for or should we wait for another?' and Jesus responds with the signs of the kingdom and reverses the question asking 'are you the ones we have been waiting for?'

references.name: Matthew 3:1-17 / Mark 1:1-11 / Luke 3:1-22 (John baptizes Jesus)
references.context: Referenced in the statement 'He has baptized his cousin Jesus and expects him to be the one to usher in a new era of justice for their people.'

references.name: Eucharist/Holy Communion
references.context: Implied through the reference to 'the advent of Jesus, his coming again as holy bread and wine' connecting Christ's presence in sacrament to his identification with the poor and marginalized.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: The entire sermon framework emphasizes God's liberation of the oppressed, resistance to oppressive power structures, and identification with the poor—central themes of liberation theology.

references.name: Contemplative activism
references.context: Described when 20,000-40,000 South African women stood in 'complete silence' for 30 minutes outside the Prime Minister's office before declaring 'You have struck a rock'—combining prayer/silence with political action.

references.name: The 1956 South African women's march and anti-apartheid pass laws
references.context: Historical example of prophetic, participatory resistance to oppressive power structures on August 9, 1956, in Pretoria where women demanded an end to apartheid 'pass laws' prohibiting Black people from designated white areas.

references.name: Triangulation (family systems theory)
references.context: The preacher uses the psychological concept of triangulation to describe how John sends a third party (Patty) to ask another party (Aunt Cheryl) to relay a message to Jesus rather than communicating directly.

references.name: Advent liturgical season
references.context: The sermon is preached on the Third Sunday of Advent and reflects on the meaning of 'advent' (coming) as participatory rather than passive waiting for Christ's coming.

primary_theme: The advent of Jesus and the kingdom of God are not passive spectacles to be observed but revolutionary realities to be participated in through prophetic witness and resistance to oppressive power structures.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling interwoven with prophetic challenge, using contemporary and historical examples (Gil Scott-Heron, 1956 South African women's march, June Jordan) alongside biblical narrative (John the Baptist, Jesus, Mary) to make a case for active, embodied participation in God's liberating work; includes pastoral reflection on family dynamics and personal transformation; employs repetition and call-and-response rhythm typical of African American homiletics.

references.name: Maggie Nelson
references.context: Nelson is cited as defining 'feeling alive' as 'the aliveness of the body tissues and working of body functions, including the heart's action and breathing.'

references.name: The Argonauts
references.context: Maggie Nelson's book is explicitly cited as the source for her definition of aliveness as a bodily, sensory experience.

references.name: Baptism (Holy Baptism)
references.context: The Christian sacrament of baptism is the structural and theological center of the sermon, discussed as a practice of welcome and warning that awakens bodily aliveness.

references.name: Eucharist (Holy Eucharist)
references.context: Holy Eucharist is paired with baptism as a sacrament that brings awareness of bodily aliveness through sensory engagement (bread, wine, palms).

references.name: Trinity
references.context: The preacher references 'celebrating life with the Trinity and the Holy Spirit' as part of baptismal vows.

references.name: Christian Saints / Sainthood
references.context: All Saints' Day tradition is discussed: saints embodied Christ's spirit, were commemorated for how they lived (not died), and were disruptive figures whose godliness became contagious.

references.name: Christ / Jesus Christ
references.context: Jesus Christ is the implicit theological authority and model for saintly living throughout; baptism seals one 'as Christ's own forever' and saints 'embodied the spirit of Jesus Christ.'

references.name: Baptismal Formula
references.context: The trinitarian baptismal formula is quoted: 'I baptize you in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.'

references.name: Matthew 28:19 (implied)
references.context: The trinitarian baptismal formula echoes the Great Commission's baptismal instruction in Matthew 28:19.

references.name: Embodied theology / Incarnational theology
references.context: The sermon emphasizes that 'we not only have bodies, but we are bodies' and that God speaks through physical matter (water, bread, oil, wine), reflecting incarnational theological commitments.

references.name: Liberation theology / Prophetic critique
references.context: The sermon invokes the 'crucified peoples of the world as our teachers' and calls attention to 'refugee crises, increasing wealth inequality, white nationalism and supremacy, sexism, and other social plagues.'

references.name: Interconnectedness / Web of being
references.context: The preacher describes being alive as existing 'in an intricate web of connection with our families, friends, watersheds, communities, cities, nations, the earth, and this universe,' reflecting ecological and relational theological thinking.

references.name: Hagiography / Cult of the Saints
references.context: The sermon references the early church practice of erecting altars at sites of saints and intercession to saints ('We ask the saints to ask God'), reflecting Catholic and Orthodox Christian traditions.

primary_theme: Baptism and the renewal of baptismal vows invite us to awaken to our embodied aliveness and, like the saints, to live disruptively in response to God's invitation to bless and heal a fractured world.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with prophetic challenge; the preacher uses sensory storytelling (cliff-side moments, concerts) to make abstract theology concrete, then pivots to social critique; employs occasional humor and direct address ('You know, when you stand at a cliff...') to create intimacy while maintaining prophetic urgency.

references.name: Rowan Williams
references.context: Former Archbishop of Canterbury cited on baptism and the Trinity: 'The church is the community of those who have been immersed in Jesus' life, overwhelmed by it. In baptisms, the waters close over their heads and are brought into new life by being given a new relationship with God and each other.'

references.name: Kelly Latimer
references.context: Contemporary iconographer who created 'The Icon Before You' (2011) and reflects on breaking traditional iconographic rules to create theological dialogue.

references.name: Mark Bizzuti Jones
references.context: Priest at Trinity Wall Street, New York City, who commissioned Kelly Latimer's iconographic work on the Trinity.

references.name: Andrei Rublev
references.context: Medieval Russian iconographer whose Trinity icon is discussed as either depicting the Trinity or the Oaks of Mamre story from Genesis; Latimer's work is modeled after Rublev's iconic composition.

references.name: Alexander Shmemon
references.context: Russian Orthodox theologian cited at the sermon's conclusion regarding the concept of 'homo adorans' (the praying human, the worshiping human) as humanity's primary vocation.

references.name: Hebrew Bible
references.context: Referenced as demonstrating that 'it is through the senses that the living God comes to us' throughout the Hebrew scriptures.

references.name: Genesis 18:1-15 (Oaks of Mamre)
references.context: The story where Abraham and Sarah are visited by three mysterious people, which early church understood as a foreshadowing of the Trinity.

references.name: The Gospels
references.context: Referenced as containing accounts of people's encounters with Jesus through the senses, demonstrating God comes through physical experience.

references.name: Acts of the Apostles
references.context: Listed alongside Gospels and letters as biblical sources for understanding sensory encounters with the living God.

references.name: Pauline Epistles (general reference to 'letters')
references.context: Referenced as expounding upon encounters with Jesus and affirming God's engagement through the senses.

references.name: The Apostles' Creed
references.context: Discussed as originally an instructional text for those preparing for baptism rather than merely a litmus test for orthodoxy.

references.name: The Nicene Creed
references.context: Referenced alongside the Apostles' Creed as having been used as tests for right belief or orthodoxy.

references.name: Protestant theology
references.context: Critiqued for the idea that 'God is primarily concerned with our brains or our minds,' in contrast to a Catholic understanding that claims the whole being.

references.name: Catholic (small 'c') or Universal Church understanding
references.context: Referenced as the undivided church before the Great Schism of 1054 (East and West together), emphasizing that God claims not just minds but whole beings in baptism and the sacraments.

references.name: The Great Schism of 1054
references.context: Historical reference marking the division between Eastern and Western churches, used to contextualize pre-schism Catholic understanding.

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: Central theological framework emphasizing that matter (water, bread, wine, bodies) is the instrument through which God relates to humanity.

references.name: Trinitarian theology
references.context: Core theological concept that links creeds and baptism; the triune God is both the subject of creeds and the formula invoked in baptism.

references.name: Homo adorans
references.context: Alexander Shmemon's concept of the human being as 'the praying human, the believing human, the worshiping human' as primary human vocation.

primary_theme: Baptism and creeds are united through Trinitarian theology and sacramental practice, emphasizing that God engages the whole person—body, senses, and matter—not merely the intellect, as exemplified in ancient Catholic Christian understanding.
rhetorical_style: Scholarly pastoral reflection combining visual-theological analysis (icons), direct theological citation, historical contextualization, and critique of Protestant intellectualism in favor of embodied sacramental theology.

references.name: Virginia Theological Seminary
references.context: Broderick is identified as a graduate; described as 'the Anglican Communion's largest seminary' with 'profound influence on his mind and on his heart.'

references.name: Book of Common Prayer
references.context: Quoted phrase: 'to read, mark, and inwardly digest the scriptures with reverence, solemnity, and joy.'

references.name: NPR spoken word essays
references.context: Used as analogy for Greer's preaching style: 'someone who's somewhere between an essayist and a poet.'

references.name: Dialogues on Race (2019 book)
references.context: Greer's essay (title approximately 'Christianity in Black and White') combines 'narrative, personal, his own, sociological observations, and theology.'

references.name: New International Version, Children's Edition (Zondervan)
references.context: Greer's childhood Bible, inscribed August 10, 1997, used to frame his formative biblical education.

references.name: Black Baptist tradition
references.context: Greer was 'raised among devout black Baptists who treated the Bible playfully with great enthusiasm,' including Bible contests, vacation Bible school, and daily Bible reading.

references.name: Episcopal Church/Anglicanism
references.context: Discussed as a religious tradition with distinctive approaches to biblical reading; contrasted with Baptist and Roman Catholic cultural influences in America.

references.name: Matthew 6:11 / Lord's Prayer
references.context: Implicit reference in opening prayer: 'give us this day our daily bread' theology related to daily scripture engagement ('the expected Bible reading one would do on a daily basis to share life with God').

references.name: Mount Nebo, Gethsemane, Mount Carmel
references.context: Biblical geographical locations that were 'as real to me as a child as places like New York City, Miami, or London.'

primary_theme: Developing conscious, articulate, and contextually-aware approaches to biblical interpretation within the Episcopal/Anglican tradition, recognizing how invisible cultural assumptions shape how contemporary readers encounter scripture.
rhetorical_style: Autobiographical narrative interwoven with scholarly observation; moves from personal testimony (childhood Baptist Bible practice) to analytical framework (context and imagination); uses analogy and comparison (Baptist vs. Episcopal traditions, literary analysis of preaching); pastoral and educational tone aimed at increasing congregational self-awareness about interpretive practices.

references.name: Robinson Meyer
references.context: Staff writer for The Atlantic who wrote about meeting climate activist Greta Thunberg and described her unusual authoritative tone for a child.

references.name: Greta Thunberg
references.context: 16-year-old climate activist whose UN General Assembly speech ('How dare you? You have stolen my dreams and my childhood') is used as a modern prophetic voice.

references.name: Candace Simpson
references.context: Friend of the preacher quoted for the definition that prophets 'are so attuned to the present that they appear to tell the future.'

references.name: Mark Oakley
references.context: Friend of the preacher cited for the definition that 'poetry puts language in a state of emergency' and 'is language that listens.'

references.name: Dr. Nancy Lynn Westfield
references.context: Biblical scholar quoted extensively on the parable of Lazarus and the rich man, arguing the rich man 'still feels entitled to ask for service' even in death and that the story reveals 'distorted values of society' and 'oppressive systems.'

references.name: Book of Amos
references.context: Prophetic book referenced for pronouncing doom on those resting comfortably in urban centers while the outskirts go without.

references.name: Luke 16:19-31 (Lazarus and the Rich Man)
references.context: Extended parable about a wealthy unnamed man and poor Lazarus that disarms and enrages; the core narrative used to frame contemporary social injustice.

references.name: Abraham's Bosom
references.context: Referenced from the Lazarus parable as the destination of Lazarus after death, contrasted with the rich man's torment.

references.name: Hades
references.context: Invoked as the place of the dead where the rich man is tormented and still maintains his sense of privilege over Lazarus.

references.name: Hymnal reference - 'This fragile earth, our island home'
references.context: Phrase appears to reference the hymn 'God of the Spacious Skies' or similar environmental liturgical language; used to identify Earth as the contemporary Lazarus.

references.name: Prophetic tradition
references.context: The sermon invokes the biblical prophetic tradition (Amos, Jesus) to frame Greta Thunberg as a modern prophet whose message disarms and enrages.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: The sermon's analysis of socioeconomic oppression, class systems, and God's solidarity with the poor reflects liberation theological frameworks.

references.name: Poetic theology
references.context: The sermon uses Mark Oakley's understanding of poetry as a vehicle for truth-telling and prophetic witness, emphasizing that poetry requires patient listening rather than quick answers.

primary_theme: Contemporary climate crisis and racial injustice, like the biblical Lazarus, represent a neglected prophetic call that those in power ignore at their peril, requiring prophetic witness and transformative action rather than empty rhetoric.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-prophetic fusion combining pastoral reflection with scholarly analysis; the preacher weaves together contemporary witness (Greta Thunberg), biblical narrative (Lazarus parable), poetic theory, and social criticism to build a prophetic challenge about systemic injustice and the Earth's suffering.

references.name: Mark (friend and Church of England priest)
references.context: Quoted for the distinction between 'here is the news' and 'once upon a time' as different modes of listening; described as having written widely about poetry and the baptized imagination.

references.name: Mark's book on poetry and baptized imagination
references.context: Introduction quoted describing poetry as a 'splash of words' where meaning is found 'not in the words but in us. In our response to the words.'

references.name: Jesus
references.context: Presented as a prolific storyteller and artist who uses both narrative parables and physical demonstrations (such as writing in dust) to create space for interpretation rather than prescribing thought.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: Referenced for the account of Jesus writing mysteriously in dust in the story of the woman caught in adultery (John 7:53-8:11).

references.name: The Parable of Lazarus and the Rich Man
references.context: Core text analyzed throughout sermon (Luke 16:19-31); focuses on naming of Lazarus versus anonymity of rich man, and the rich man's lack of self-awareness and compassion even in Hades.

references.name: Dr. Nancy Lynn Westfield
references.context: Theologian at Drew University's Theological School cited for analysis of the rich man's lack of self-awareness and persistent entitlement to service from lower classes even in Hades.

references.name: Abraham
references.context: Referenced in the Lazarus parable as the figure with whom the rich man converses in the afterlife; represents justice and divine welcome.

references.name: The Four Gospels
references.context: Noted as containing Jesus' parables, stories, prayers, and poems that have kept listeners engaged for millennia.

references.name: Favorite Bible professor
references.context: Quoted as saying 'Enjoy the spray of the text' regarding how to approach scripture reading.

references.name: Trinity (One holy and undivided Trinity)
references.context: Invoked in the opening blessing of the sermon.

references.name: Narrative theology / Story-based interpretation
references.context: Underlying the sermon's emphasis on stories and parables as privileged modes of spiritual communication; Jesus located among 'a story-based people.'

references.name: Poetry and imaginative interpretation
references.context: Explicitly invoked through the concept of 'baptized imagination' and the idea that poetic/narrative openness enables spiritual transformation.

references.name: Justice, compassion, and equity
references.context: Central theological values invoked through critique of the rich man's failure to understand these notions; reflects liberation theology concern for the poor and marginalized.

references.name: The 'dustbins of history'
references.context: Used to describe how the powerless and marginal are typically rendered invisible in cultural narratives, contrasting with Jesus' inversion of this pattern.

primary_theme: Jesus uses narrative and open-ended storytelling to invite imaginative, compassionate listening that disrupts human systems of power and creates space for divine grace to work—particularly illustrated through the parable of Lazarus and the rich man, which inverts cultural expectations by naming the poor and exposing the rich man's incapacity for compassion.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with literary analysis and scholarly theological commentary; uses personal friendship and lived examples (water skiing, patios, bedtime reading) alongside biblical exegesis and citations of contemporary theologians to make accessible a sophisticated argument about narrative, imagination, and spiritual transformation.

references.name: King James Bible / Authorized Version
references.context: The preacher reflects on how the King James Version shaped his childhood understanding of Jesus and scripture, contrasting its poetic language ('Be not forgetful to entertain strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares') with the NRSV translation.

references.name: New Revised Standard Version (NRSV)
references.context: The preacher cites the NRSV's rendering of Hebrews 13:2 ('do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers') as a modern alternative to the King James phrasing.

references.name: Hebrews (Book of)
references.context: The sermon's primary text is Hebrews 13:2 regarding hospitality to strangers; the preacher also references Hebrews 12:1 ('cloud of witnesses') and discusses the author's concept of 'Jesus, the author and finisher of faithfulness.'

references.name: Genesis 18:1-15 (Abraham and Sarah's hospitality to the three visitors at Mamre)
references.context: The preacher uses this narrative as the primary biblical exemplar of entertaining angels unawares, detailing Abraham's hospitable reception versus Sodom and Gomorrah's inhospitality.

references.name: Ezekiel 16:49
references.context: The preacher cites Ezekiel's definition of Sodom's sin: 'She and her daughters had pride, excess of food, and prosperous ease, but did not aid the impoverished and the needy,' establishing that Sodom's sin was inhospitality, not homosexuality.

references.name: Letter of Jude
references.context: The preacher mentions the blessing from Jude recited every Sunday in his childhood church, rendered in King James English.

references.name: Matthew 25 (implied - judgment of nations based on treatment of strangers/prisoners/sick)
references.context: Though not explicitly cited, the preacher's emphasis on Jesus being present 'in prisoners as he is in the bread and wine' aligns with Matthew 25:31-46 theology.

references.name: Luke 1-2 / Matthew 2:13-15 (Jesus as refugee in Egypt)
references.context: The preacher references Jesus, Mary, and Joseph becoming 'a refugee in Egypt due to political unrest in his homeland,' establishing Jesus's identification with the displaced.

references.name: Panashe Chigumatsi
references.context: The preacher cites the Zimbabwean writer and recommends her book 'These Bones Will Rise Again,' quoting her reflection on the question 'Is this a human being?'

references.name: These Bones Will Rise Again
references.context: Panashe Chigumatsi's book is recommended and quoted directly regarding the nature of human personhood and the question of whether someone is 'a human being.'

references.name: Ubuntu / 'Hoon-hoo' (Bantu philosophy)
references.context: The preacher invokes the Bantu philosophical principle 'A person is a person through others,' citing it as foundational to ethical personhood and parental discipline in southern African cultures.

references.name: Palestinian Jewish context of Jesus
references.context: The preacher describes Jesus as 'an uneducated Palestinian Jew living in an obscure corner of the Roman Empire,' invoking scholarly historical-critical understanding of Jesus's social location.

references.name: Biblical scholarship (credible scholars on Sodom and Gomorrah)
references.context: The preacher cites 'credible biblical scholars' who have asserted that the sin of Sodom was inhospitality, not homosexuality, and that the concept of homosexuality as a category 'did not even exist then.'

primary_theme: Hospitality to strangers—rooted in biblical narrative and ubuntu philosophy—is the measure of human ethical personhood and Christian baptized life, since we may entertain angels unawares and Jesus himself knew displacement, imprisonment, and marginalization.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection interwoven with scholarly argument; the preacher uses personal memoir (childhood experiences with King James Bible), biblical narrative exposition (Abraham and Sarah, Sodom and Gomorrah), scholarly correction (refuting homophobic interpretations of Sodom), and philosophical invocation (ubuntu) to build an ethical case for radical hospitality.

references.name: Luke 13:10-13 (Woman with bent back healed on Sabbath)
references.context: The sermon's central biblical narrative: a woman bent over for 18 years is healed by Jesus on the Sabbath despite objections from a synagogue leader.

references.name: Psalm 103:1
references.context: Quoted as words likely on the woman's lips: 'Bless the eternal one, oh my soul and all that is within me. Bless God's holy name.'

references.name: Exodus 15 (Miriam's song after the Red Sea crossing)
references.context: Alluded to as a parallel to the woman's praise: 'a dance and a song not unlike the one Miriam performed just after Israel's release from Egyptian enslavement.'

references.name: Exodus 20:8-11 (Fourth Commandment - Remember the Sabbath)
references.context: Referenced as a gift meant to express love and community care, not as legalistic restriction; discussed in relation to Lent teachings.

references.name: Genesis 2:2-3 (God's rest on the seventh day)
references.context: Invoked to establish the theological foundation of Sabbath rest: 'after six days of cosmic work, God rests on the seventh.'

references.name: Isaiah 58 (Isaiah on the Sabbath and justice)
references.context: Referenced to connect Sabbath observance with justice work: 'hand in glove with observing the Sabbath is offering food to the hungry and satisfying the needs of the afflicted.'

references.name: The Ten Commandments
references.context: Discussed as gifts for communal life rather than restrictions, particularly the Sabbath commandment.

references.name: Episcopal/Anglican liturgical and theological traditions
references.context: Referenced as a tradition that values 'rules, regiments, rituals' that 'compose an element of the beauty of our liturgical and theological traditions.'

references.name: Hebrew Exodus and wilderness narrative (enslavement, wilderness wandering, exile, return)
references.context: Invoked as the woman's ancestral history, establishing God's role as 'companion to her people in enslavement, wilderness wandering, exile, and return.'

references.name: Liberation theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's repeated emphasis on liberation from oppression, sexism, and systemic injustice ('Woman, thou art loosed from the ravages of sexism... from oppression... from the terrorism of white supremacists') reflects liberation theological frameworks.

references.name: Prophetic biblical tradition (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's closing challenge to perceive and respond to systemic injustice echoes the prophetic call for justice-oriented action embedded in Isaiah 58 and broader Hebrew prophetic literature.

primary_theme: Jesus prioritizes human healing and liberation over rigid adherence to religious law and rules, calling the church to perceive those most in need and work toward their freedom from all forms of oppression and constraint.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with prophetic challenge; the preacher uses imaginative engagement with the biblical story to build toward a contemporary prophetic indictment of systemic injustice (sexism, white supremacy, hunger, oppression), framed within pastoral and liturgical language appropriate to Episcopal worship.

references.name: Audre Lorde
