references.context: Preacher cites Lorde's essay 'A Burst of Light' and her journal entry from November 17, 1986, reflecting on living with a second cancer diagnosis and the urgency to alter patterns of isolation.

references.name: A Burst of Light
references.context: Lorde's essay consisting of journal entries written after her second cancer diagnosis, used as a model of attentiveness and prophetic wisdom in the face of mortality.

references.name: Book of Ecclesiastes
references.context: Preacher references the nihilistic reading 'everything is pointless' from Ecclesiastes as an example of failed imagination and despair.

references.name: Isaiah 2:4
references.context: Quoted: 'Then they will beat their swords into iron plows and their spears into pruning tools. Nation will not take up sword against nation,' used to envision a future of disarmament.

references.name: Negro Spiritual - 'Study War No More'
references.context: Preacher cites the spiritual 'I'm going to lay down my burden down by the riverside and study war no more,' tracing its inspiration to Isaiah's vision of peace.

references.name: Prophetic tradition
references.context: Preacher invokes prophetic figures and the prophetic tradition as models for whom should be trusted with the common good, alongside poets, musicians, and comedians.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: Implicit in the preacher's focus on vulnerable populations, structural violence, and the call for justice embedded in prophetic imagination and God's alternative vision.

references.name: Imagination as theological category
references.context: The concept of 'failure of imagination' and living 'in God's imagination' suggests influence from contemporary prophetic theology, particularly Walter Brueggemann's concept of prophetic imagination.

primary_theme: In the face of repeated mass violence, the preacher calls the church to recover prophetic imagination and choose attentiveness to the present moment and human dignity over despair and the instruments of war.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic challenge combined with pastoral reflection; the preacher uses contemporary tragedy (mass shootings), literary testimony (Audre Lorde), biblical vision (Isaiah), and African American spiritual tradition to construct an argument about moral imagination and the sacred duty to resist violence and demand justice.

references.name: Audre Lorde
references.context: Preacher cites Lorde's essay 'A Burst of Light' and her journal entry from November 17, 1986, reflecting on living with a second cancer diagnosis and the urgency to alter patterns of isolation.

references.name: A Burst of Light
references.context: Lorde's series of journal entries written after being diagnosed with cancer a second time, from which the preacher quotes extensively to illustrate attentiveness and wisdom in the face of mortality.

references.name: Ecclesiastes
references.context: The preacher references the sentiment from Ecclesiastes that 'everything is pointless' and connects this to nihilistic despair that can be imposed violently on others.

references.name: Isaiah (prophet's vision)
references.context: Preacher cites Isaiah's vision: 'Then they will beat their swords into iron plows and their spears into pruning tools' and 'They will no longer learn how to make war' as a model for disarmament and peace.

references.name: Isaiah 2:4 (or similar)
references.context: The specific Isaiah passage about converting weapons into farming implements and nations no longer learning war, used as the biblical foundation for the sermon's prophetic vision.

references.name: Negro spiritual tradition
references.context: Preacher invokes the African American spiritual 'I'm Going to Lay Down My Burden Down by the Riverside' and 'Ain't Going to Study War No More' as inspired by Isaiah's vision.

references.name: I'm Going to Lay Down My Burden Down by the Riverside / Ain't Going to Study War No More
references.context: African American spiritual cited as descendant of Isaiah's vision, expressing the commitment to refuse warfare and violence.

references.name: Prophetic tradition (biblical)
references.context: Preacher employs prophetic rhetoric calling for dismantling of weapons industries and imaginative resistance to violence; invokes 'prophetic precision' when discussing Audre Lorde's attentiveness.

references.name: Liberation theology / social justice tradition
references.context: The sermon's focus on systemic injustice, disproportionate harm to vulnerable populations, and the failure of imagination as a root cause of violence reflects liberation theological concerns.

references.name: Failure of imagination
references.context: Preacher names 'violence toward vulnerable populations is almost always a failure of imagination'—a concept echoing James Baldwin and African American prophetic critique of systemic violence.

references.name: God's imagination (eschatological vision)
references.context: Preacher invokes 'demanding a world as it is in God's imagination' rather than accepting the world as it is—reflecting process theology and eschatological hope traditions.

primary_theme: In the face of mass violence and systemic injustice, faith communities must cultivate an imaginative vision—rooted in biblical prophecy and the wisdom of those facing mortality—that values human life over profits and transforms instruments of war into tools of cultivation and peace.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic challenge interwoven with pastoral reflection; the preacher combines scholarly citation (Audre Lorde), biblical exegesis (Isaiah), testimonial narrative (mass shootings, the mother at Walmart), and lyrical invocation (Negro spirituals) to build an urgent moral argument for disarmament and cultural transformation. The style moves between concrete contemporary violence, philosophical meditation on mortality, and visionary hope.

references.name: Candace Simpson
references.context: Friend who articulates that prophets are sensitive to present truth rather than predicting the future: 'Prophets are so sensitive to the world that it appears that they're telling the future. What they're really doing is telling the truth about the present that no one wants to hear.'

references.name: Book of Amos
references.context: Amos 8:1-3 - The vision of the basket of summer fruit and warnings of the end, famine, and exploitation of the poor.

references.name: Hebrew language/Biblical languages
references.context: Preacher explains that in Hebrew, 'basket of summer fruit' is a pun on the word 'end,' revealing linguistic depth lost in English translation.

references.name: Walter Brueggemann
references.context: Hebrew Bible scholar cited for analysis of 'the sociopolitical location of prophets in the Hebrew Bible' and the understanding that prophets were often groups rather than individuals.

references.name: Greek etymology (apocalypse)
references.context: Preacher explains that 'apocalypse' comes from Greek meaning 'unveiling'—tearing away the veil to see things as they actually are, reframing the term beyond its scary contemporary usage.

references.name: Hebrew Bible prophetic tradition
references.context: Preacher describes prophets as agrarian musicians and poets who traveled singing warnings, functioning like contemporary comedians with searing social commentary.

references.name: New Testament prophetic tradition
references.context: Preacher notes that prophets in both Hebrew Bible and New Testament focused on disdain for 'exploitative economic and political arrangements.'

references.name: Gospel of Luke (Martha and Mary)
references.context: Luke 10:38-42 - Referenced through discussion of Mary sitting at Jesus' feet while Martha serves, used to illustrate the need for both contemplative and active roles in community.

references.name: Episcopal Eucharistic prayer tradition
references.context: Quoted phrase 'This fragile earth, our island home' from Episcopal liturgy used to emphasize gentleness toward creation.

references.name: Repentance (theological concept)
references.context: Preacher defines repentance as 'a reorientation, a changing of direction of one's life' rather than purely penitential understanding.

references.name: Seminary education / Greek study
references.context: Preacher self-deprecatingly mentions not doing well in Greek in seminary but now finding biblical languages more interesting 'on this side of ordination,' establishing scholarly credibility.

references.name: Climate crisis and ecological exploitation
references.context: Preacher connects Amos's apocalyptic hyperbole to contemporary environmental concerns, suggesting prophetic warnings are increasingly literal rather than metaphorical.

references.name: Empathy/emotional practice
references.context: Reference to Richard's earlier point that 'when we stop feeling, when we stop practicing empathy, we are absent the word of the Lord,' invoking a contemplative/emotional theological stance.

primary_theme: Prophets like Amos speak uncomfortable truth about present injustice and ecological exploitation, calling communities to repentance and reorientation through both contemplative listening to Jesus and active pursuit of justice.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic blend combining scholarly analysis (biblical linguistics, theologian citations), personal narrative (seminary anecdotes, friendship with Simpson), prophetic challenge (climate crisis application), and liturgical tradition (Episcopal eucharistic language), with accessible analogies (comedians, cocktail parties) to bridge ancient and contemporary concerns.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: The entire sermon is structured around Luke 10:1-20, the commission of the seventy-two disciples.

references.name: Luke 10:1-2
references.context: Jesus commissions seventy-two and says 'The harvest is bigger than you can imagine, but there are few workers.'

references.name: Luke 10:3-11
references.context: Instructions to the seventy-two: send them out as lambs among wolves, carry no wallet or bag, greet no one, offer peace to houses, heal the sick, announce God's kingdom.

references.name: Luke 10:17-20
references.context: The seventy-two return joyfully reporting that demons submit to them; Jesus tells them to rejoice instead that their names are written in heaven.

references.name: Matthew 10:16
references.context: Implicit parallel to the 'lambs among wolves' metaphor used for disciples.

references.name: The Crucifixion
references.context: Cited as the clearest embodiment of the vulnerability and risk of love at the heart of Christian faith.

references.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
references.context: King's phrase 'the beloved community' is invoked as an alternative name for God's kingdom/reign.

references.name: Kingdom of God language (Gospel tradition)
references.context: Preacher analyzes how 'Kingdom of God' functions as euphemism for two things: how the world looks when God is in charge, and what it means to be wrapped in God's very self.

references.name: Medieval and Early Modern Christian Imperialism
references.context: Preacher critiques how kingdom language justified 'plunder and forced baptisms of the Middle Ages and the imperialism of more recent centuries.'

references.name: Liberation theology / Postcolonial critique of hierarchy
references.context: The neologism 'kin-dom' (replacing 'kingdom') is presented as a way to remove hierarchical language and emphasize kinship and family structures.

references.name: Denominational conflict over human sexuality
references.context: Referenced as a contemporary example of realignment 'just as happened in our denomination 15 years ago, and is happening across denominations across Christianity.'

references.name: United Methodist Church / Episcopal Church
references.context: Concrete example of Holy Family community in Houston shifting denominations due to doctrinal disputes.

references.name: Risk of love / Vulnerability of love
references.context: Core theological claim: 'At the heart of the Christian religion is the risk of love, the vulnerability of love, of exposing ourselves to be loved and to love others and have that love rejected.'

references.name: Sacramental practice (Baptism, Anointing, Eucharist)
references.context: Preacher describes God's reign as embodied in 'practices that draw us to tables, bring us to river and ocean shores, seeking like an infant to be bathed in water, to have our foreheads anointed with oil.'

references.name: Hospitality ethic (biblical/Christian tradition)
references.context: The logic of welcoming guests, sharing meals, and the mutuality of blessing/rejection is central to how the preacher interprets God's vulnerable reign.

references.name: Christian mysticism / Apophatic theology
references.context: Language of mystery and elusiveness: 'The mystery shrouding it makes it just elusive enough, just slippery enough to slide through our fingers to miss it.'

primary_theme: God's reign is not coercive power or hierarchical dominion but vulnerable, mutual love embodied in practices of hospitality, healing, shared meals, and kinship—contrasting sharply with how Christendom has historically misused kingdom language to justify imperialism and domination.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic reflection combining theological critique, scriptural exegesis, sacramental imagery, and personal testimony; the preacher uses historical analysis to challenge the congregation's understanding of divine power while inviting them into embodied, vulnerable practices of love.

references.name: Mark 5:1-20 (Demon-Possessed Man in Gerasenes)
references.context: The primary biblical text for the sermon, recounting Jesus casting out Legion from a demon-possessed man and sending the demons into a herd of pigs.

references.name: Isaiah (Pork Prohibition)
references.context: Referenced as the Isaiah lesson read earlier in the service, which emphasizes the Mosaic law forbidding consumption of pork.

references.name: Pentecost
references.context: Referenced in the sermon title 'The Second Sunday after Pentecost,' situating the sermon within the liturgical calendar after Acts 2.

references.name: Baptism / Baptismal theology
references.context: The preacher discusses 'the dignity bestowed upon us in the waters of baptism' and notes that the Paschal mystery defines 'the baptized life.'

references.name: Paschal Mystery
references.context: Explicitly named as what 'theologians call the Paschal mystery'—the life, death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus as the transformative center of Christian faith.

references.name: Law of Moses / Mosaic Covenant
references.context: Referenced when discussing how the law 'emphatically forbids God's covenant people from consuming pork' and how Roman occupation disrupts Jewish identity.

references.name: Liberation theology (implied)
references.context: The sermon's extended analysis of Roman occupation as a framework for understanding demonic possession reflects liberation theological hermeneutics of reading scripture through the lens of oppression and resistance.

references.name: Postcolonial/Subaltern Studies (implied)
references.context: The discussion of 'coded language' used by oppressed people and how the narrator employs 'subversive power' in literary form reflects postcolonial interpretive strategies.

references.name: African American Homiletical Tradition (implied)
references.context: The extended use of the mother's voice as theological authority, the sermonic refrain 'It ain't nothing but the devil,' and the call-and-response structure reflect African American preaching conventions.

references.name: Semiotics / Symbolic Interpretation (implied)
references.context: The analysis of pigs as 'outward and visible signs of inward invisible occupation' employs sacramental/semiotic interpretive methodology.

primary_theme: The demonic possession narrative in Mark 5 functions as coded resistance literature addressing Roman occupation of Jewish territory, and Jesus's healing of the demon-possessed man offers liberation from imperial forces that render the occupied unrecognizable to themselves—a spiritual reality that echoes in the lived experiences of African American communities.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-autobiographical testimony interwoven with scholarly biblical exegesis; the preacher uses his mother's voice and lived experience to authorize theological interpretation while employing historical-critical and postcolonial hermeneutical analysis of the biblical text, alternating between intimate pastoral reflection and prophetic political analysis.

references.name: Katie Patterson
references.context: Scottish artist who established Future Library in Norway in 2014, a project collecting literary manuscripts from contemporary authors to be read in 2114.

references.name: Han Kong
references.context: South Korean author who contributed her manuscript 'Dear Son, My Beloved' to Future Library, wrapping it in white cloth and dragging it through a Norwegian forest as a ceremonial act.

references.name: Acts of the Apostles
references.context: Biblical passage recounting Paul and Silas encountering an enslaved woman with a spirit of divination whose owners had them jailed when their profit was threatened.

references.name: Acts 16 (implied)
references.context: The specific account of Paul and Silas in Philippi with the enslaved fortune-teller girl and the earthquake in the jail.

references.name: Mark's Gospel - Gerasenes Exorcism
references.context: Jesus performs an exorcism where a demon called 'Legion' (an imperial militaristic term) possesses a herd of pigs, disrupting the local economy.

references.name: Mark 5:11-13 (implied)
references.context: The demon Legion possessed a herd of pigs that ran into the sea, sending the local economy into chaos.

references.name: Pentecost
references.context: Referenced as 'that great feast of the Holy Spirit in which we commemorate the Spirit's outpouring and thousands are baptized into the early Jesus movement.'

references.name: St. Paul
references.context: Presented as a biblical character who exorcised the demon from the enslaved woman and whose capacity to feel annoyed validates the preacher's own emotional range.

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Invoked as the authority through whom Paul casts out demons and as the founder of the Jesus movement.

references.name: The Holy Spirit / Holy Ghost
references.context: Presented as a liberating force that frees individuals and communities from systems of exploitation and dehumanization.

references.name: Episcopalianism
references.context: The preacher notes that Episcopalians are 'not as enthusiastic about demon possession and spirits and angels' as some other religious traditions, suggesting a more rationalist theological approach.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: Implicit in the sermon's focus on economic exploitation, bodily autonomy, and systemic oppression as demonic forces that the Spirit liberates people from.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Implicit in the emphasis on the Spirit's presence in concrete, embodied struggles against exploitation rather than abstract spiritual realms.

references.name: Prophetic preaching tradition
references.context: The sermon's critique of imperial economics, slavery, and systems of dehumanization reflects the prophetic tradition of biblical interpretation.

primary_theme: The Holy Spirit liberates individuals and communities from systems of economic, social, and political exploitation, transforming death-dealing structures just as Christ's exorcisms freed possessed persons to wholeness.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with prophetic critique; the preacher uses the Future Library project as an extended metaphor for hope in the future, contrasts it with the enslavers' short-term economic greed, and draws parallels between demonic possession in scripture and systemic oppression in the present day.

references.name: St. Thomas the Apostle
references.context: Thomas seeks to touch Jesus's wounds post-resurrection; his doubt becomes a model for faithful questioning rather than faithlessness.

references.name: Amy Powell
references.context: Cited as theologian who defines 'Resurrection faith' as having courage to look at our wounds, tell truth, apologize, and express pain.

references.name: Whitney Houston
references.context: Referenced via anecdote about an interview where she responds to accusations by saying 'show me the receipts,' paralleling Thomas's demand for proof.

references.name: John 20 (The Resurrection of Jesus and Thomas's doubt)
references.context: Post-resurrection appearance where Jesus retains crucifixion wounds and invites Thomas to touch them; Jesus does not ask Thomas to stop doubting but to lean into doubt.

references.name: Matthew 28:19-20 (The Great Commission / Baptism)
references.context: Implied in discussion of baptism as initiation into the Jesus movement and placement of baptized people 'against the big questions' of existence.

references.name: Hamlet
references.context: Reference to 'mortal coil' echoes Hamlet's soliloquy ('To be or not to be'), evoking existential questioning about life, death, and human purpose.

references.name: Liberationist theology / Prophetic theology
references.context: Sermon addresses white supremacy, sexism, anti-Semitism, income inequality, and transnational cyber warfare as contexts for Christian discipleship; prophetic reframing of suffering.

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: Emphasized in understanding baptism and Eucharist as means of grace that plunge believers into ultimate questions rather than sheltering them from reality.

references.name: Episcopal theological tradition
references.context: Preacher identifies Thomas as 'the first Episcopalian' because of his doubt; suggests Anglican/Episcopal embrace of questioning faith and intellectual honesty.

primary_theme: Resurrection faith demands that Christians courageously confront wounds—personal, collective, and cosmic—rather than deny them, and that doubt and questioning are essential to authentic discipleship.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral homiletical argument blending prophetic social critique, sacramental theology, narrative anecdote (Whitney Houston), biblical exegesis, and contemporary vernacular language to invite baptismal candidates and congregation into radical honesty about suffering and hope.

references.name: Winnie Varghese
references.context: Priest from New York City who reframes 'take up your cross' for LGBTQ people, arguing that God does not give crosses—the world does.

references.name: Elizabeth Johnson
references.context: Theologian cited for her concept of 'deep incarnation' as the radical divine reach through human flesh into the living web of organic life.

references.name: Niels Gregerson
references.context: Danish theologian cited for the idea that in the incarnate Christ, God enters biological tissue and shares the life conditions of all creation (foxes, sparrows, grass, soil).

references.name: Matthew 16:24 (or parallel)
references.context: The phrase 'Take up your cross and follow Jesus' is referenced as a common Christian teaching that is reinterpreted throughout the sermon.

references.name: Roman crucifixion practices
references.context: Execution by crucifixion is described as a form of Roman imperial execution reserved for non-citizens and those who threatened the social, political, and economic order.

references.name: Luke 23:32-39 (or parallel crucifixion accounts)
references.context: Reference to the two insurrectionists crucified on either side of Jesus, who threatened the political and economic order.

references.name: John 19:12-15 (or parallel trial accounts)
references.context: Allusion to the accusation against Jesus that he was claiming to be emperor, and the crowd's response 'we have no emperor but Caesar.'

references.name: Gospel healing and table fellowship narratives
references.context: References to Jesus's healing of the sick, physical contact with outcasts, and breaking bread with the least respectable as embodiments of God's kingdom.

references.name: Eucharistic prayer tradition (Episcopal Church)
references.context: Quoted phrase that Jesus 'completes the offering of his whole life' on the cross; reflects liturgical theology.

references.name: At the Cross (hymn)
references.context: The hymn 'At the cross, at the cross, where I first saw the light' is quoted from the preacher's childhood at Mount Carmel Missionary Baptist Church.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: Implicit throughout the sermon in its focus on the oppressed, imperial violence, and God's solidarity with those crushed by systemic injustice.

references.name: Incarnational theology / Deep Incarnation
references.context: The overarching intellectual framework that God enters fully into creation and bodily suffering to express solidarity with all existence.

references.name: African American Baptist homiletic tradition
references.context: Reflected in the preacher's personal testimony from Mount Carmel Missionary Baptist Church and the sermonic use of hymns and communal memory.

references.name: Nonviolent resistance tradition
references.context: Jesus's 'nonviolent vision' is explicitly named as a prophetic stance against imperial power that resulted in his crucifixion.

references.name: Matthew 6:26 (or Luke 12:6-7)
references.context: Implicit reference to Jesus teaching about sparrows as part of God's providential care; cited by Gregerson as evidence of God's solidarity with all creatures.

references.name: Feminist theology / Feminine divine imagery
references.context: The preacher refers to God as 'she' when describing God entering the cosmos, reflecting inclusive theological language.

primary_theme: The cross represents not divine punishment but divine solidarity—God's incarnate compassion entering into human suffering and the suffering of all creation to share the fate of the oppressed and vulnerable.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-driven pastoral theology blending personal testimony (childhood church experience, LGBTQ solidarity), scholarly citation of contemporary theologians, prophetic social critique of imperial violence and systemic injustice, and liturgical/hymnic reflection to build toward affirmation of redemptive suffering.

references.name: Exodus 3
references.context: Moses encounters the burning bush in chapter 3 of the Exodus narrative; the foundational passage for the entire sermon.

references.name: Genesis 3
references.context: Referenced when God asks the first earthlings where they are, paralleling God's call to Moses.

references.name: Genesis (creation account)
references.context: Invoked to explain how God forms a 'natural temple' through creation acts, and to reference Moses being made from 'red dirt' and 'stardust and atoms.'

references.name: Alexander Shmemann
references.context: Russian Orthodox theologian quoted on the concept of 'Homo Adorans' — the praying human, the priestly human, the human who worships.

references.name: Homo Adorans
references.context: Shmemann's key theological concept defining humanity's original identity as those with capacity to bless God and stand as priests at the center of the world.

references.name: Jack Miles
references.context: Author of 'God, a Biography,' cited to interpret the divine name 'I AM' as 'I am what I shall be' — an invitation to discover God's identity over time.

references.name: God, a Biography
references.context: Miles's work is cited for its interpretation of the meaning of God's self-identification at the burning bush.

references.name: T.S. Eliot
references.context: Poet cited for a playful and profound poem about a cat in meditation, used as an analogy for God's ineffable, mysterious nature.

references.name: T.S. Eliot's poem on the cat's name
references.context: Eliot's poem describing a cat contemplating 'the thought of the thought of his name' and his 'ineffable effable...deep and inscrutable singular name.'

references.name: Mysticism
references.context: Prayer is described as 'according to some mystics, the practice of paying attention,' grounding the sermon in contemplative spiritual tradition.

references.name: Russian Orthodox theology
references.context: Shmemann's theological framework is drawn from Orthodox Christian thought, particularly its sacramental and priestly understanding of humanity.

references.name: Systematic theology
references.context: Contrasted with experiential knowledge of God: God reveals himself 'as a collage rather than four volumes of systematic theology, as flashes rather than statements.'

references.name: The Ground of Being
references.context: Referenced when the sermon speaks of 'comprehend[ing] the ground of all being,' a phrase associated with Paul Tillich's theological vocabulary.

references.name: Liberation theology / Prophetic theology
references.context: Implicit in the sermon's emphasis on God's concern for the enslaved, oppressed, and marginalized ('the outcast and the downcast'), and divine action against imperial overreach.

references.name: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
references.context: Referenced multiple times as the patriarchal lineage through which God identifies himself, connecting personal identity to ancestral history.

references.name: Exodus narrative (slavery and liberation)
references.context: God's knowledge of and commitment to deliver Israel from Egyptian bondage and imperial domination.

references.name: Christian liturgy and worship
references.context: The sermon argues that Christian liturgy at its best uses sensory experience to remind us of our identity as Homo Adorans, those called to bless and worship.

primary_theme: Prayer and worship as acts of sacred attention that reveal human identity as Homo Adorans (the priestly, blessing creature), grounded in the encounter between Moses and the burning bush as a call to perceive the holy in the ordinary and stand in solidarity with the oppressed.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling blended with scholarly theological reflection, using personal memoir (the Texas running story) to illuminate biblical themes, supported by literary and theological authorities; moves between exegetical observation, mystical contemplation, and prophetic social consciousness.

references.name: Reverend Dr. Blair Money
references.context: Money is quoted as the intellectual authority for the sermon's central interpretive claim: 'Jesus' God was healing all the wrong people.'

references.name: 1 Kings 17
references.context: The story of the widow of Zarephath and the prophet Elijah is the first biblical narrative used to illustrate God's boundary-crossing healing.

references.name: 2 Kings 5
references.context: The story of Naaman the Syrian and the prophet Elisha is the second biblical narrative used to demonstrate God healing an enemy outsider.

references.name: Luke 4 (Gospel reading for Fourth Sunday after Epiphany)
references.context: The unnamed gospel reading about Jesus in his hometown that sparks congregational rage; references Jesus withholding healing from his own people while healing others.

references.name: Epiphany season
references.context: The liturgical season after Epiphany emphasizes God's revelation to all flesh, nations, and peoples, establishing the theological framework for the sermon.

references.name: Dr. King
references.context: King's concept of 'the fierce urgency of now' is invoked to emphasize the immediate demand of Christian witness and testimony.

references.name: Eucharist/Sacrament of Communion
references.context: The sacramental tradition of bread and wine is used as a concrete example of healing and restoration that churches risk hoarding rather than sharing.

references.name: Liberation theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's emphasis on God's preferential concern for the marginalized (widows, outsiders, enemies, the impoverished) reflects liberation theological frameworks.

references.name: Prophetic tradition
references.context: The figures of Elijah and Elisha are invoked as prophetic models who cross social and ethnic boundaries in obedience to God.

primary_theme: God's radical inclusion and boundary-crossing love challenges religious communities to examine how they have hoarded divine grace rather than witnessing to God's healing power available to all people, especially outsiders and enemies.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral prophetic challenge; the preacher uses biblical narratives as interpretive lenses to interrogate contemporary Christian practice, punctuated by emphatic refrains ('Jesus' God was healing all the wrong people') and rhetorical questions directed at the congregation's conscience.

references.name: Trinity
references.context: Invoked in the opening blessing: 'In the name of the one holy and undivided Trinity'

references.name: Hebrews 10:10
references.context: Quoted directly: 'It is by God's will that we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all'

references.name: Psalms (unspecified)
references.context: The Hebrews writer quotes from Psalms: 'sacrifices and offerings you have not desired, but a body you have prepared for me'

references.name: Luke 1:46-55 (Magnificat)
references.context: Mary's song quoted: 'My soul proclaims the greatness of the Lord, who has scattered the proud in their conceit, who has cast down the mighty from their thrones, who has lifted up the lowly and filled the hungry and sent the rich away empty-handed'

references.name: Luke 1:39-45 (Mary visits Elizabeth)
references.context: Referenced as Mary performing her revolutionary song first for Elizabeth, her cousin, and the unborn John the Baptist leaping in response

references.name: Acts 1:14
references.context: Allusion to 'the 11 remaining apostles, some women, the text says, and Mary, mother of Jesus, waiting in an upper room'

references.name: 17th century theologian (unnamed)
references.context: An unnamed 17th century theologian is cited regarding Mary's priesthood: 'her greeting of Elizabeth imposed her power on Elizabeth's baby, further sanctifying John'

references.name: Bishop Michael Curry
references.context: Referenced as the presiding Bishop who calls Jesus's followers 'the Jesus movement': 'that fledgling early band of followers who caught the vision of all that Jesus did, taught, and embodied'

references.name: Black Church tradition / Black Church theology
references.context: Invoked twice: first regarding 'sanctified imagination' practiced in the black church, and later to explain how this tradition fills in narrative blanks with 'flourishes and vibrant hues and robust tones'

references.name: Sanctified imagination
references.context: A concept from black church theology: 'the practice of filling in the blanks of hearing a story on its surface and adding the flourishes and vibrant hues and robust tones'

references.name: Womanist theology / Mary as priest
references.context: The sermon develops a theology of Mary as the 'very first priest' to elevate Christ's body and as an embodiment of sanctified imagination and prophetic resistance

references.name: Liberation theology (implicit)
references.context: Embedded in the analysis of Mary's revolutionary song and her imagination of 'a world turned not upside down but right side up' in response to imperial occupation and violence

primary_theme: Mary as the first priest and embodiment of sanctified imagination—a prophetic, revolutionary figure whose defiant hope in the face of imperial violence and whose practice of filling biblical narrative with vibrant human detail offer a model for Christian resistance and joy today.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-pastoral with prophetic elements; the preacher uses close reading of visual art (the chapel sculpture), scriptural exegesis through a musical metaphor, and theological reflection grounded in black church tradition to reframe Mary as a priest and revolutionary figure whose imagination Christians should emulate.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Opening citation: 'So with many other exhortations, John proclaimed the good news to the people' (Luke 3:18).

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: Primary biblical figure analyzed throughout sermon; characterized as prophetic voice calling for repentance and economic justice.

references.name: Matthew 3:7-10 / Luke 3:7-9
references.context: Reference to John's harsh language: 'brood of vipers and axe laying at the root of the tree' as non-endearing idioms.

references.name: Book of Zephaniah
references.context: Cited for the promise of transformation: 'I will change the shame of the outcasts into praise' (Zephaniah 3:19-20, implied).

references.name: Luke 3:10-14
references.context: Jesus's teaching through John's voice: 'Whoever has two coats must share with anyone who has none...Do not extort money from anyone by threats or false accusation.'

references.name: Willie James Jennings
references.context: Quoted twice as scholarly authority on John the Baptist's gospel message and economic justice demands on soldiers and tax collectors.

references.name: Roman imperial theology
references.context: Invoked through inscription on Augustus statue: 'Providence has given us Augustus...savior...the gospel, the good news for the world' as contrast to Christian gospel.

references.name: Augustus / Caesar
references.context: Historical figure representing imperial power and competing 'gospel' that serves the powerful rather than the marginalized.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: Entire sermon implicitly draws on liberation theology framework: emphasis on God's preferential option for the poor, economic justice, and transformation of oppressive systems.

references.name: Prophetic tradition
references.context: John the Baptist positioned within prophetic lineage: 'Like prophets before him and since, John was not polite company.'

references.name: Baptism
references.context: Central theological practice representing radical reorientation and physical commitment to repentance and new life.

references.name: Eucharist / Holy Communion
references.context: Reference to 'God's holy table' as ongoing practice embodying the gospel of sharing and care.

references.name: Walt Disney
references.context: Used as cultural reference point: John's personality is 'the stuff of fringe indie movies, not Walt Disney lore'—contrast between prophetic realism and sanitized commercial culture.

primary_theme: John the Baptist's proclamation of repentance and economic justice as 'good news' to the poor stands in radical contrast to imperial propaganda, demanding a complete reorientation of society toward equity and dignity.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic-scholarly synthesis combining pastoral intimacy ('I know people like John the Baptist'), scriptural close reading, historical-political analysis (Roman empire), explicit scholarly citation (Jennings), and liturgical-ecclesial vision (baptism, communion, community transformation).

references.name: Theory of Evolution
references.context: Referenced as a catalyst for the emergence of various Christian scriptural interpretation styles over the last 100 years in response to modernity.

references.name: Literary Theory / Plain Reading
references.context: Preacher critiques the 'plain reading' approach that literary theorists classify as reading a text without context to determine meaning.

references.name: Anglican Tradition / Anglicanism
references.context: Identified as the tradition that rejected plain reading in favor of viewing scripture as complex literary forms.

references.name: Episcopal Church Biblical Hermeneutics
references.context: Described as viewing scripture as a complex array of literary groups (wisdom verses, poetry, mythobiography, epistolary).

references.name: Society of St. John the Evangelist
references.context: An Episcopal monastic community in suburban Boston quoted on the Anglican tradition of understanding authority as graceful synergy between scripture, tradition, reason, and experience.

references.name: Scripture, Tradition, Reason, and Experience (Quadrilateral)
references.context: Invoked as the Episcopal approach to biblical authority—a graceful synergy combining multiple sources of wisdom in interpretation.

references.name: Apocalyptic Literature (Biblical Genre)
references.context: Identified as a biblical literary genre more concerned with casting present realities in cosmic terms than fortune-telling.

references.name: Daniel
references.context: Cited alongside Mark as apocalyptic readings assigned for the day; referenced as examples of enigmatic and haunting texts requiring sophisticated interpretation.

references.name: Book of Mark
references.context: Apocalyptic gospel passage read on this liturgical day; alluded to for its warnings about false messiahs ('I am he').

references.name: Romans 13
references.context: Cited as a passage misused by those who justify inhumanity at the nation's border without proper contextual interpretation.

references.name: Romans 1
references.context: Referenced as a passage misappropriated to impose anti-LGBTQ laws through misinterpretation.

references.name: Genesis 19
references.context: Mentioned as a passage misused to justify anti-LGBTQ laws without proper contextual understanding.

references.name: 1 Timothy 2
references.context: Cited as a passage historically misused to justify the subjugation of women in church and society.

references.name: 1 Corinthians 14
references.context: Referenced as a passage misappropriated to justify the subjugation of women.

references.name: Gospel Accounts of the Trial and Crucifixion of Jesus
references.context: Identified as texts that have been weaponized to justify anti-Semitic violence, referencing the Pittsburgh synagogue shooting.

references.name: "A text without a context is a pretext for a subtext"
references.context: Proverbial statement cited as commonly heard in church and used from the pulpit to emphasize that meaning requires contextual understanding.

references.name: Tradition as 'the vote of those who have died'
references.context: A quotation (possibly from Burke or Chesterton) used to describe the importance of listening to voices from the past in biblical interpretation.

references.name: Liturgical Reading Formula: Read, Mark, Learn, and Inwardly Digest
references.context: Referenced from the Episcopal liturgy as the standard for scriptural engagement, later expanded to mean deep, contextual, compassionate interpretation.

primary_theme: Biblical interpretation in the Episcopal tradition requires a collaborative, contextual, and multifaceted approach drawing on scripture, tradition, reason, and experience—resisting simplistic or dangerous misreadings that weaponize sacred texts.
rhetorical_style: Scholarly pastoral argument combined with prophetic challenge: the preacher uses historical and theological education to defend sophisticated hermeneutics while calling out contemporary misuses of scripture in social harm (anti-LGBTQ laws, border violence, anti-Semitism).

references.name: Dr. Wil Gaffney
references.context: Cited as author of 'Womanist Midrash' to argue that gender distinction first occurs in Genesis 2, not Genesis 1, and that the Hebrew 'Adam' is more expansive than traditionally translated.

references.name: Womanist Midrash
references.context: Gaffney's work is quoted to reinterpret the Genesis creation account and challenge traditional gender binary readings of scripture.

references.name: New Testament scholar (referenced but not named, likely Pheme Perkins)
references.context: Cited to argue that Jesus' divorce teaching in Mark is an indictment of Herod and not a prohibition against divorced women escaping abuse.

references.name: Pheme Perkins (implied as 'famed Perkins')
references.context: Referenced to establish that children in first-century context were regarded as 'non-persons' and property, and that homeless and indigenous peoples are modern examples of such social categories.

references.name: Genesis 1-3
references.context: The creation narrative is examined for how it has been misused as a pretext for heteronormative and patriarchal doctrine, with focus on the meaning of 'Adam' and gender differentiation.

references.name: Genesis 2
references.context: Referenced as the chapter where gender distinction first occurs in scripture, according to Gaffney's womanist midrash interpretation.

references.name: Genesis 3
references.context: Discussed in relation to the nuances of translating biblical Hebrew and the multiplicity of meanings for 'Adam' across different chapters.

references.name: Mark's Gospel (divorce passage)
references.context: Examined as a text that quotes Genesis and is often misused to condemn divorce, but actually critiques Herod and addresses hardness of heart.

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: Referenced as a biblical figure whose criticism of Herod resulted in his beheading, paralleling Jesus' risk in making similar indictments.

references.name: Womanist theology/hermeneutics
references.context: Gaffney's 'Womanist Midrash' represents a womanist theological approach to biblical interpretation that centers women's and marginalized people's experiences.

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: The sermon implicitly employs liberation theological frameworks by emphasizing God's preferential option for the marginalized, non-persons, and those at society's edges.

references.name: Midrash
references.context: Invoked as a Jewish hermeneutical method that allows for expansive, imaginative interpretation of scripture beyond literal readings.

references.name: Baptist homiletic tradition
references.context: The preacher references the Baptist practice of calling for 'amens' from the congregation, noting their church practices differently.

references.name: Baptismal theology
references.context: Referenced as the source of Christian identity and incorporation into the 'new Adam' and new creation, linking sacramental identity to ethical transformation.

references.name: Christological reinterpretation of Adam
references.context: The sermon presents Jesus as the 'new Adam' and 'new earthling' embodying a new humanity that transcends domination and establishes mutuality and love.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The sermon implies that God's nature transcends masculine singular categorization and that humans made in God's image reflect divine multiplicity.

references.name: Feminist biblical hermeneutics
references.context: Employed throughout to expose how biblical texts have been used as pretexts for cruelty against women and LGBTQ people.

references.name: Queer theology
references.context: Implicitly invoked when discussing transgender, gender non-binary, genderqueer, and gender non-conforming identities and their theological significance.

primary_theme: Biblical texts must be read with historical and linguistic context to recover liberative readings that challenge patriarchy and heteronormativity, and to reveal Jesus' preferential option for the marginalized, non-persons, and those at society's edges.
rhetorical_style: Scholarly argument combined with prophetic challenge, employing close textual analysis, appeals to contemporary biblical scholars, linguistic excavation, and contemporary social witness to transform how listeners understand familiar biblical passages and their ethical implications.

references.name: Scholars of comedy
references.context: Opening analogy that good comedians are 'wizards of timing' rather than masters of humor, establishing timing as a key intellectual framework for the sermon.

references.name: Dr. Fame Perkins
references.context: Scholar of early Christianity cited to argue that conflict and secrecy in Mark's Gospel relates to the timing of announcing Jesus as Messiah.

references.name: Mark (Gospel of Mark)
references.context: Primary biblical text for the sermon; Mark 8:27-31 passage about Peter's confession and Jesus's passion prediction.

references.name: Mark 1
references.context: Referenced as the wilderness temptation account with Satan's 40-day trial, paralleled to Peter's temptation of Jesus.

references.name: Dorothy Sowell
references.context: Theologian cited as coining the term 'Christofascist' in her book *The Window of Vulnerability* to describe desire for a God without justice and a Jesus without a cross.

references.name: The Window of Vulnerability
references.context: Theologian Dorothy Sowell's work where she defines 'Christofascist' as those wanting a sanitized God divorced from justice and suffering.

references.name: Satan/Devil
references.context: The tempter in Mark 1:12-13 who offers Jesus shortcuts to power; later echoed in Peter's rebuke of Jesus's passion prediction.

references.name: Peter/Simon Peter
references.context: Disciple in Mark 8:29-33 who correctly identifies Jesus as Messiah but rebukes Jesus for predicting suffering; later rebuked by Jesus as 'Satan.'

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: Implied in Sowell's coinage of 'Christofascist' and critique of a God without justice; emphasizes God's solidarity with the suffering.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Central to the sermon's argument that God in Christ suffers with humanity, making God credible in human experience.

references.name: Jazz vs. classical music
references.context: Metaphor comparing Jesus as a 'jazz musician well-experienced in syncopation' while disciples are 'classically trained,' illustrating the gap between divine and human timing.

references.name: The DMV
references.context: Contemporary reference used to illustrate God's solidarity with mundane human suffering and frustration in the incarnation.

references.name: Advent (December)
references.context: Liturgical calendar reference noting when Mark 1 wilderness temptation was previously preached on.

references.name: The shape of the cross/cruciform love
references.context: Central theological claim that God's love is expressed in the shape of a cross, encompassing suffering and the full spectrum of human experience.

references.name: Mark 8:27-31
references.context: Direct reading of Peter's confession ('You are the Messiah') and Jesus's first passion prediction, establishing the core text.

references.name: Mark 8:32-33
references.context: Jesus's rebuke of Peter: 'Get behind me, Satan,' framed as addressing those with 'foot-in-mouth syndrome.'

references.name: Christian history
references.context: Reference to 'many throughout Christian history' who, like Peter, preferred a Jesus without suffering, without humanity.

references.name: Common English Bible translation
references.context: Preacher notes the CEB translates 'Son of Man' as 'the human one,' highlighting a theological/linguistic choice about Jesus's humanity.

references.name: Sunday school theology
references.context: Gentle critique of rote doctrinal correctness ('like every good Sunday school student should') that misses deeper implications.

primary_theme: God's redemptive love is expressed through identification with human suffering rather than circumventing it, requiring perfect divine timing that human disciples repeatedly fail to recognize, making incarnational solidarity—not power—the shape of God's credibility with humanity.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic blend combining scholarly citation, accessible metaphor (jazz vs. classical music, the DMV), biblical exegesis, and prophetic critique of 'Christofascism' to argue for a costly, incarnational theology of divine solidarity with suffering.

references.name: Krista Tippett
references.context: Interview host whom the preacher listens to annually during Lent.

references.name: Viggen Groyan (also transcribed as Garoyan)
references.context: Arminian theologian whose reflections on the physicality of Christian faith and the role of senses in church architecture and devotion are extensively quoted and form a central intellectual foundation for the sermon.

references.name: Armenian genocide
references.context: Historical reference mentioned in connection with Groyan's biographical context and reflections.

references.name: Scholars of theater
references.context: Invoked for the concept of 'the fourth wall' - the space between performer and audience that sacramental practice breaks.

references.name: The fourth wall
references.context: Theater critical concept applied to how sacraments and biblical narratives engage the full sensory experience of congregants, breaking the boundary between observer and participant.

references.name: Etymologists
references.context: Scholars cited for pointing out that 'transfigured' derives from the root of 'metamorphosis,' establishing the linguistic and conceptual connection.

references.name: Alice Walker
references.context: Author of The Color Purple, whose dialogue between Celie and Shug about God's desire to be perceived in the world's beauty is quoted at length to articulate sensory perception of the divine.

references.name: The Color Purple
references.context: Novel by Alice Walker featuring characters Celie and Shug discussing God's pleasure in creation and the importance of noticing beauty; the 'color purple' becomes a metaphor for divine presence.

references.name: Jeff Lee, Bishop of Chicago
references.context: Bishop whose sermon formulation 'baptism makes real not true' is quoted extensively and serves as a key theological framework distinguishing between God's pre-existing love and its sacramental dramatization.

references.name: Genesis 1 (Spirit of God hovering over the deep)
references.context: Alluded to as one of the waters into which baptism invites us to enter, connecting baptism to creation itself.

references.name: Exodus 14 (Red Sea parting)
references.context: Referenced as the Red Sea's parted waters through which baptism invites us, symbolizing liberation from brutality.

references.name: Matthew 3 / Mark 1 / Luke 3 (Baptism of Jesus in the Jordan)
references.context: Alluded to as the Jordan River into which baptism invites us to enter, connecting individual baptism to Christ's baptism.

references.name: Matthew 17:1-8 / Mark 9:2-8 / Luke 9:28-36 (Transfiguration)
references.context: The central biblical narrative of the sermon, exploring how Jesus's transfiguration revealed who he had always been rather than transforming him into something new.

references.name: Sacramental theology / Sacraments (Baptism, Eucharist/Communion)
references.context: The intellectual and liturgical framework throughout the sermon, emphasizing how sacraments engage all senses and make divine truth experientially real.

references.name: Sensory theology / Theology of embodiment
references.context: The underlying intellectual tradition emphasizing the honing of senses, the body's involvement in worship, and the holiness of physical perception—drawn from Groyan and Walker but representing a broader theological movement.

references.name: Liturgical theology
references.context: The practice of engaging vestments, incense, icons, kneeling, and the sign of the cross as theologically meaningful actions that deepen humanity and perception.

primary_theme: Christian faith is fundamentally embodied and sensory; sacraments and liturgical practices engage all the senses to make real—not merely assert as true—God's infinite love and presence, allowing believers to perceive what was always there, much as the Transfiguration revealed Jesus's eternal identity.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with scholarly support; the preacher combines personal testimony (annual listening to Groyan), extended literary quotation (The Color Purple), theological analysis (distinction between 'makes real' vs. 'makes true'), and liturgical grounding, building an argument through accumulation of complementary sources rather than systematic argumentation.

references.name: Krista Tippett
references.context: Host of interview series that the preacher listens to annually during Lent.

references.name: Viggen Groyan (also spelled Garoyan)
references.context: Arminian theologian whose reflections on the physicality of Christian faith and the role of senses in church architecture and devotion are extensively quoted and serve as intellectual foundation for the sermon's argument.

references.name: Armenian genocide
references.context: Referenced in context of Groyan's biographical experience and reflections.

references.name: Theater scholars / Fourth wall concept
references.context: The preacher cites 'scholars of theater' regarding the fourth wall (space between performer and audience) as an analogy for how sacramental practice breaks the boundary between biblical narrative and lived experience.

references.name: Etymologists
references.context: Referenced for analysis of the Greek root word for 'transfigured' relating to English word 'metamorphosis'.

references.name: Alice Walker
references.context: Author cited for her theological reflection on God's desire to be perceived in creation's beauty through the character dialogue in The Color Purple.

references.name: The Color Purple
references.context: Novel containing dialogue between Celie and Shug about God's pleasure in being noticed in beauty; used as parallel theological statement to Christian sensory perception.

references.name: Jeff Lee
references.context: Bishop of Chicago whose sermon distinction between baptism making something 'real not true' is quoted at length as key theological principle.

references.name: Red Sea
references.context: Alluded to in baptismal typology as symbol of liberation from brutality.

references.name: Jordan River / Jesus's baptism
references.context: Referenced as part of baptismal narrative typology that baptism invokes.

references.name: Genesis 1:2
references.context: Referenced through allusion to 'the spirit of God hovers over the face of the deep at the beginning of creation' as part of baptismal theological significance.

references.name: The Transfiguration (Matthew 17, Mark 9, Luke 9)
references.context: Central biblical event used throughout sermon to illustrate how revelation makes real (not true) what was always present; core theological parallel to baptism.

references.name: Sacramental theology / Sacramentalism
references.context: The theological framework underlying the entire sermon's argument that physical/sensory practices make divine presence tangible and real in human experience.

references.name: Sensory theology / Embodied spirituality
references.context: The intellectual tradition emphasizing the role of all five senses in spiritual perception and devotion, central to Groyan's work and the sermon's argument.

references.name: Metamorphosis
references.context: The etymological principle that transfiguration means revealing what was always present, not becoming something new; used to reframe understanding of transformation.

references.name: Christology / Christological theology
references.context: Implicit throughout discussion of Jesus as God's beloved son and the incarnational principle that God becomes experienceable through physical/material reality.

references.name: Communion/Eucharistic theology
references.context: Referenced alongside baptism as another sacrament whose meaning changes with context and life circumstances; part of broader sacramental argument.

primary_theme: Christian faith engages all human senses and reveals God's always-present love through sacramental practices (baptism, incense, icons, physical movement) that make divine reality tangible and real rather than merely abstract.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-scholarly reflection combining personal narrative (annual Lenten listening ritual), direct quotation from theological authorities (Groyan, Bishop Lee), literary analysis (Alice Walker), etymological instruction, and liturgical-sensory description, layered with repeated structural parallels between transfiguration and baptism to build theological argument through accumulation and resonance rather than linear argument.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer
references.context: Preacher directs congregation to page 856 to find the Episcopal catechism and the definition of prayer; the collect is described as an Episcopal liturgical tradition.

references.name: The Collect (Episcopal liturgical practice)
references.context: Acts as 'both a relic of the past, a signpost of what is possible, and a companion for the present Christian life'; gathers congregational prayer and summarizes themes of scripture and the day.

references.name: Episcopal catechism/ancient catechetical practice
references.context: Bishops would travel and baptize people, interrogating them with questions; modern catechism preserves this practice in liturgical form.

references.name: Christian mystical tradition
references.context: Preacher references how 'mystics have described God as close to us as our next breath is,' establishing intimacy between believer and God.

references.name: Rowan Williams
references.context: Quoted as describing God as 'that depth around all things and beyond all things' into which one sinks in prayer; God as mystery that opens futures and offers transformations.

references.name: Acts 17:28 (implied)
references.context: Preacher paraphrases 'this God in whom we live and move and have our being,' a direct reference to Paul's speech at the Areopagus.

references.name: The poet as inhabiting her poem (implicit poetic tradition)
references.context: God fills in gaps between human words 'the way a poet inhabits her poem,' suggesting poetic epistemology and the mystical understanding of divine presence in language.

references.name: Christian theology of unworthiness/grace
references.context: Central theme that unworthiness, shame, and embarrassment need not prevent prayer; grace sustains us regardless of circumstance.

references.name: Christological doctrine (Jesus's worthiness)
references.context: Preacher commends listeners to 'our Lord Jesus Christ, whose worthiness before God sustains us, no matter the circumstance.'

references.name: The ground of being (implicit Tillichian language)
references.context: References to God as foundational 'reservoir of all goodness' and 'fountain of all wisdom' reflect existentialist theological language about God as Being itself.

primary_theme: Prayer is not a complicated practice requiring perfect words or formulas, but rather a simple, generous response to God's initiating love—one that God understands even in our unworthiness, confusion, and silence.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with liturgical authority; combines scholarly reference (catechism, named theologians) with accessible narrative illustration (hospital waiting room example) and gentle prophetic reassurance to remove barriers to prayer.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer
references.context: Preacher directs congregation to page 856 to find the catechism and the definition of prayer; the collect is the liturgical focus of the sermon.

references.name: The Catechism (Episcopal tradition)
references.context: Preacher explains catechism comes from ancient practice when bishops would travel and baptize people, interrogating them with questions.

references.name: Acts 17:28
references.context: Implicit reference in phrase 'God in whom we live and move and have our being' - direct allusion to Paul's speech at the Areopagus.

references.name: Rowan Williams
references.context: Quoted describing God as 'that depth around all things and beyond all things' and discussing how prayer involves sinking into that depth and receiving love and transformation.

references.name: Christian Mysticism
references.context: Preacher references mystics who have described God as close as our next breath; invokes the mystical understanding of intimacy with God.

references.name: The Collect (liturgical form)
references.context: The collect is described as gathering or summarizing what the congregation is feeling, what scripture readings address, and what we might want to feel.

references.name: Matthew 6:8 (implied)
references.context: The collect's acknowledgment that 'God knows what we need before we even ask' alludes to Jesus's teaching about God knowing our needs.

references.name: Poetic imagination
references.context: Preacher describes how God can fill in gaps between our words 'the way a poet inhabits her poem,' invoking poetic sensibility as analogy for divine understanding.

primary_theme: Prayer is not a complicated practice requiring perfect words or formulas, but rather a generous and expansive response to God's initiating love that can occur with or without words, drawing us into the depth of God's presence.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with liturgical grounding; combines theological teaching (via quoted authority), scriptural allusion, imaginative analogy (hospital waiting room, poet), and direct address to congregation's lived experience of shame, doubt, and prayer difficulty.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: The sermon explicitly frames itself within 'the gospel according to Mark' and references the season of studying signs in Mark's gospel.

references.name: Mark 6:14-29 (Herod and John the Baptist narrative)
references.context: The entire sermon is structured around the story of Herod, John the Baptist's execution, and rumors about Jesus being John raised from the dead.

references.name: Matthew 14:1-12 / Mark 6:14-29
references.context: References to healing miracles, woman with hemorrhage, little girl being raised from death, and disciples anointing with oil—framed as part of the broader gospel healing narrative.

references.name: Elijah
references.context: People speculate Jesus was Elijah, the Hebrew prophet who did not die but was taken to heaven in a chariot of fire.

references.name: Pontius Pilate
references.context: Herod's indecision and waffling is compared to Pontius Pilate's hesitation before crucifying Jesus.

references.name: Prophetic tradition (Hebrew Bible)
references.context: John the Baptist is described as doing 'what prophets do'—raising a ruckus in the court of the powerful.

references.name: Liberation theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon emphasizes God's identification with the marginalized, exploited, beheaded, and lynched rather than with the powerful—a core liberation theology claim.

references.name: African American experience of lynching (historical/theological reference)
references.context: Preacher draws explicit parallel between early Christians' precarious existence under Herod and Black Americans' lived reality of lynching in the first half of the 20th century.

references.name: Feminist/womanist theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon alludes to mothers warning daughters about constant threats of violence and assault based on gender identity—a womanist theological concern.

references.name: The Civil Rights era / 'the '60s'
references.context: Referenced as an example of 'people doing the right thing getting cut down' because they were gaining headway.

primary_theme: God identifies with the marginalized and those who challenge unjust power through their very existence, even when it costs them their lives, and this was the precarious reality facing early Christians and continues to be the reality for oppressed communities today.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic pastoral challenge combined with narrative storytelling and contemporary contextualization; the preacher moves between biblical exegesis, personal confession, and historical-social parallels to invite congregational identification with both the ethical perils of power and the spiritual hope of God's side-ness with the dispossessed.

references.name: Rowan Williams
references.context: Williams, described as 'the poet, theologian, and former Archbishop of Canterbury,' is quoted: 'the job of doctrine is to hold us still before Jesus.'

references.name: St. Paul
references.context: Paul is cited as an early Christian theologian whose life 'was derailed by Jesus on a trek to Damascus' and whose articulation of the Trinity 'was trending in that direction' though not yet in its most robust form.

references.name: Daniel Stramara
references.context: Stramara is quoted describing the Trinity's dynamism: 'The three are intimately interconnected...Their relationships are not static, but revolve around one another...a wheeling around, a whirling about, a making dizzy.'

references.name: Book of Isaiah
references.context: Isaiah 6 is extensively quoted and discussed, recounting Isaiah's mystical vision of God in the temple with seraphs, and his cry 'Woe is me, for I am a man of unclean lips, and I live among a people of unclean lips. Yet my eyes have seen the Lord of hosts.'

references.name: 2 Corinthians
references.context: 2 Corinthians (likely 13:14) is quoted: 'the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God and the communion of the Holy Spirit be with you all,' as an early Christian articulation of trinitarian theology.

references.name: Baptismal formula
references.context: The trinitarian baptismal formula 'in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit' is referenced multiple times as liturgical practice.

references.name: Early church councils
references.context: Reference to councils held over '17 or so centuries' and a specific anecdote about 'one early council in either the 4th or 5th century where there was a fistfight between two bishops over the Trinity.'

references.name: Christian mysticism
references.context: The sermon employs mystical language and framework throughout, emphasizing 'mystery and awe,' mystical ritual experience, and the encounter with the divine that precedes doctrine.

references.name: Trinitarian theology / doctrine of the Trinity
references.context: The entire sermon centers on the historical development and contemporary meaning of trinitarian doctrine, particularly the concepts of perichoresis (interpenetration), divine dance, and relational theology.

references.name: Psalm 46:10
references.context: Implicit allusion: 'to be still and know that God is God and we are not' echoes Psalm 46:10 ('Be still, and know that I am God').

references.name: The concept of 'encyclical' and 'anacyclical' glory
references.context: Stramara's vocabulary about the Trinity being both 'encyclical' (circling inward) and 'anacyclical' (circling back, resumptively encompassing) reflects apophatic and cataphatic theological traditions.

references.name: Liturgical theology
references.context: The sermon emphasizes the liturgical enactment of trinitarian theology in baptism, marriage solemnization, and Eucharist as vehicles for participation in the divine dance.

references.name: Medieval mystics / mystical theology
references.context: The reference to encounters with the 'living God' and the priority of mystery/awe over intellectual understanding reflects apophatic mystical tradition.

references.name: Whirling Dervish imagery
references.context: The phrase 'whirling dervishes of love' alludes to Sufi mystical practice (particularly associated with Rumi) as a metaphor for divine love and participation.

primary_theme: Christian doctrine, specifically the doctrine of the Trinity, is not a dry intellectual abstraction but a living mystery meant to hold believers still before Jesus, inviting them into a dynamic, loving relationship that models and transforms human community, work, art, and all creation.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral theological reflection combining scholarly citation with mystical imagery, liturgical engagement, and prophetic invitation; the preacher moves from intellectual exposition (councils, historical development) to experiential engagement (standing still, dancing, being caught up in divine life) to transformative application (social and cultural implications).

references.name: Gospel According to Luke
references.context: The preacher analyzes Luke's account of the risen Jesus asking disciples for food, noting Luke's answer to scholarly debates about the nature of Christ's resurrected body is 'yes' to both spiritual and physical dimensions.

references.name: John 20:27 (or Luke 24:39)
references.context: Jesus's words 'Look at my hands and my feet. See that it is I myself' are quoted multiple times to emphasize Christ's embodied resurrection and continuity with his crucified self.

references.name: Luke 24:42-43 (implied)
references.context: The account of Jesus eating broiled fish with his disciples after the resurrection is the primary narrative text being exegeted throughout the sermon.

references.name: Mary Magdalene as 'first preacher of the resurrection'
references.context: Preacher references Mary Magdalene's role in announcing the resurrection to the disciples as a theological claim about her authority as the first witness and proclaimer.

references.name: Acts 3:13-15 (Peter's speech)
references.context: Quoted passage beginning 'The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob has glorified God's servant Jesus' is attributed to Peter and used to show how witnesses contextualized the resurrection theologically.

references.name: Centurion's servant (resuscitation account)
references.context: Referenced as an example of prior resuscitations in Jewish/Christian tradition to distinguish the unique significance of Jesus's resurrection from mere resuscitation.

references.name: Lazarus (resuscitation account)
references.context: Referenced alongside the centurion's servant as evidence that resuscitations had occurred before, making Jesus's resurrection significant not for being a resurrection but for who was resurrected.

references.name: Pilate (Gospel passion narratives)
references.context: Referenced in Acts passage about Jesus being 'rejected in the presence of Pilate' as part of the passion narrative context for understanding resurrection.

references.name: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
references.context: Invoked in the Peter speech as the God of the patriarchs, establishing continuity between Old Testament covenant and New Testament resurrection.

references.name: Episcopal/Episcopalian theology
references.context: Preacher humorously suggests that Luke's answer of 'yes' to both material and immaterial dimensions of the resurrection reflects an Episcopalian both/and theological sensibility.

references.name: Scholarly debate on resurrection body
references.context: Preacher references 'one of the great questions among scholars' about whether Christ's resurrected body was spiritual, physical, or both, locating the sermon within academic theological discourse.

references.name: Liberation theology (implicit)
references.context: The claim that resurrection of 'an impoverished, itinerant Palestinian Jewish rabbi executed by the world's most powerful empire' is a sign that 'God is near to those thrown about into the trash bins of history' reflects liberation theological concerns.

references.name: Pastoral/incarnational theology (implicit)
references.context: The emphasis on God dignifying trauma through remembrance ('never, ever, ever forgetting' scars) reflects incarnational theology emphasizing God's solidarity with human suffering.

references.name: Christian liturgical calendar (Lent/Easter structure)
references.context: Sermon frames the resurrection message within the liturgical structure of Lent (40 days) and Easter (50-day feast), emphasizing that 'Easter is longer' than Lent.

references.name: Baptismal theology
references.context: Referenced as 'the baptized life' characterized by deep joy rooted in wounds becoming scars and relationships on the mend, suggesting participation in Christ's death-resurrection pattern.

primary_theme: The resurrection of Jesus demonstrates God's embodied presence and solidarity with human suffering, redefining the significance of Christ's resurrection not merely as a miraculous event but as a sign of God's redemptive concern for the marginalized and oppressed, and offering a model of joy grounded in transformed trauma rather than denial of it.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative theology with prophetic undertones; the preacher uses personal family memory (Texas summer meals with family), imaginative reconstruction of biblical scenes (disciples broiling fish in confusion), and scholarly theological engagement (questions about resurrection body) to move from the particular and domestic to the universal and political significance of Easter.

references.name: Anne Carson
references.context: Poet cited for her essay 'Economy, Its Fragrance' offered as a panel discussion on the difference between poetry and prose 20 years ago, used to interpret the literary and soulful nature of the Mary/Judas/Jesus vignette.

references.name: Economy, Its Fragrance
references.context: Anne Carson's essay presented as a panel discussion examining the distinction between poetry and prose through the lens of the anointing story.

references.name: The Gospel of John
references.context: John 12:1-8 — the account of Mary anointing Jesus' feet with expensive perfume at Bethany, with Judas objecting on grounds of waste, forms the entire scriptural foundation of the sermon.

references.name: John 11 (Lazarus raising)
references.context: Reference to Jesus raising Lazarus from the dead, which frames the narrative structure and sets the context for the dinner scene at Mary and Martha's home.

references.name: The Last Supper tradition
references.context: The preacher notes that Judas 'gets the short end of the stick in every depiction of the Last Supper,' invoking the broader canonical and artistic tradition of Judas's portrayal.

references.name: The Passover festival
references.context: Referenced as the context for Jesus' 'strategic journey to Jerusalem for his prophetic demonstrations' occurring six days after the anointing.

references.name: Resurrection theology
references.context: The sermon's poetic structure is 'anchored by death on one end and resurrection on the other,' establishing a theological framework central to interpreting the story's meaning.

references.name: Poetry as theological method
references.context: Carson's argument that poetry operates through restraint and excess, cutting words without cutting language, is used to interpret the fragrant excess of the anointing as a theological statement.

references.name: The medium is the message
references.context: Invoked when the preacher discusses how 'the austerity of the peace is the peace itself' and 'something glorious and beautiful and compelling cannot be contained by the limits of a column or a page.'

references.name: Existentialism
references.context: Carson's framing raises 'two existential questions: Is death wasted on God? Is life wasted on mortals?' — reflecting existential concern with waste, meaning, and mortality.

primary_theme: Judas's accusation that Mary's anointing of Jesus with expensive perfume wastes resources needed for the poor reveals his scarcity-minded greed rather than genuine charity, while the poetic excess of her gesture embodies a theology of abundance, beauty, and worship that overflows rational economic calculation and prefigures Christ's resurrection.
