rhetorical_style: Scholarly pastoral reflection grounded in close literary analysis; the preacher uses Anne Carson's poetic-theological interpretation to reframe a biblical narrative, moving from character analysis (Judas's hidden motives) through literary-critical method (poetry vs. prose, sensory imagery) to theological insight (God's abundant love that overflows death itself). The tone is contemplative and invitational rather than combative, inviting congregants to imaginative participation in the story's deeper meanings.

references.name: Penal Substitutionary Atonement
references.context: Presented as one possible interpretation of the crucifixion's meaning that the preacher warns against reducing the crucifixion to a single dimension.

references.name: Christus Victor
references.context: Cited as an ancient theological framework for understanding the crucifixion alongside penal substitutionary atonement.

references.name: Marcus (friend, unnamed)
references.context: Referenced for the phrase 'the word made fresh' as a description of the Christian vocation.

references.name: Mark's Gospel
references.context: The primary scriptural text analyzed throughout; this year's passion gospel reading with emphasis on its narrative style and brutality.

references.name: Mount of Olives / Jesus's entry to Jerusalem
references.context: Referenced to show the contrast between Jesus's peaceful procession and Pontius Pilate's imperial procession occurring simultaneously.

references.name: Pontius Pilate
references.context: Mentioned as the Roman provincial governor who decides the fate of Jesus in the passion narrative.

references.name: Simon of Cyrene
references.context: The foreign passerby compelled by soldiers to carry Jesus's cross; used to illustrate complicity in brutality beyond one's control.

references.name: Liberation theology / Critique of systems of domination
references.context: The preacher identifies Mark's narrative as containing a 'quiet critique of systems of domination' and imperial power structures.

references.name: Existentialism / Existential question
references.context: Invoked when addressing 'the daunting existential question' of the meaning-making of suffering in Christian communities.

references.name: Passion Sunday (Latin etymology: suffering)
references.context: The liturgical context and meaning of the day explained through its etymological connection to suffering (passio).

references.name: Solidarity theology / God's suffering with humanity
references.context: The central theological claim that God builds credibility through suffering in solidarity with human experience in concrete situations.

primary_theme: Mark's passion narrative presents the crucifixion not as abstract theological doctrine but as a brutal historical event that reveals God's solidarity with human suffering and critiques systems of imperial domination.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic reflection combining narrative analysis, theological critique, and existential witness; the preacher moves from academic biblical analysis to prophetic indictment of systemic violence to pastoral affirmation of God's presence in suffering.

references.name: The Decalogue / Ten Commandments
references.context: Preacher announces he will be preaching on the Decalogue, which is one way of saying the Ten Commandments.

references.name: Exodus 20:12 (Honor your father and mother)
references.context: Preacher recalls that his parents only quoted this commandment growing up, emphasizing 'that your days may be long on the earth.'

references.name: The Ten Commandments (Hollywood film with Charlton Heston as Moses)
references.context: Referenced as one of the ways the Ten Commandments has been used throughout history as the subject of fanciful Hollywood films.

references.name: Exodus 20:1-2
references.context: Quoted: 'I am the God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the house of enslavement' - used as the curiously emphatic reminder at the outset of the Exodus text.

references.name: Noah and his family in the ark
references.context: Referenced as one of three separate but related accounts of God's faithfulness heard over three weeks in Lent.

references.name: Sarah and her family
references.context: Referenced as the second account of God's faithfulness in the Lenten cycle.

references.name: The people of Israel / descendants of Jacob
references.context: Preacher notes that for some 400 years, the descendants of Jacob toiled under oppressive pharaohs.

references.name: Book of Exodus - Joseph narrative
references.context: The book of Exodus explains that Israel's oppression began with a pharaoh who did not remember Joseph, the ancestor who caused Egypt to flourish.

references.name: Book of Genesis - Joseph story
references.context: Referenced as the source of the Joseph story that explains how the Israelites came to be in Egypt.

references.name: Genesis creation narratives
references.context: God reminds Israel of the origins of the world and creation itself by taking them back to formative Genesis narratives.

references.name: Exodus 20:8-11 (The Sabbath command)
references.context: Quoted and emphasized as no surprise given the context of liberation: 'Remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy. For in six days, the Lord made heaven and earth, but rested the seventh day.'

references.name: Walter Brueggemann
references.context: Cited as theologian who states: 'Israel rests because God rests' and that God is not a workaholic.

references.name: Walter Brueggemann (implicit - second citation)
references.context: Preacher quotes Brueggemann again on healing of creation: 'requires a disengagement from the dominant systems of power and wealth.'

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: The sermon's entire framework interprets the Ten Commandments as liberation from oppression and as a counter-vision to imperial domination.

references.name: Prophetic critique of consumer capitalism / workaholism
references.context: The sermon critiques modern culture's valuation of people only for economic productivity and its workaholic ethos.

primary_theme: The Ten Commandments represent not arbitrary rules but a liberating ethical and communal vision given to formerly enslaved people, with the Sabbath command at its heart offering an alternative to exploitative systems of productivity and domination.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with prophetic challenge, using personal anecdotes, historical narrative, theological commentary (via Brueggemann), and contemporary social critique to reinterpret ancient scripture for modern congregational life.

references.name: Mark Gospel (Mark narrative, Mark 8)
references.context: The sermon analyzes the Transfiguration account in Mark's Gospel, examining the Greek word 'transfigured' and its theological implications.

references.name: Romans (St. Paul's letter to the Romans)
references.context: The preacher notes that St. Paul used the same Greek word for 'transfigured' that Mark used in describing Jesus's transformation.

references.name: Moses
references.context: Moses appears in the Transfiguration narrative flanking Jesus alongside Elijah, representing the law and prophetic tradition.

references.name: Elijah
references.context: Elijah appears in the Transfiguration narrative flanking Jesus alongside Moses, representing prophetic witness.

references.name: St. Peter (Peter, James, and John)
references.context: Peter's confused reaction to the Transfiguration drives much of the sermon's narrative analysis, particularly his impulsive desire to build dwellings.

references.name: Dr. Fame Perkins
references.context: Quoted to establish that 'the transfiguration cannot override the necessity of Jesus's suffering and death' and that 'glory and suffering cannot be separated.'

references.name: Metamorphosis (Greek etymology)
references.context: The preacher explores how the Greek word for 'transfigure' is the root of the English word 'metamorphosis,' comparing Jesus's transformation to a caterpillar's biological process.

references.name: Incarnation theology
references.context: The sermon argues that incarnation, suffering, death, resurrection, and ascension are all dimensions of Jesus's transfiguration and identity.

references.name: Christian baptism and baptismal theology
references.context: Baptism is presented as the sacrament where we glimpse our true identity in Christ and are invited into Christian transformation.

references.name: Christology of suffering and glory
references.context: The sermon addresses the theological tension between Christ's glory and Christ's suffering, arguing they cannot be separated.

references.name: Mystical contemplation
references.context: The preacher invokes a contemplative approach, instructing listeners to 'listen, contemplate' rather than 'blabber aimlessly' in the face of divine mystery.

primary_theme: The Transfiguration reveals that Jesus's inner reality—his divine identity—encompasses both glory and suffering, and Christian transformation invites us to become our authentic selves in Christ rather than something altogether different.
rhetorical_style: Scholarly-pastoral synthesis combining linguistic analysis, narrative exegesis, theological interpretation via secondary sources, and personal pastoral reflection on the implications for Christian living.

references.name: John 1 (Prologue - Word Made Flesh)
references.context: The poetic prologue that acclaims Jesus Christ as the word made flesh and how this word begins drawing people into a countercultural movement of love.

references.name: John 1:43-51 (Nathanael/Philip narrative)
references.context: The primary text: Jesus encounters Philip, Philip finds Nathanael, Nathanael questions if anything good can come from Nazareth, Philip invites him to 'come and see,' and Jesus reveals he saw Nathanael under the fig tree.

references.name: King James Version (Bible translation)
references.context: Preacher cites the KJV phrasing of Nathanael's question: 'can there any good thing come out of Nazareth?'

references.name: John 1 (Jesus's 'Come and See' invitation)
references.context: First instance where Jesus offers the invitation 'come and see' to new disciples who ask where he is staying.

references.name: John 4 (Samaritan Woman at the Well)
references.context: Second instance of 'come and see' invitation: the Samaritan woman invites others after her conversation with Jesus at the well.

references.name: John 11 (Lazarus narrative)
references.context: Third instance of 'come and see' invitation: Mary and Martha invite Jesus to come see where their deceased brother has been laid.

references.name: Moses and the Prophets
references.context: Philip declares to Nathanael: 'we have found him about whom Moses and the prophets spoke.'

references.name: Adam Surer
references.context: Senior editor at The Atlantic, cited for the observation: 'it is a convenient trick to rob a person of all they have, even their own body, and then mock them for their impoverishment and blame it on their nature.'

references.name: Anti-Semitic German programs (14th-20th centuries)
references.context: Historical analysis of how bias incrementally dehumanizes communities; cited as example of how stereotyping questions lead to systemic violence.

references.name: Ruby Sells
references.context: Theologian quoted for the phrase about maintaining a center that is 'both tough-minded and tender-hearted' in relation to systemic evil and human impact.

references.name: The Incarnation (Christian doctrine)
references.context: Central theological concept: God taking on flesh in Jesus Christ as an encounter and experience rather than an argument or debate.

references.name: Scholarly and popular biblical criticism (violence and genocide)
references.context: Preacher references 'much discussion in scholarly and popular circles alike about the Bible's depictions and mandates for war, genocide, and violence.'

references.name: Dehumanization as precursor to violence
references.context: The guiding question when analyzing violence: 'When exactly did one group begin to understand the other group as subhuman?' This reflects scholarly work in genocide studies and social psychology.

primary_theme: God's incarnation in Jesus Christ challenges human biases and stereotypes by inviting personal encounter and recognition of the full humanity of marginalized peoples and places.
rhetorical_style: Narrative exposition combined with prophetic challenge: the preacher tells the biblical story of Nathanael, draws out its contemporary implications regarding racism and dehumanization, cites scholarly authorities and historical examples, and closes with pastoral exhortation to maintain 'tough-minded' prophetic witness alongside tender-hearted inclusion.

references.name: Gospel of John (John 1:1-18)
references.context: The entire opening passage of the sermon is the Johannine prologue, read in full as the foundation for the theological meditation.

references.name: Genesis creation narrative
references.context: Referenced when the preacher says 'let's hearken back to the Genesis narrative' to show how incarnation adds texture to original creation.

references.name: St. Athanasius
references.context: Cited by name for the patristic doctrine that God became human 'so that we too can become like God.'

references.name: St. Paul
references.context: Cited for teaching that incarnation in 'the fullness of time' happened so humans could 'receive adoption as children of the divine.'

references.name: Pauline adoption theology (Romans 8:15)
references.context: The preacher invokes Paul's concept of crying 'Abba, Father' as a result of divine adoption through the incarnation.

references.name: Impressionism / Denver Art Museum exhibit on women artists
references.context: Used as extended metaphor to frame the gospel narrative as portraiture, with the Word as sitter and the gospel narrator as artist.

references.name: The 'ground of being'
references.context: Phrase used when describing incarnation as 'the ground of our being, the source of our existence'; characteristic of Paul Tillich's existential theology.

references.name: Patristic incarnational theology
references.context: The entire sermon builds on the Eastern church father concept that God's incarnation in Christ makes deification (theosis) possible for humans.

references.name: Christological interpretation of John
references.context: The preacher uses high Christology to interpret the prologue, treating the Word/Logos as divine person requiring contemplative attentiveness.

references.name: Sacramental/incarnational theology
references.context: The sermon emphasizes God's tangible presence in flesh, breath, and human embodiment as the central mystery of Christian faith.

primary_theme: The Incarnation as God's definitive self-disclosure: through Jesus Christ as the Word made flesh, God breaks through doctrinal abstraction to reveal His loving nature and invite humanity into divine adoption.
rhetorical_style: Contemplative artistic meditation blending scriptural close reading, extended visual metaphor (portraiture and Impressionist painting), and pastoral theological reflection on incarnational mystery.

references.name: The Preacher's Wife
references.context: Preacher mentions it as a personal Advent/Christmas tradition: 'It's not Christmas for me until I've watched the preacher's wife.'

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Primary biblical text analyzed throughout the sermon for its distinctive literary style and direct approach to presenting Jesus.

references.name: Mark 1:1
references.context: Quoted directly: 'The beginning of the good news of Jesus Christ, the Son of God' as example of Mark's concise, direct narrative style.

references.name: Mark 1:4-6 (John the Baptist passage)
references.context: 'John the baptizer appeared in the wilderness proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins'—core text examining sensory, embodied spirituality.

references.name: Isaiah 40:3 (voice crying in the wilderness)
references.context: Alluded to in discussion of John the Baptist as 'the one crying in the wilderness,' referenced twice in the sermon.

references.name: Exodus (manna, quail, pillars of cloud and fire)
references.context: Referenced as 'the honeymoon days of the Exodus' showing God's vulnerable love through sustenance in the wilderness.

references.name: Good Friday and Easter
references.context: Referenced as liturgical markers distinguishing Advent as a season for 'people who spend more time in a Good Friday world than dwelling on thrilling Easter sunrises.'

references.name: Luke 1 (annunciation narratives)
references.context: Implicit reference to Mary and Elizabeth as 'a teenage girl believed by no one' and 'a woman beyond childbearing age' becoming parents—unlikely Advent characters.

references.name: Advent liturgical tradition
references.context: Central framework: Advent as theological season emphasizing waiting, questioning, rumor, and whisper rather than certainty and direct answer.

references.name: Mark (the preacher's friend)
references.context: Friend cited as saying 'faith resembles something more like a collage than a systematic Christian doctrine' and introduces the concept of 'theological foxes.'

references.name: Isaiah Berlin's fox and hedgehog concept
references.context: Implicit reference through phrase 'theological foxes who remain unconvinced that reality is mirrored neatly in the recitation of neat maxims.'

references.name: Seminary education
references.context: Referenced as context for preacher's skepticism of 'dramatic call stories' told by seminary classmates, introducing personal theological methodology.

references.name: Christian doctrine/systematic theology
references.context: Contrasted with collage-like faith; represents the organized, rational theological approach the preacher questions.

references.name: Mystical/sensory spirituality tradition
references.context: Central to sermon's argument: full-bodied experience (locusts, honey, water, camel hair) as opposed to modern rational thinking.

references.name: Prophetic tradition
references.context: John the Baptist placed within prophetic lineage: 'Like the prophets before him, John was inviting his nation and people to remember what it means to love.'

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Implicit throughout: emphasis on God's presence in fragile, earthly, embodied forms ('manger and stable, not throne and palace').

references.name: Liberation theology themes
references.context: Implicit in focus on 'unlikely characters'—'survivors of sexual assault, refugees, minorities, those living in impoverishment'—as bearers of good news.

references.name: Google/search bar culture
references.context: Referenced as representing modern rational shortcut-seeking: 'Advent is the theological opposite of a search bar.'

references.name: Cable news visual culture
references.context: Contemporary cultural critique: 'When cable news chyrons act as visual sirens and frantic schedules distract us from attentiveness.'

references.name: Fashion Week (New York City)
references.context: Humorous reference to John the Baptist's fashion choices: 'I don't know how that would go at Fashion Week in New York City.'

references.name: Hymn tradition ('Prepare the Way of Zion')
references.context: Referenced as Advent hymn: 'it's not Advent for me until I've bounced to prepare the way of Zion. What a hymn.'

primary_theme: Advent invites us to encounter God's coming through fragile, whispered, embodied encounters with unlikely people and margins, rather than through dramatic certainty, rational answers, or displays of power.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic reflection combining personal testimony, sensory imagery, cultural critique, and liturgical wisdom; uses metaphor and antithesis (whisper vs. crash shout, margin vs. center, collage vs. system) to build argument while addressing contemporary spiritual seekers with doubt and question.

references.name: Book of Proverbs
references.context: Used to illustrate that wisdom is distinct from knowledge and power, with the example of one wise person bringing down a city of warriors.

references.name: Proverbs (wisdom literature tradition)
references.context: The preacher grounds the sermon in the biblical wisdom tradition where wealth, notoriety, and power are not indicators of virtue.

references.name: Jesus/Gospel accounts
references.context: Jesus is described as 'emerging from the wisdom culture of his family and people' and considering himself within the wisdom tradition.

references.name: Parable of the Ten Bridesmaids/Virgins
references.context: The entire sermon is built on this parable (Matthew 25:1-13), exploring wisdom through the metaphor of prepared vs. unprepared bridesmaids.

references.name: Matthew 25:1-13
references.context: Direct quotations from the parable: 'The wise took flasks of oil with their lamps, the foolish did not' and 'for you know neither the day nor the hour.'

references.name: Eschaton/end times theology
references.context: Scholars are noted as debating whether the parable explores 'the eschaton,' but the preacher reframes it beyond chronological time to personal 'end of time moments.'

references.name: Sanctified imagination
references.context: The preacher invokes 'our sanctified imaginations' to realize personal eschatological moments, suggesting theological reflection on the imagination's role in spiritual understanding.

references.name: Justice as social/communal
references.context: The statement 'If justice is social, then so is wisdom' reflects liberation theology and communal ethics traditions.

references.name: Communal discernment tradition
references.context: Wisdom is presented as emerging through community, collective effort, and 'the gathered community' rather than individual epiphany.

primary_theme: Wisdom is not wealth, power, or knowledge, but a communal practice of preparedness, vigilance, and collective discernment that emerges through waiting, error, observation, and the shared work of staying awake in the ambiguity of an uncertain future.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic reflection combining biblical exegesis with contemporary social examples (gun violence, addiction, job loss, grief), using narrative storytelling and repeated rhythmic refrains ('the waiting, waiting, waiting') to build emotional and theological weight.
